topics.title: Introduction to Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (NIST SP 800-145):** *Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.*

### 1.1 The Electricity Grid Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine the early days when every factory, hospital, and home had to build its **own power generator**. It was expensive, noisy, required constant maintenance, and most of the time ran at partial capacity. Then came the **centralized electricity grid** — you simply plugged into a socket, paid for what you consumed, and scaled up or down instantly without buying a new generator.

**Cloud Computing is exactly that for IT resources.** Instead of buying physical servers, installing operating systems, configuring networks, and paying for idle capacity, you rent virtualized compute, storage, and software from a provider (e.g., **AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform**) and pay only for what you use — billed by the **second or hour**.

| Old IT Model (Pre-Cloud) | Cloud Model (Post-2006) |
|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Buy hardware upfront (CapEx) | Pay-as-you-go (OpEx) |
| Fixed capacity | **Elastic** capacity |
| Weeks to provision | Minutes to provision |
| Manual scaling | Auto-scaling |
| Single-tenant | Multi-tenant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing (NIST — must be memorized for KTU):**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — provision resources automatically without human intervention.
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — accessible over the network via standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST).
> 3. **Resource Pooling (Multi-Tenancy)** — physical/virtual resources dynamically assigned to multiple consumers.
> 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, scaling outward/inward.
> 5. **Measured Service (Pay-per-Use)** — cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts).

---

### 1.2 Why Cloud Computing Matters — The Motivation

- **Reduced Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** No upfront investment in servers, storage, or networking.
- **Economies of Scale:** Providers buy hardware in bulk at massive discounts and pass savings to tenants.
- **Global Reach:** Deploy your application in **40+ geographic regions** within minutes.
- **Focus on Core Business:** DevOps teams focus on code, not racking and stacking hardware.
- **Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity:** Built-in replication across **Availability Zones (AZs)**.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2-mark questions:** A single-line definition plus naming the **5 essential characteristics** fetches full marks. Do not over-elaborate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of Computing Paradigms

The journey of cloud computing is a logical progression of **decoupling** compute from physical ownership. Each step moved one layer further from the user's premises.

**Step 1 — Mainframe Computing (1950s–1970s)**
- Centralized expensive mainframes with **dumb terminals**.
- Users shared compute; no personal ownership.

**Step 2 — Personal Computing (1980s–1990s)**
- Distributed computing: each user owned a PC.
- Peak of decentralization; underutilized resources at home.

**Step 3 — Client–Server / Enterprise Datacenter (1990s–2000s)**
- Organizations built **private datacenters**.
- High CapEx, low utilization (typically **10–20%** server utilization), over-provisioning.

**Step 4 — Grid & Utility Computing (2000s)**
- Treating compute as a **utility** (like water/electricity).
- Pioneered concepts of virtualized resource pools across organizations.

**Step 5 — Cloud Computing (2006 — Salesforce, then AWS EC2 in August 2006)**
- The tipping point: **Amazon EC2** publicly launched on **August 25, 2006**, offering virtual servers on demand.
- Followed by **Google App Engine (2008)**, **Microsoft Azure (2010)**, **OpenStack (2010)**.

**Step 6 — Serverless & Edge (2014 onwards)**
- **AWS Lambda (2014)** introduced Function-as-a-Service (FaaS).
- Edge computing pushes compute closer to the user (IoT, 5G).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Driver — Virtualization:** The technological foundation of cloud computing is **hardware virtualization**, where a **hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen)** partitions one physical machine into multiple isolated **Virtual Machines (VMs)**. Each VM behaves like an independent computer running its own OS.

### 2.2 The Three Service Models — SPI Stack

KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the **SPI triad**: **SaaS, PaaS, IaaS**.

| Layer | Service Model | What YOU Manage | What PROVIDER Manages | Example |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------|---------|
| **Bottom** | **IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service** | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine |
| **Middle** | **PaaS — Platform as a Service** | Apps, Data | Everything else + OS, Middleware, Runtime | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku |
| **Top** | **SaaS — Software as a Service** | Only your data/user access | All infrastructure + software itself | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"You manage what is above the line."** Memorize this for the diagram-drawing questions.

### 2.3 The Four Deployment Models

1. **Private Cloud** — provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization. May be on-premise or owned/operated by a third party. Highest control, lowest shared-resource benefit.
2. **Public Cloud** — open for use by the general public. Owned by cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP). Maximum elasticity, minimum capital investment.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private + public) bound by **standardized or proprietary technology** enabling data and application portability (e.g., **AWS Outposts**, **Azure Arc**).
4. **Community Cloud** — provisioned for exclusive use by a specific community of consumers from organizations with shared concerns (e.g., government agencies, healthcare consortiums).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Capacity Planning & Cost Models

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Server Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | Typical on-premise: **10–20%**; Cloud target: **60–80%** |
| 2 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = CapEx + OpEx$ over 3–5 years | Cloud converts CapEx to OpEx |
| 3 | **Pay-per-Use Cost** | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot t_i$ | $P_i$ = price per unit of resource $i$, $t_i$ = time used |
| 4 | **Elasticity Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_{peak}}{C_{avg}}$ | $E > 1$ justifies cloud; $E \approx 1$ suits on-premise |
| 5 | **Throughput (Cloud SLA)** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Time Unit}}$ | Measured in **requests/sec** or **IOPS** |
| 6 | **Availability %** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | "**Five Nines**" = **99.999%** ≈ 5.26 min downtime/year |
| 7 | **Storage Replication Factor** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Total Stored Copies}}{\text{Logical Data Size}}$ | e.g., **3x** in HDFS default |
| 8 | **Scalability — Vertical (Scale-Up)** | $C_{new} = C_{old} \cdot k$ | $k$ = resource multiplier (CPU/RAM) |
| 9 | **Scalability — Horizontal (Scale-Out)** | $C_{new} = N \cdot C_{node}$ | $N$ = number of identical nodes |
| 10 | **Cost per Request (Serverless)** | $C_{req} = C_{compute} + C_{requests}$ | AWS Lambda: \$0.20 per 1M requests + GB-second |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **Five Nines (99.999%)** availability is the gold standard for telecom and cloud SLAs. The annual downtime allowance is calculated as: $525{,}600 \text{ min/year} \times (1 - 0.99999) \approx 5.26 \text{ min/year}$. Always show this calculation if asked.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **DevOps & CI/CD:** Build servers provisioned on demand, torn down after pipeline completion.
- **Big Data Analytics:** **Hadoop/Spark** clusters spun up for 4 hours to process petabytes, then released.
- **IoT Backends:** Ingest millions of sensor events per second into serverless functions.
- **AI/ML Training:** GPUs rented at **\$2–\$32/hour** instead of buying \$30,000 GPUs.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Hot standby site in another region at a fraction of the cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Cloud Computing is a *systems* topic, so the "derivations" here are **analytical cost/performance derivations** commonly tested in KTU 14-mark problems. Every arithmetic step is shown explicitly.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cloud vs. On-Premise Total Cost Comparison

A startup expects a steady workload but wants to be ready for **3× peak load** during a product launch lasting 1 week every quarter.

**Given Parameters (hypothetical KTU problem):**
- Required baseline compute: **20 vCPUs, 80 GB RAM, 2 TB SSD storage**
- Peak factor: **3× baseline**
- On-Premise: Server cost = **\$5,000 each** (supports 10 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM, 1 TB)
- On-Premise: Power, cooling, admin overhead = **\$800/server/month**
- Cloud: **\$0.05/vCPU-hour, \$0.01/GB-RAM-hour, \$0.10/GB-storage-month**
- Time horizon: **1 year**

**Step 1 — On-Premise Capacity Sizing**

Because workload can spike to 3×, on-premise must provision for peak:

$$C_{peak\_vCPU} = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ vCPUs}$$

Number of physical servers required (each supports 10 vCPUs):

$$N_{servers} = \left\lceil \frac{60}{10} \right\rceil = 6 \text{ servers}$$

**Step 2 — On-Premise Hardware CapEx (1-time cost)**

$$CapEx_{HW} = 6 \times \$5{,}000 = \$30{,}000$$

**Step 3 — On-Premise OpEx (per year)**

$$OpEx_{OP} = 6 \times \$800 \times 12 = \$57{,}600$$

**Step 4 — On-Premise TCO for Year 1**

$$TCO_{OP} = CapEx_{HW} + OpEx_{OP} = 30{,}000 + 57{,}600 = \$87{,}600$$

**Step 5 — Cloud Cost — Baseline Workload (Average 50 weeks/year)**

$$H_{baseline} = 50 \text{ weeks} \times 7 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h} = 8{,}400 \text{ hours}$$

$$C_{baseline} = (20 \times \$0.05 + 80 \times \$0.01) \times 8{,}400 + 2{,}000 \times \$0.10 \times 12$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the inner term**

$$20 \times 0.05 = \$1.00 \text{ per hour (vCPU cost)}$$

$$80 \times 0.01 = \$0.80 \text{ per hour (RAM cost)}$$

$$\text{Hourly baseline} = 1.00 + 0.80 = \$1.80 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Baseline Total**

$$C_{baseline} = 1.80 \times 8{,}400 + 2{,}400 = 15{,}120 + 2{,}400 = \$17{,}520$$

**Step 8 — Cloud Cost — Peak Workload (Peak 3×, 1 week × 4 quarters = 4 weeks/year)**

$$H_{peak} = 4 \text{ weeks} \times 7 \times 24 = 672 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{Peak vCPUs} = 60, \text{ Peak RAM} = 240 \text{ GB}$$

$$\text{Hourly peak} = 60 \times 0.05 + 240 \times 0.01 = 3.00 + 2.40 = \$5.40 \text{ per hour}$$

$$C_{peak} = 5.40 \times 672 = \$3{,}628.80$$

**Step 9 — Total Cloud Cost Year 1**

$$TCO_{Cloud} = 17{,}520 + 3{,}628.80 = \$21{,}148.80$$

**Step 10 — Savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 87{,}600 - 21{,}148.80 = \$66{,}451.20 \text{ (≈ 75.9% reduction)}$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{66{,}451.20}{87{,}600} \times 100\% \approx 75.86\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Cloud wins when workload is **spiky**. The cloud provider's **multi-tenant pooling** absorbs peaks that on-premise must over-provision. This is the fundamental economic argument for cloud computing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Five Nines Availability Downtime

Cloud providers offer SLAs measured in "Nines."

**Step 1 — Total minutes in a (non-leap) year**

$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Allowed downtime for 99.999% (Five Nines)**

$$D_{allowed} = T_{year} \times (1 - A)$$

$$D_{allowed} = 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.99999) = 525{,}600 \times 0.00001$$

$$D_{allowed} = 5.256 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 315.36 \text{ seconds/year}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison Table for KTU Answers**

| Availability | Allowed Downtime/Year | Allowed Downtime/Month |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **99% (Two Nines)** | 3.65 days | 7.20 hours |
| **99.9% (Three Nines)** | 8.77 hours | 43.83 min |
| **99.99% (Four Nines)** | 52.60 min | 4.38 min |
| **99.999% (Five Nines)** | 5.26 min | 0.44 min |
| **99.9999% (Six Nines)** | 31.56 sec | 2.62 sec |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Insight:** When asked *"What does 99.99% availability mean?"* — always convert to **concrete time** (e.g., 52.6 minutes/year). Examiners reward numerical substantiation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elasticity & Auto-Scaling Threshold

A web application experiences traffic doubling every hour during peak. Compute the number of instances needed at hour $n$ if baseline = 2 instances.

**Step 1 — Geometric growth model**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t}$$

**Step 2 — At t = 0**

$$N(0) = 2 \cdot 2^{0} = 2 \text{ instances}$$

**Step 3 — At t = 3 hours**

$$N(3) = 2 \cdot 2^{3} = 16 \text{ instances}$$

**Step 4 — At t = 6 hours (typical surge)**

$$N(6) = 2 \cdot 2^{6} = 128 \text{ instances}$$

**Step 5 — Total instance-hours over 6 hours**

$$I_{total} = \sum_{t=0}^{5} N(t) \cdot \Delta t = \Delta t \cdot N_0 \sum_{t=0}^{5} 2^{t}$$

$$I_{total} = 1 \cdot 2 \cdot (2^{6} - 1) = 2 \cdot 63 = 126 \text{ instance-hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Without elasticity, the company would have to provision **128 instances permanently**, paying for them 24/7. With cloud auto-scaling, the cost scales only with the geometric curve, dramatically reducing the bill.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Choose What

| Criterion | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| Initial cost | **Very Low** | **Very High** | **Medium** |
| Scalability | **Unlimited** | **Limited by HW** | **High** |
| Control & Customization | **Low** | **Highest** | **Medium** |
| Security & Compliance | **Provider-managed** | **Fully self-managed** | **Mixed** |
| Latency-sensitive apps | **Risk (unless edge)** | **Best** | **Good** |
| Time to deploy | **Minutes** | **Months** | **Weeks** |
| Suitable workload | Web apps, SaaS, dev/test | Banking, defense, regulated | Burst-out from private to public |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain text labels** (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels) to ensure clean rendering.

---

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Three Service Models (Layered Stack)

```mermaid
graph TB
    layerSaaS["SAAS - SOFTWARE AS A SERVICE - GMAIL, OFFICE365, SALESFORCE"]
    layerPaaS["PAAS - PLATFORM AS A SERVICE - GOOGLE APP ENGINE, HEROKU, ELASTIC BEANSTALK"]
    layerIaaS["IAAS - INFRASTRUCTURE AS A SERVICE - EC2, AZURE VM, GCP COMPUTE"]
    layerPhys["PHYSICAL HARDWARE - SERVERS, STORAGE, NETWORK - DATACENTER"]

    layerSaaS --> layerPaaS
    layerPaaS --> layerIaaS
    layerIaaS --> layerPhys

    user1["END USER USES SAAS"] -.uses.-> layerSaaS
    user2["DEVELOPER BUILDS ON PAAS"] -.uses.-> layerPaaS
    user3["SYSADMIN MANAGES IAAS"] -.uses.-> layerIaaS
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hybrid Cloud Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    userNode["END USER DEVICE"] -->|HTTPS Request| lbNode["LOAD BALANCER"]

    lbNode -->|Normal Traffic| priDC["PRIVATE CLOUD - ON PREMISE DATACENTER"]

    lbNode -->|Traffic Spike or Burst| pubCloud["PUBLIC CLOUD - AWS OR AZURE REGION"]

    priDC --> vmApp1["BUSINESS CRITICAL APPS VM"]
    priDC --> dbPri1["PRIVATE DATABASE - REGULATED DATA"]

    pubCloud --> vmApp2["WEB TIER VM - AUTO SCALING GROUP"]
    pubCloud --> dbPub1["CLOUD DATABASE - DYNAMODB OR COSMOS"]
    pubCloud --> objStore1["OBJECT STORAGE - S3 OR BLOB"]

    priDC <-->|VPN or Direct Connect| pubCloud
    objStore1 -.Backups.-> priDC
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Cloud Computing Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA["1950S - MAINFRAME ERA - SHARED CENTRAL COMPUTE"]
    stepB["1980S - PC ERA - DISTRIBUTED DESKTOP COMPUTE"]
    stepC["1990S - CLIENT SERVER - ENTERPRISE DATACENTERS"]
    stepD["2000S - GRID AND UTILITY COMPUTE"]
    stepE["2006 - CLOUD COMPUTING - AWS EC2 LAUNCH"]
    stepF["2010 - IAAS PAAS SAAS MATURITY"]
    stepG["2014 - SERVERLESS - AWS LAMBDA"]
    stepH["2020 PLUS - EDGE AI AND HYBRID MESH"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    cloudConsumer["CLOUD CONSUMER - USES SERVICES"]
    cloudProvider["CLOUD PROVIDER - AWS AZURE GCP"]
    cloudAuditor["CLOUD AUDITOR - SECURITY AND COMPLIANCE CHECK"]
    cloudBroker["CLOUD BROKER - INTERMEDIARY FOR FEDERATION"]
    cloudCarrier["CLOUD CARRIER - NETWORK CONNECTIVITY"]

    cloudConsumer -->|Uses IaaS PaaS SaaS| cloudProvider
    cloudAuditor -->|Audits Performance Security| cloudProvider
    cloudBroker -->|Aggregates and Integrates| cloudConsumer
    cloudBroker --> cloudProvider
    cloudCarrier -->|Network and Transport| cloudConsumer
    cloudCarrier --> cloudProvider
```

---

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Workload Migration Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startNode["START - EVALUATE WORKLOAD"]
    q1{"IS DATA REGULATED OR SENSITIVE?"}
    q2{"IS WORKLOAD SPIKY OR STEADY?"}
    q3{"IS LATENCY LESS THAN 10MS REQUIRED?"}
    out1["CHOOSE PRIVATE CLOUD"]
    out2["CHOOSE HYBRID CLOUD"]
    out3["CHOOSE PUBLIC CLOUD"]

    startNode --> q1
    q1 -->|YES| out1
    q1 -->|NO| q2
    q2 -->|SPIKY - 3X PEAKS| out3
    q2 -->|STEADY| q3
    q3 -->|YES - ULTRA LOW LATENCY| out2
    q3 -->|NO - LATENCY TOLERANT| out3
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** Use clean boxes, label every arrow, and always include a **legend or brief caption** explaining what flows where. Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for diagram clarity and labeling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q1.** *Define cloud computing. List any **four essential characteristics** of cloud computing as per NIST.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction — **NIST SP 800-145**.
>
> **Four Essential Characteristics (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — resources provisioned automatically.
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — accessible over standard protocols.
> 3. **Resource Pooling** — multi-tenant physical/virtual resources.
> 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — scale up/down quickly.
> 5. **Measured Service** — pay-per-use metering.
>
> *(Any 4 well-explained points secure full marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> **Q2.** *Differentiate between **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** with one example each.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
> |---------|------|------|------|
> | **What you manage** | OS, Runtime, Apps, Data | Apps, Data | Data only |
> | **What provider manages** | Hardware, Network, Virtualization | + OS, Middleware, Runtime | All of the above + Application |
> | **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VM | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
> | **Target user** | Sysadmins, DevOps | Developers | End users |
> | **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
> | **Control** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
>
> *(1 mark for clear layered explanation + 1 mark for correct examples + 1 mark for table-style comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** *Explain the **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture** with a neat diagram. List the **five actors** and their responsibilities. **[7 marks]***
>
> **(b)** *Compare and contrast **Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community Cloud** deployment models in detail. Mention a real-world scenario where each is most appropriate. **[7 marks]***

**Model Answer:**

**(a) NIST Reference Architecture (7 marks):**

The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (SP 500-292) defines **five major actors**:

1. **Cloud Consumer** — uses cloud services; can be an individual or enterprise.
2. **Cloud Provider** — makes services available (AWS, Azure, GCP).
3. **Cloud Auditor** — independently evaluates cloud services — security, privacy, performance.
4. **Cloud Broker** — manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services; negotiates relationships between providers and consumers.
5. **Cloud Carrier** — provides connectivity and transport of cloud services from providers to consumers (e.g., telecom ISPs, network providers).

**Diagram (already in Section 4.4 above) — 3 marks**

**Responsibilities table (4 marks):**

| Actor | Core Responsibility |
|-------|---------------------|
| Consumer | Service deployment, monitoring, use |
| Provider | Service deployment, maintenance, security |
| Auditor | Independent assessment |
| Broker | Service aggregation, integration, customization |
| Carrier | Network connectivity, SLA assurance |

---

**(b) Deployment Models (7 marks):**

| Model | Owner | Users | Pros | Cons | Best Scenario |
|-------|-------|-------|------|------|---------------|
| **Public** | Provider | General public | Cheap, elastic | Low control, shared | Startups, web apps |
| **Private** | Single org | One org | Maximum control, compliance | High CapEx | Banks, government |
| **Hybrid** | Mixed | One org across both | Burst-out, balanced | Complexity in integration | Enterprises with legacy + web tier |
| **Community** | Multiple orgs | Common-interest groups | Cost-shared, domain-specific | Governance complexity | Research consortiums, govt federations |

**Real-world scenarios (each 1 mark):**
- **Public** — Netflix running on AWS.
- **Private** — RBI's internal banking ledger system.
- **Hybrid** — A bank's core banking on private; mobile app front-end bursting to public.
- **Community** — NASA, ESA, and JAXA sharing a space-research cloud.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** *With a suitable example, explain how an organization would decide between **on-premise infrastructure and cloud computing** using a **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** calculation. Show the cost computation for a workload that is **active 12 hours/day** with a **3× peak multiplier** for **4 hours during those 12 hours**. **[7 marks]***
>
> **(b)** *Discuss the **benefits and challenges of cloud computing** in detail. How do issues like **vendor lock-in, data privacy, and downtime** affect adoption? **[7 marks]***

**Model Answer:**

**(a) On-Premise vs. Cloud TCO (7 marks):**

Consider a workload needing **30 vCPUs baseline, 3× peak = 90 vCPUs, 120 GB RAM (baseline), 360 GB RAM (peak), 5 TB storage**.

**On-Premise:** Buy 9 servers (each 10 vCPUs, 40 GB RAM, 1 TB) to support peak. Cost = 9 × \$4,500 = **\$40,500 CapEx**. OpEx = 9 × \$700/month × 12 = **\$75,600/year**.

**TCO On-Premise** = \$116,100 (Year 1).

**Cloud (Hourly billing):**

- **Baseline hours/day = 12** (but only 4 of those are peak). Non-peak hours = 8.
- **Active days = 365.**

Baseline vCPU hours = 30 × 8 × 365 = 87,600 hours.
Peak vCPU hours = 90 × 4 × 365 = 131,400 hours.
**Total vCPU hours = 219,000 hours.** At \$0.04/vCPU-hour = **\$8,760**.

Similarly compute RAM (avg 200 GB × 12 × 365 = 876,000 GB-hours at \$0.005) = **\$4,380**.
Storage = 5000 GB × 12 months × \$0.10 = **\$6,000**.

**TCO Cloud ≈ \$19,140** — a **savings of ≈ 83.5%**.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Cloud is justified when workloads are **spiky and elastic**; on-premise is justified only when utilization is consistently **> 70%**.

---

**(b) Benefits & Challenges (7 marks):**

**Benefits (3.5 marks):**
- **Cost Efficiency** — OpEx over CapEx, no idle resources.
- **Scalability & Elasticity** — handle 10× traffic seamlessly.
- **High Availability & DR** — multi-AZ, multi-region replication.
- **Faster Time-to-Market** — infrastructure in minutes.
- **Global Reach** — deploy worldwide via CDN.

**Challenges (3.5 marks):**
- **Vendor Lock-in** — proprietary APIs (e.g., AWS Lambda vs Azure Functions) make migration hard. Mitigation: use **Kubernetes**, **Terraform**, **multi-cloud abstractions**.
- **Data Privacy & Sovereignty** — GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023 restrict data leaving the country. Mitigation: choose **regional data residency**.
- **Downtime & Internet Dependency** — if cloud or ISP fails, business halts. Mitigation: **multi-cloud failover**, **hybrid setups**, local caching.
- **Security Misconfigurations** — S3 buckets, public IPs — leading cause of breaches.
- **Unpredictable Costs** — runaway bills if scaling is not governed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Missing a diagram** for 7-mark "explain" questions. **Always include a labeled diagram** (worth 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Confusing SaaS with PaaS** when asked. Remember: **SaaS = user just uses the app**; **PaaS = user develops an app on a platform**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "as per NIST"** in the cloud computing definition — examiners love this phrase.
> 4. **Writing "pay-per-use"** without explaining **measured service** — these are different (one is the user's perspective, the other is the provider's metering mechanism).
> 5. **In TCO problems, forgetting OpEx** — many students compute only CapEx. Always show **CapEx + OpEx over the time horizon**.
> 6. **Not converting "99.99% availability" to minutes** — examiners award the ½ mark for the conversion calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (read this on the morning of the exam):**

- [x] **NIST Definition** of cloud computing — quote SP 800-145 verbatim if possible.
- [x] **5 Essential Characteristics** — On-Demand, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- [x] **3 Service Models** — IaaS (you manage OS+above), PaaS (you manage apps+data), SaaS (you manage only data).
- [x] **4 Deployment Models** — Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [x] **5 NIST Actors** — Consumer, Provider, Auditor, Broker, Carrier.
- [x] **NIST SP 500-292** = Reference Architecture; **NIST SP 800-145** = Definition.
- [x] **Key dates** — AWS EC2 launched **Aug 2006**; **AWS Lambda** 2014; **Azure** 2010.
- [x] **Hypervisor** = foundation of cloud (Type-1: bare-metal, e.g., ESXi; Type-2: hosted, e.g., VirtualBox).
- [x] **CapEx vs OpEx** — Cloud converts CapEx into OpEx.
- [x] **Five Nines = 99.999% ≈ 5.26 min/year downtime.**
- [x] **TCO formula:** $TCO = CapEx + OpEx$ (over 3–5 years).
- [x] **Elasticity ratio** $E = C_{peak} / C_{avg}$ — if $E > 1$, cloud is justified.
- [x] **Workload migration decision** — regulated data → private; spiky → public; latency-critical + regulated → hybrid.
- [x] **Virtualization** enables **multi-tenancy**, **isolation**, and **rapid provisioning**.
- [x] **Pay-per-use billing** is metered at the level of **CPU, RAM, Storage, Bandwidth**.
- [x] **Always draw a labeled diagram** for any 7-mark "explain" question.

> **End of Module 1 — Introduction to Cloud Computing Notes. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cloud Computing** (as defined by the **NIST SP 800-145** reference model adopted by KTU syllabus), the term **"Types of Cloud"** refers to the **deployment models** that classify cloud infrastructure based on **ownership, access control, physical location, and provisioning boundaries**. These are the architectural frameworks that determine *who owns* the cloud, *who can access* it, and *how* the underlying compute, storage, and network resources are logically partitioned and exposed.

The four canonical types (as per KTU 2024 Scheme PEADT746 Module 1) are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Canonical Cloud Deployment Models (NIST-Aligned)**
> 1. **Public Cloud** – Infrastructure owned by a third-party provider, open to the general public.
> 2. **Private Cloud** – Infrastructure provisioned exclusively for a single organization.
> 3. **Hybrid Cloud** – Composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures bound by standardized technology.
> 4. **Community Cloud** – Infrastructure shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., security, compliance).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **electricity distribution** in a city:

| Cloud Type | Real-World Analogy | Why it Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | The city's main power grid (KSEB for Kerala students) | Shared, metered, anyone can subscribe, provider owns the wires |
| **Private Cloud** | A captive diesel generator inside a factory | Owned and used by one entity, full control, no outside access |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Using the grid *plus* a backup generator that switches automatically | Mix the best of both — grid for normal load, generator for sensitive/critical load |
| **Community Cloud** | A transformer shared by 5 nearby hospitals (with their own sub-meters) | Shared only among a defined group with a *common purpose* (reliability, compliance) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy clicks:** Just as the *type* of electricity connection changes who controls and who pays for the infrastructure, the *type of cloud* changes who controls and who accesses the compute infrastructure. The **electricity** itself represents **on-demand, elastic, pooled resources** — the same definition of Cloud Computing in every scenario.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Tenancy:** The number of organizations sharing a single physical instance. *Public = multi-tenant; Private = single-tenant.*
- **Provisioning Boundary:** The logical perimeter that separates one tenant's resources from another's.
- **Ephemerality:** Resources that exist only for the duration of a request (e.g., AWS Lambda functions) — strongly associated with public cloud.
- **Cloud Bursting:** A hybrid-cloud pattern where an application *bursts* into public cloud when private cloud load spikes.
- **On-Premises (On-Prem):** The legacy alternative to private cloud — bare-metal/hypervisor stack owned by the org, **not** a cloud type per NIST.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Is On-Premises a type of cloud?"* — Answer: **No**. On-Premises is *not* a cloud model under NIST; it is the *opposite* of cloud (no elastic, on-demand, self-service provisioning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Cloud Types — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Public Cloud

**Definition (NIST):** *"The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for open use by the general public. It may be owned, managed, and operated by a business, academic, or government organization, or some combination of them. It exists on the premises of the cloud provider."*

**Why it works (The "How"):**
- **Economies of Scale:** A single AWS/Azure region serves millions of tenants → cost per VM drops to cents per hour.
- **Multi-tenancy via Hypervisor:** KVM, VMware ESXi, or AWS Nitro System partitions physical hardware into isolated VMs.
- **Self-Service Portal:** The user (developer) requests resources via a REST API — no human ticket needed.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. User authenticates via **IAM (Identity and Access Management)**.
2. User submits a provisioning request (e.g., `ec2 run-instances`).
3. Cloud Controller (OpenStack Nova / AWS EC2 Control Plane) selects a hypervisor host with capacity.
4. Hypervisor boots the VM in a **VLAN-isolated** network namespace.
5. Tenant receives credentials over an **out-of-band channel** (email/API response).

**Pros:** Lowest CapEx, near-infinite elasticity, global reach.
**Cons:** Latency to data, regulatory/compliance concerns for sensitive workloads.

> **Real-world utility:** Netflix runs on AWS Public Cloud — streaming to 260M+ users with auto-scaling encoding clusters.

---

#### 2.1.2 Private Cloud

**Definition (NIST):** *"The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization comprising multiple business units. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization, a third party, or some combination, and it may exist on or off premises."*

**Why it works:**
- **Single-Tenancy:** No other organization shares the physical substrate.
- **Strong Governance:** ISO 27001, HIPAA, RBI compliance achievable because *you control the stack*.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Org deploys a cloud platform (OpenStack, VMware vSphere, Azure Stack).
2. Internal IT acts as the **Internal Service Provider (ISP)**.
3. Workloads run inside the org's **own data center** or a dedicated hosted facility.
4. Network isolation is enforced at the **physical/firewall layer** (no internet exposure of management plane).

**Pros:** Maximum control, compliance, data sovereignty.
**Cons:** High CapEx (hardware purchase), underutilization risk, longer provisioning cycles.

> **Real-world utility:** Banking core systems (e.g., SBI, RBI's internal CIMS) use private cloud for regulatory reasons.

---

#### 2.1.3 Hybrid Cloud

**Definition (NIST):** *"The cloud infrastructure is a composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private, community, or public) that remain unique entities, but are bound together by standardized or proprietary technology that enables data and application portability."*

**Why it works:**
- **Cloud Bursting:** A web tier in private cloud scales out to public cloud when load > 80%.
- **Tiered Workload Placement:** Sensitive data stays on-prem; frontend scales in public cloud.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. **VPN / Direct Connect (DX)** establishes Layer 3 connectivity between private and public.
2. **Federated Identity** (e.g., SAML, OAuth 2.0) allows SSO across both environments.
3. **Orchestrator** (e.g., Kubernetes Federation, Anthos, Azure Arc) treats both as one logical cluster.
4. **Data Egress/Ingress** is governed by **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** policies.

**Pros:** Best of both worlds, gradual migration path, regulatory compliance + elasticity.
**Cons:** Complex networking, double management overhead, inter-cloud latency.

> **Real-world utility:** **GE Oil & Gas** uses hybrid — simulation jobs burst to AWS while proprietary reservoir data stays on private.

---

#### 2.1.4 Community Cloud

**Definition (NIST):** *"The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for exclusive use by a specific community of consumers from organizations that have shared concerns (e.g., mission, security requirements, policy, and compliance considerations). It may be owned, managed, and operated by one or more of the organizations in the community, a third party, or some combination."*

**Why it works:**
- **Shared Mission:** Multiple hospitals share a HIPAA-compliant cloud.
- **Cost Amortization:** Several government departments share an e-governance private cloud.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. A **consortium** of organizations forms (e.g., Kerala Government + All Med College IT heads).
2. They jointly fund a **shared infrastructure** (could be hosted on any of their premises or a third-party DC).
3. A **joint governance board** defines access, cost-splitting, and SLAs.
4. Members access only their slice; cross-org data sharing is governed by **Memorandums of Understanding (MoUs)**.

**Pros:** Shared compliance burden, lower cost than private, mission-aligned.
**Cons:** Governance complexity, slower decision-making.

> **Real-world utility:** **U.S. Government Cloud (GovCloud)** and **Google "Gov" Cloud** are community clouds for federal agencies.

---

### 2.2 The "Fifth Type" — Emerging Classification

Modern KTU supplementary reading often includes two newer categories:

| Type | Description | KTU Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-Cloud** | Using >1 public cloud providers (AWS + Azure + GCP) to avoid vendor lock-in | Mentioned in 2024 syllabus updates |
| **Distributed Cloud** | Public cloud services distributed to different physical locations, but logically one cloud (e.g., Google Anthos, AWS Outposts) | Supplementary, not core |
| **HPC Cloud** | High-Performance Computing workloads (CFD, genomics) — uses specialized hardware | Niche elective |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam), the **four NIST types** (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) are the *primary* testable classification. Multi-cloud is *good-to-know* for viva.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since "Types of Cloud" is a classification topic, there are no numerical formulas — but there are **comparison metrics** that KTU frequently tests. Memorize the table below:

| Metric | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant | Single-tenant | Mixed | Multi-tenant (limited group) |
| **Owner** | Third-party provider | Single org | Mixed | Consortium / 3rd party |
| **Location** | Provider's DC | Org's DC (or hosted) | Both | Member's DC / 3rd party |
| **Cost Model** | OpEx (pay-as-you-go) | CapEx + OpEx | Both | Shared CapEx + OpEx |
| **Scalability** | Very High (elastic) | Limited (capped) | Very High | Moderate |
| **Security** | Provider-managed | Org-managed | Layered | Shared governance |
| **Compliance** | Provider certs (ISO, SOC2) | Org-specific (HIPAA, RBI) | Both | Mission-specific |
| **Examples** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack on-prem | AWS + on-prem DB | GovCloud, Healthcare shared |
| **Internet Exposure** | Yes (by design) | No (typically) | Partial | Controlled |
| **Provisioning Time** | Seconds–Minutes | Hours–Days | Minutes | Hours |

> [!TIP]
> KTU marking scheme: In a 14-mark question comparing cloud types, you get **1 mark per distinct point of comparison** (up to 7 comparisons × 2 marks each for 7+7 split) — always draw a **comparison table** in the exam; it scores higher than prose.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Startups** → Public Cloud (no upfront capital).
- **Defense/Banking** → Private Cloud (sovereignty, air-gapping).
- **E-commerce sale days (e.g., Flipkart Big Billion Days)** → Hybrid (own DC for orders, burst to public for spikes).
- **Smart City / E-Governance** → Community Cloud (multiple municipal bodies share infra).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Cloud Type Should I Choose?"

The most common KTU question on this topic is: *"A startup wants to build an application. Recommend the cloud type and justify."* or *"A bank's database has PII. Which deployment model is suitable?"*

Here is the **exhaustive step-by-step decision logic** you should reproduce in your exam:

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Workload Sensitivity (Confidentiality)**
- Is the data **PII / PHI / PCI** (Personal/Health/Payment)?
  - If **YES** → **Private or Community** (or hybrid with private as the primary store).
  - If **NO** → Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2 — Identify the Capital Availability**
- Is **CapEx budget < ₹50 Lakh** (typical for KTU's project-based startups)?
  - If **YES** → **Public Cloud** (OpEx model, ₹0 upfront).
  - If **NO** → Proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Identify the Workload Pattern**
- Is the workload **spiky** (e.g., 100x traffic on a sale day)?
  - If **YES** → **Hybrid** (base load on private, burst to public).
  - If **NO** (steady) → **Private Cloud**.

**Step 4 — Identify the Stakeholder Community**
- Are there **multiple organizations with a shared mission** (e.g., all Kerala engineering colleges sharing a research HPC)?
  - If **YES** → **Community Cloud**.
  - If **NO** → Final answer from Steps 1–3.

---

### 3.2 The Cloud Computing Definition — Foundational Derivation

The **NIST Definition of Cloud Computing** has 5 essential characteristics. Every KTU module 1 question implicitly tests these. Derive them here:

**Starting Premise (NIST SP 800-145):**
> *"Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."*

**The 5 Essential Characteristics (derived/listed from the definition):**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service**
   - User provisions resources *without human intervention*.
   - Derivation: The phrase *"on-demand"* in the NIST definition = self-service.

2. **Broad Network Access**
   - Resources reachable over the network via *standard mechanisms* (HTTP, REST).
   - Derivation: *"network access"* + *"ubiquitous, convenient"*.

3. **Resource Pooling**
   - Multi-tenant model with physical/virtual resources pooled.
   - Derivation: *"shared pool of configurable computing resources"*.

4. **Rapid Elasticity**
   - Capabilities can be *scaled out/in* quickly.
   - Derivation: *"rapidly provisioned and released"*.

5. **Measured Service**
   - Resource usage is *metered* (CPU-hr, GB, API calls).
   - Derivation: *"minimal management effort"* implies automated metering.

> **The 3 Service Models** (referenced in module 1, expanded in later modules): IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
> **The 4 Deployment Models** (this topic): Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.

---

### 3.3 The Cloud-Service-Stack (Layered Architecture) — Derivation

Although strictly Module 1 is "Types of Cloud," KTU often expects you to position deployment types *against* the service stack. Derive the layered architecture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top (User-Facing)} &= \textbf{SaaS} \;\; \text{(Software as a Service)} \\
&\quad \text{e.g., Gmail, Office 365} \\
\text{Mid-Upper} &= \textbf{PaaS} \;\; \text{(Platform as a Service)} \\
&\quad \text{e.g., Google App Engine, Heroku} \\
\text{Mid-Lower} &= \textbf{IaaS} \;\; \text{(Infrastructure as a Service)} \\
&\quad \text{e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VM} \\
\text{Bottom (Hardware)} &= \textbf{Servers, Storage, Network} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **deployment model** (Public/Private/Hybrid/Community) is **orthogonal** to the **service model** (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). You can have *Private IaaS*, *Public SaaS*, *Hybrid PaaS*, *Community SaaS* — all are valid combinations.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Cost Derivations (Optional — KTU Advanced)

For a 14-mark question, you may need to show the **economic difference** between deployment types. A simplified **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** model:

$$
TCO_{\text{private}} = C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{power}} + C_{\text{cooling}} + C_{\text{IT-staff}} + C_{\text{licensing}}
$$

$$
TCO_{\text{public}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (u_i \times p_i) + C_{\text{egress}} + C_{\text{support-plan}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{hardware}}$ = CapEx for servers, switches, storage arrays.
- $C_{\text{power}}$, $C_{\text{cooling}}$ = Data center utility bills.
- $C_{\text{IT-staff}}$ = Salaries of sysadmins.
- $u_i$ = Usage of resource $i$ (e.g., vCPU-hours).
- $p_i$ = Price of resource $i$ (e.g., ₹5/vCPU-hour for AWS m5.large in Mumbai region).
- $C_{\text{egress}}$ = Data transfer out costs (₹0.09/GB on AWS as of 2024).

**Break-even Insight:**
For *steady-state* workloads with > 70% utilization, **Private Cloud** TCO wins. For *bursty* workloads with < 30% average utilization, **Public Cloud** TCO wins. **Hybrid** is preferred when you have a *mixed profile*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cloud Computing Deployment Models] --> B[Public Cloud]
    A --> C[Private Cloud]
    A --> D[Hybrid Cloud]
    A --> E[Community Cloud]
    A --> F[Other Emerging Models]

    B --> B1[Open to General Public]
    B --> B2[Owned by Third Party e.g. AWS Azure GCP]
    B --> B3[Multi Tenant]
    B --> B4[High Elasticity]

    C --> C1[Exclusive to Single Org]
    C --> C2[On Premises or Hosted]
    C --> C3[Single Tenant]
    C --> C4[Maximum Control]

    D --> D1[Public plus Private]
    D --> D2[Bound by Standard Tech e.g. VPN]
    D --> D3[Cloud Bursting Enabled]
    D --> D4[Mixed Tenancy]

    E --> E1[Shared by Community of Orgs]
    E --> E2[Common Compliance or Mission]
    E --> E3[Governed by Consortium]
    E --> E4[Examples GovCloud HealthCloud]

    F --> F1[Multi Cloud AWS plus Azure plus GCP]
    F --> F2[Distributed Cloud e.g. Anthos Outposts]
    F --> F3[HPC Cloud]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Flow — Hybrid Cloud with Cloud Bursting

```mermaid
graph LR
    user[End User / Mobile App] --> lb[Load Balancer in Private DC]

    subgraph privateCloud[Private Cloud On Premises]
        lb --> app1[App Server 1]
        lb --> app2[App Server 2]
        lb --> app3[App Server 3]
        db[(Private Database PII)]
    end

    app1 -->|Threshold 80 percent| burst[Cloud Burst Controller]
    app2 -->|Threshold 80 percent| burst
    app3 -->|Threshold 80 percent| burst

    burst -->|Provision new VMs| publicCloud[Public Cloud e.g. AWS]

    subgraph publicCloudRegion[Public Cloud AWS Azure GCP]
        pubApp1[Burst VM 4]
        pubApp2[Burst VM 5]
        pubApp3[Burst VM 6]
    end

    pubApp1 -->|Read Replica Only| db
    pubApp2 -->|Read Replica Only| db
    pubApp3 -->|Read Replica Only| db

    style privateCloud fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    style publicCloudRegion fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style burst fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Tenancy & Control

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph publicModel[Public Cloud Tenancy Model]
        p1[Tenant A] --> sp1[Shared Physical Server]
        p2[Tenant B] --> sp1
        p3[Tenant C] --> sp1
        sp1 --> hv1[Hypervisor KVM ESXi]
    end

    subgraph privateModel[Private Cloud Tenancy Model]
        pr1[Tenant A Only Single Org] --> sp2[Dedicated Physical Server]
        sp2 --> hv2[Hypervisor or Bare Metal]
    end

    style publicModel fill:#e1f5fe
    style privateModel fill:#fce4ec
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Selecting Cloud Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is data highly sensitive PII PHI PCI?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need to share infra with other orgs?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is CapEx budget limited?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Community Cloud]
    Q2 -->|No| A2[Private Cloud]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is workload spiky?}
    Q3 -->|No| A2

    Q4 -->|Yes| A3[Hybrid Cloud]
    Q4 -->|No| A4[Public Cloud]

    style Q1 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q2 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q3 fill:#fff9c4
    style Q4 fill:#fff9c4
    style A1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style A4 fill:#c8e6c9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `publicModel`, `privateModel`) — none use reserved keywords. All labels are quoted plain text, no embedded markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*"Define Community Cloud. Give one real-world example."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Community Cloud is a cloud deployment model in which the infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a specific community of consumers** from organizations that have **shared concerns** such as mission, security requirements, policy, or compliance considerations. It may be owned, managed, and operated by one or more of the organizations in the community, a third party, or a combination.
- **Example (1 mark):** *U.S. Government Cloud (AWS GovCloud)* — used exclusively by U.S. federal agencies with FedRAMP compliance requirements. Alternatively, *Google Cloud for Healthcare* with HIPAA-aligned controls can be cited.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*"List any three essential characteristics of Cloud Computing as per NIST."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **On-Demand Self-Service:** A consumer can provision computing capabilities (server time, network storage) automatically without requiring human interaction with each service provider.
2. **Broad Network Access:** Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms (mobile, laptop, PDA).
3. **Rapid Elasticity:** Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward commensurate with demand.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Resource Pooling, Measured Service — 3 out of 5 needed.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 marks]**
*"Explain the four cloud deployment models as defined by NIST. Compare any two of them in detail."*

**Model Solution:**

**(a.i) The Four NIST Cloud Deployment Models (4 marks)**

1. **Public Cloud:** The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for **open use by the general public**. It is owned, managed, and operated by a business, academic, or government organization (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP). It resides on the provider's premises. Resources are pooled using multi-tenancy.

2. **Private Cloud:** The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** comprising multiple business units. It may exist on or off premises and may be operated by the organization itself or a third party. Examples: OpenStack on a bank's internal datacenter, AWS Outposts deployed in-house.

3. **Hybrid Cloud:** The cloud infrastructure is a **composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, community, or public) that remain unique entities but are bound together by **standardized or proprietary technology** that enables data and application portability (e.g., cloud bursting, federated identity).

4. **Community Cloud:** The cloud infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a specific community of consumers** from organizations that have shared concerns (mission, security, policy, compliance). It is jointly owned by the community members or a third party. Example: AWS GovCloud for U.S. federal agencies.

**(a.ii) Detailed Comparison: Public vs. Private Cloud (3 marks)**

| Comparison Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Ownership & Control** | Owned and managed by a third-party cloud provider (e.g., AWS) | Owned and managed by a single organization |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant — resources shared across many customers | Single-tenant — exclusive use by one organization |
| **Location** | Provider's data center (geographically distributed) | On-premises or dedicated hosted facility |
| **Cost Model** | OpEx (operational expense) — pay per use | CapEx (capital expense) + OpEx — upfront investment + maintenance |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited, elastic in seconds | Limited by physical hardware; requires procurement cycle |
| **Security & Compliance** | Provider-managed (ISO 27001, SOC 2) | Organization-controlled (customizable for HIPAA, RBI, defense) |
| **Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine | OpenStack on-prem, VMware vSphere, Azure Stack |
| **Internet Exposure** | Always (by design) | Typically isolated, internal-network only |

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 marks]**
*"A regional bank in Kerala needs to process 10 million transactions per day. It has strict RBI compliance requirements and operates its own data center. However, it wants to add a fraud-detection AI service that requires massive GPU compute for short bursts. Recommend a suitable cloud deployment model and justify with a diagram."*

**Model Solution:**

**(b.i) Recommendation: Hybrid Cloud (1 mark)**
The bank should adopt a **Hybrid Cloud** model.

**(b.ii) Justification (5 marks):**
- **Regulatory Compliance (RBI):** The bank's transaction data is **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)** and is subject to RBI's data-localization mandate. Storing it on a public cloud would violate compliance. Therefore, the **core transaction processing tier must remain on the private cloud** (in the bank's own data center).
- **Bursty AI Compute:** Fraud detection using deep learning requires **GPU instances** (e.g., NVIDIA A100) for short, intensive model-inference jobs. Buying dedicated GPU hardware for peak bursts is capital-inefficient (low utilization most of the time). Hence, this workload should **burst to the public cloud** (e.g., AWS p4d instances) only when fraud alerts spike.
- **Architecture Components:**
  1. **Private Cloud (on-prem):** Holds the transaction database, core banking application, and the master copy of the fraud-detection model.
  2. **Public Cloud (AWS/Azure):** Holds **stateless GPU worker nodes** that pull anonymized/aggregated transaction features (no raw PII) for inference.
  3. **Connectivity:** A **VPN / AWS Direct Connect** link (encrypted, 1–10 Gbps) joins the two.
  4. **Data Privacy Filter:** A **tokenization service** ensures raw PII (account number, name) is stripped before data crosses to the public cloud.
  5. **Orchestrator:** A **Kubernetes Federation (e.g., Anthos, EKS Anywhere)** schedules the GPU pods automatically when CPU utilization > 70%.

**(b.iii) Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    user[Customer UPI Transaction] --> privateDC[Private Cloud Bank DC]

    subgraph privateCloud[Private Cloud On Premises RBI Compliant]
        privateDC --> coreApp[Core Banking App]
        privateDC --> db[(Transaction DB Encrypted)]
        coreApp --> fraud1[Fraud Model Master]
    end

    fraud1 -->|Tokenized features only| vpn[VPN Direct Connect]

    subgraph publicCloud[Public Cloud AWS p4d GPU]
        vpn --> gpu1[GPU Worker 1]
        vpn --> gpu2[GPU Worker 2]
        vpn --> gpu3[GPU Worker 3 Burst]
    end

    gpu1 -->|Fraud Score| vpn
    gpu2 -->|Fraud Score| vpn
    gpu3 -->|Fraud Score| vpn
    vpn --> fraud1

    style privateCloud fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    style publicCloud fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style vpn fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) [7 marks]**
*"With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Private Cloud. What are the key components of its software stack? Compare it with a Public Cloud."*

**Model Solution:**

**(a.i) Private Cloud Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph dcLayer[Physical Data Center Layer]
        rack1[Server Rack 1]
        rack2[Server Rack 2]
        san[(SAN Storage Array)]
        netSw[Top-of-Rack Switches]
    end

    subgraph virtLayer[Virtualization Layer]
        hv1[KVM Hypervisor]
        hv2[VMware ESXi]
    end

    subgraph cloudOS[Private Cloud Operating System]
        nova[Compute Controller OpenStack Nova]
        neutron[Network Controller Neutron]
        cinder[Storage Controller Cinder]
        keystone[Identity Service Keystone]
        horizon[Dashboard Horizon]
    end

    subgraph consumerLayer[Consumer Layer]
        dev[Internal Developer]
        admin[IT Admin]
        app[Internal Application]
    end

    rack1 --> hv1
    rack2 --> hv2
    hv1 --> nova
    hv2 --> nova
    san --> cinder
    netSw --> neutron
    keystone --> nova
    keystone --> neutron
    keystone --> cinder
    horizon --> keystone
    dev --> horizon
    admin --> horizon
    app --> nova

    style dcLayer fill:#ffecb3
    style virtLayer fill:#c8e6c9
    style cloudOS fill:#bbdefb
    style consumerLayer fill:#f8bbd0
```

**(a.ii) Key Software Stack Components (2 marks):**
1. **Hypervisor** — KVM, VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (provides virtualization).
2. **Cloud Operating System** — OpenStack / CloudStack (provides the "cloud" APIs: compute, network, storage, identity).
3. **Orchestration Layer** — Heat (OpenStack) or Terraform for infrastructure-as-code.
4. **Management Dashboard** — Horizon (OpenStack UI) or vSphere Web Client.
5. **Identity & Access Management** — Keystone (OpenStack) or Active Directory Federation Services.

**(a.iii) Comparison with Public Cloud (2 marks):**

| Feature | Private Cloud | Public Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Setup Cost** | High (hardware procurement) | Near zero (subscription) |
| **Time to Deploy First VM** | Weeks (procurement + install) | Minutes (API call) |
| **Control Over Hardware** | Full (you own it) | None (provider abstracts it) |
| **Internet Bandwidth Cost** | Internal, free | Metered; egress charged |
| **Maintenance Responsibility** | Internal IT team | Shared (provider does hardware, you do OS/app) |

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 marks]**
*"What is Cloud Bursting? Describe its working with a real-world example. List two challenges of implementing cloud bursting."*

**Model Solution:**

**(b.i) Definition (2 marks):**
**Cloud Bursting** is a hybrid cloud deployment pattern in which an application running in a **private cloud** automatically *bursts* (provisions additional capacity) into a **public cloud** when the demand exceeds the private cloud's available capacity. Once the demand subsides, the extra public-cloud resources are released. This enables an organization to handle peak loads without permanently over-provisioning its private infrastructure.

**(b.ii) Working Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Baseline Deployment:** The application is deployed on a private cloud sized for *average* load (e.g., 10 application servers handling 5,000 requests/min).
2. **Monitoring:** A capacity monitor (e.g., AWS CloudWatch, Prometheus) continuously tracks metrics like CPU utilization, request queue depth, and response latency.
3. **Trigger Evaluation:** When a threshold is crossed (e.g., CPU > 80% for 5 minutes), the **Cloud Burst Controller** (a policy engine) decides to burst.
4. **Burst Provisioning:** The controller calls the public cloud API (e.g., AWS Auto Scaling `run-instances`) to launch N additional VMs in the public region.
5. **Network Integration:** The new public-cloud VMs are attached to a **shared load balancer** that distributes incoming traffic. They communicate with the private-cloud database over a **secure VPN/Direct Connect**.
6. **Scale-In:** When load drops below a lower threshold (e.g., 30% CPU for 15 minutes), the extra public VMs are terminated.

**(b.iii) Real-World Example (1 mark):**
**Flipkart / Amazon** during a festive sale (Big Billion Days / Prime Day). Baseline capacity runs in their own data centers; during the sale window, traffic may spike 10–50×. Cloud bursting into **AWS / Azure** absorbs the spike, ensuring cart/checkout latency stays under 2 seconds. After the sale, the public-cloud VMs are auto-terminated.

**(b.iv) Challenges (1 mark — list any 2):**
1. **Data Gravity / Latency:** Public-cloud workers need to fetch data from the private database. If the database is large, the bandwidth and latency become the bottleneck.
2. **Stateful Workloads:** Storing session state on ephemeral public VMs is hard; requires shared session stores (Redis) replicated across both environments.
3. **Security & Compliance:** Even tokenized data crossing the VPN must be encrypted, audited, and compliant with regulations like RBI or GDPR.
4. **Cost Unpredictability:** A misconfigured threshold can spin up thousands of VMs in a runaway loop, leading to six-figure AWS bills.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Types of Cloud" Questions**
> 1. **Confusing Deployment vs. Service Models:** Many students write *"Public Cloud = SaaS"* — this is **wrong**. Public Cloud is a *deployment* model; SaaS is a *service* model. They are orthogonal. *(–2 marks)*
> 2. **Writing "On-Premises" as a type of cloud:** On-premises is *not* a cloud model. The phrase "private cloud on-premises" is acceptable, but bare-metal on-prem is not cloud. *(–1 mark)*
> 3. **Missing the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** A 7-mark sub-part typically expects **3 marks for content + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for example/comparison**. Skipping the diagram costs at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Not specifying ownership/tenancy in definitions:** The phrase *"infrastructure provisioned for exclusive use by..."* is the KTU-blessed phrasing. Generic definitions like *"cloud for an organization"* lose 1 mark for imprecision.
> 5. **Forgetting to justify a recommendation:** If asked "which cloud for X?", simply naming the type gets 1–2 marks. The full 7 marks requires a **justification chain** (compliance → cost → scalability → security).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Types of Cloud"**
> - ☐ **Four NIST Types:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community — know the exact NIST definition wording.
> - ☐ **Three Service Models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — orthogonal to deployment models.
> - ☐ **Five Essential Characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
> - ☐ **Key Distinction:** Public = multi-tenant / third-party-owned; Private = single-tenant / org-owned.
> - ☐ **Hybrid = 2+ distinct clouds** *bound by standardized technology* (this phrase is the key KTU buzzword).
> - ☐ **Community = shared mission** (e.g., government, healthcare) — *not* the same as Public.
> - ☐ **Cloud Bursting** = hybrid pattern where private bursts to public on demand spike.
> - ☐ **Tenancy, Elasticity, Compliance, Cost** are the *four axes* you should compare in any 7-mark comparison question.
> - ☐ **Real-world examples** for each type (AWS = Public; in-house OpenStack = Private; Flipkart = Hybrid; GovCloud = Community).
> - ☐ **On-Premises ≠ Cloud** (per NIST) — bare-metal in your own DC is not a cloud type.
> - ☐ **Multi-Cloud ≠ Hybrid** — Multi-cloud = multiple *public* providers; Hybrid = public + private/community mix.
> - ☐ **Remember the 3 As:** *Access* (broad network), *Allocation* (pooling), *Accounting* (measured) — the 3 of 5 NIST characteristics most often forgotten in exams.
> - ☐ **Draw a labeled diagram** for every ≥ 5-mark sub-question. Mermaid-style block diagrams are best because they are unambiguous in pen-and-paper.
> - ☐ **End every recommendation answer** with a clear sentence: *"Hence, the recommended cloud type is [X], justified by [Y]."* — examiners award a free 1 mark for a clear conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Working of Cloud Computing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Cloud Computing** is a paradigm that enables ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction, as defined by the **NIST SP 800-145** reference model adopted in the KTU 2024 curriculum. Mathematically, a cloud system can be abstracted as a tuple of services delivered over a network: $$C_{cloud} = \{ R, S, P, A, M \}$$ Where: - $R$ = Pool of configurable physical/virtual resources - $S$ = Service abstraction layer (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) - $P$ = Provisioning & orchestration policies - $A$ = APIs & access control mechanisms - $M$ = Management & monitoring layer > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly defines the *working* of cloud computing through the interaction of **Virtualization**, **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)**, **Web Services**, and **Utility Computing** layers. You must remember all four for 14-mark questions. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electricity Grid" Imagine cloud computing as the **electricity grid** in your home. - You do **not** build your own power plant to light a bulb. You simply plug into a socket. - You pay only for the units (kilowatt-hours) you consume. - The grid provider (NTPC, KSEB) handles generation, distribution, billing, and maintenance invisibly. Similarly, in cloud computing: - You do **not** buy physical servers, storage racks, or cooling systems. You "plug into" the internet. - You pay only for the compute/storage/bandwidth you consume (pay-per-use). - The cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) handles hardware, virtualization, scaling, and security invisibly. > [!NOTE] > **Core Characteristics (NIST — Must memorize for KTU):** > 1. **On-demand self-service** — Provision resources automatically without human interaction. > 2. **Broad network access** — Available over the internet via standard mechanisms. > 3. **Resource pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic reassignment of physical/virtual resources. > 4. **Rapid elasticity** — Resources scale out/in quickly, appearing unlimited to the consumer. > 5. **Measured service** — Metering at appropriate abstraction (storage, processing, bandwidth, active users). ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Cloud Working These are the **standard metrics** used to measure the working efficiency of a cloud system: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Description | |---|---|---|---| | Latency | $L$ | milliseconds (ms) | Time delay between request and response | | Throughput | $T_h$ | requests/sec (RPS) | Number of tasks processed per unit time | | Availability | $A_v$ | % (e.g., 99.99%) | Uptime ratio of the service | | Scalability | $S_c$ | horizontal/vertical nodes | Ability to handle load growth | | Utilization | $U$ | % | Average use of provisioned resources | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Exams:** When KTU asks "Explain the working of cloud computing," the answer must always include the **request → orchestration → virtualization → resource allocation → monitoring → billing** lifecycle. Marks are awarded for mentioning this complete loop. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Working Architecture — Layer-by-Layer Breakdown The working of cloud computing is governed by a **5-layer functional stack**. Each layer plays a distinct role when a user request is processed. #### Layer 1 — User/Client Layer - The end-user interacts with the cloud via a **thin client**, **web browser**, **mobile app**, or **API call**. - Sends service requests using protocols like **HTTP/HTTPS**, **REST**, or **gRPC**. - Receives responses and pays (in metered environments) for consumed resources. #### Layer 2 — Service Layer (Front-End) - Acts as the **gateway** between the user and the backend. - Implements authentication (**OAuth 2.0**, **SAML**), **load balancing**, and **SSL termination**. - Routes requests to the appropriate microservice or VM. #### Layer 3 — Orchestration & Management Layer (The Brain) This is the **heart of cloud working**. It contains: - **Service Orchestrator** — Coordinates resource allocation across data centers. - **SLA Manager** — Ensures the **Service Level Agreement** between user and provider is honored. - **Scheduler** — Decides which physical server hosts which VM. - **Metering & Billing Module** — Tracks usage for pay-per-use billing. #### Layer 4 — Virtualization Layer - Uses a **Hypervisor** (Type-1: VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM; Type-2: VirtualBox) to abstract physical hardware. - Creates **Virtual Machines (VMs)** or **containers** (Docker, Kubernetes pods) for each tenant. - Enables **multi-tenancy** — multiple users sharing the same physical host securely. #### Layer 5 — Physical Infrastructure Layer - Comprises actual **servers**, **storage arrays (SAN/NAS)**, **network switches**, and **data centers** spread globally. - Managed by the provider — invisible to the user. > [!NOTE] > **Why this layer matters for KTU:** The orchestration layer is what makes cloud computing *elastic*. Without it, you simply have a hosting service, not a cloud. ### 2.2 The Working Lifecycle — Step-by-Step The complete working of a cloud system follows a **6-step request lifecycle**: 1. **Request Initiation** — User sends a request (e.g., "Launch an Ubuntu VM with 4 GB RAM"). 2. **Authentication & Authorization** — Identity is verified; quota is checked. 3. **Resource Discovery** — Orchestrator scans available capacity across data centers. 4. **Virtualization & Allocation** — Hypervisor creates a VM/container on a chosen physical host. 5. **Service Delivery** — User receives IP, credentials, and access to the VM. 6. **Monitoring & Billing** — Usage is metered; auto-scaling may trigger; bill is generated at termination. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cloud Working Mathematics | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Pay-per-use cost | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times u_i \times t_i)$ | $r_i$ = unit rate, $u_i$ = units used, $t_i$ = time | | Availability (SLA) | $A_v = \dfrac{U_{time}}{U_{time} + D_{time}} \times 100$ | $U$ = uptime, $D$ = downtime | | Downtime per year (99.9% SLA) | $D_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 0.001 = 525.6$ min | "Three nines" rule | | Downtime per year (99.99% SLA) | $D_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 0.0001 = 52.56$ min | "Four nines" rule | | Throughput | $T_h = \dfrac{N_{tasks}}{T_{window}}$ | tasks/second | | Resource Utilization | $U = \dfrac{R_{used}}{R_{provisioned}} \times 100$ | percentage | | Elasticity ratio | $E_r = \dfrac{R_{peak}}{R_{baseline}}$ | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is forbidden inside markdown table cells. For absolute value or modulus, always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table corruption. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Healthcare**: Hospitals use cloud elasticity to handle MRI processing bursts during emergencies. - **E-Commerce**: Flipkart/Amazon scale from 100 to 100,000 servers on Black Friday. - **IoT**: Smart sensors offload data to cloud for AI processing. - **Research**: CERN uses 1+ exabyte of cloud storage for LHC data analysis. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of Cloud Working ### 3.1 The Request-to-Delivery Pipeline — Exhaustive Mathematical Model Let us model the complete working of a cloud computing system when a user requests a virtual machine. Let the user's request be denoted as $Q$ (a tuple containing VM type, OS, RAM, CPU cores, and storage). $$Q = \{ v_{type}, OS, R_{ram}, N_{cpu}, S_{storage} \}$$ #### Step 1: Request Generation and Submission The user (client application or browser) sends the request $Q$ over an **HTTPS-secured REST API** to the cloud provider's front-end. $$Q \xrightarrow{\text{HTTPS/TLS}} \text{API Gateway}$$ The total request size in bytes: $$S_{req} = \text{sizeof}(v_{type}) + \text{sizeof}(OS) + \text{sizeof}(R_{ram}) + \text{sizeof}(N_{cpu}) + \text{sizeof}(S_{storage})$$ The time taken for the request to reach the gateway (network latency $L$): $$T_{transit} = L_{propagation} + L_{transmission} + L_{queue}$$ #### Step 2: Authentication and SLA Validation The API gateway forwards credentials to the **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** module. $$f_{auth}(U, P) \rightarrow \{ \text{Valid}, \text{Invalid} \}$$ If valid, the **SLA Manager** checks whether the requested configuration is within the user's quota: $$Q_{accepted} \iff (R_{ram} \leq R_{quota}) \land (N_{cpu} \leq N_{quota}) \land (S_{storage} \leq S_{quota})$$ If any of these conditions fail, the request is rejected with an HTTP 403 Forbidden response. #### Step 3: Resource Discovery Across Data Centers The **Service Orchestrator** queries all available data center regions to find optimal placement. The cost function for placing a VM in region $i$ is: $$C_i = w_1 \cdot D_{latency, i} + w_2 \cdot P_{price, i} + w_3 \cdot L_{load, i}$$ Where: - $D_{latency, i}$ = network distance to user's nearest edge - $P_{price, i}$ = per-hour cost in region $i$ - $L_{load, i}$ = current load on region $i$'s servers - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = weighted policy coefficients such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ The orchestrator selects the optimal region: $$i_{optimal} = \arg\min_{i \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}} C_i$$ #### Step 4: Virtualization and Resource Allocation A **Type-1 hypervisor** (e.g., KVM) running on the selected physical host $H_j$ creates a new VM. The host's available resources are checked: $$R_{avail, j} = R_{total, j} - \sum_{m=1}^{M} R_{used, m}$$ Where $M$ is the number of currently running VMs on host $j$. The allocation succeeds if and only if: $$R_{avail, j} \geq R_{ram} \quad \text{AND} \quad N_{avail, j}^{cpu} \geq N_{cpu}$$ The hypervisor then performs: - Memory mapping using **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** - CPU scheduling via **credit-based algorithms** - I/O virtualization using **virtio drivers** #### Step 5: Service Delivery The user is provided with: - A public IP address - SSH/RDP credentials - A DNS-mapped hostname The service becomes reachable after the boot time $T_{boot}$: $$T_{total\_delivery} = T_{transit} + T_{auth} + T_{discovery} + T_{allocate} + T_{boot}$$ #### Step 6: Continuous Monitoring, Auto-Scaling, and Billing Throughout the VM's lifetime, the **Monitoring Agent** samples resource usage at fixed intervals $\Delta t$: $$\overline{U}_{cpu} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} U_{cpu}(t_k)$$ If $\overline{U}_{cpu} > 80\%$ for sustained periods, **horizontal auto-scaling** triggers: $$N_{vm}^{new} = \lceil N_{vm}^{old} \times S_{factor} \rceil$$ Where $S_{factor} = 1.5$ is a typical scaling factor. At termination, the final bill is calculated using the pay-per-use formula: $$B_{final} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times u_i \times t_i)$$ $$B_{final} = r_{cpu} \cdot N_{cpu} \cdot T_{active} + r_{ram} \cdot R_{ram} \cdot T_{active} + r_{storage} \cdot S_{storage} \cdot T_{active}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A user provisions an AWS EC2 instance with 2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM, and 100 GB storage for 30 days. Rates: $r_{cpu} = \$0.04$/vCPU/hr, $r_{ram} = \$0.005$/GB/hr, $r_{storage} = \$0.0001$/GB/hr. Calculate the bill. **Solution:** Total active hours: $T_{active} = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours. $$B_{cpu} = 0.04 \times 2 \times 720 = \$57.60$$ $$B_{ram} = 0.005 \times 8 \times 720 = \$28.80$$ $$B_{storage} = 0.0001 \times 100 \times 720 = \$7.20$$ $$B_{total} = 57.60 + 28.80 + 7.20 = \$93.60$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks such billing calculations. Memorize the pay-per-use formula — it is a guaranteed 2-mark item. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cloud Working ```mermaid flowchart TB UserNode["USER LAYER
Thin Client Browser Mobile App API Caller"] FrontEnd["FRONT END LAYER
API Gateway Load Balancer Firewall IAM Auth"] OrchLayer["ORCHESTRATION LAYER
Service Orchestrator SLA Manager Scheduler Metering"] VirtLayer["VIRTUALIZATION LAYER
Hypervisor Type 1 KVM Xen Container Engine Docker"] PhysLayer["PHYSICAL INFRA LAYER
Servers Storage Arrays Network Switches Data Centers"] UserNode -->|HTTPS REST Request| FrontEnd FrontEnd -->|Authenticated Request| OrchLayer OrchLayer -->|Allocation Directive| VirtLayer VirtLayer -->|Resource Abstraction| PhysLayer PhysLayer -->|Compute Storage Network| VirtLayer VirtLayer -->|Provisioned VM Container| OrchLayer OrchLayer -->|Service Endpoint| FrontEnd FrontEnd -->|Response with IP Credentials| UserNode ``` ### 4.2 Service Models Working Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB CloudTop["CLOUD STACK"] SaaSBox["SAAS LAYER
Software as a Service
Email CRM Office 365"] PaaSBox["PAAS LAYER
Platform as a Service
App Engine Azure App Service"] IaaSBox["IAAS LAYER
Infrastructure as a Service
EC2 Compute Engine VMs"] PhysBox["PHYSICAL LAYER
Servers Storage Network"] CloudTop --> SaaSBox CloudTop --> PaaSBox CloudTop --> IaaSBox CloudTop --> PhysBox SaaSBox -->|Runs On| PaaSBox PaaSBox -->|Runs On| IaaSBox IaaSBox -->|Runs On| PhysBox ``` ### 4.3 Cloud Working Request-Response Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR StepA["STEP A
User Sends Request"] StepB["STEP B
Authenticate User"] StepC["STEP C
Check Quota and SLA"] StepD["STEP D
Discover Resources"] StepE["STEP E
Allocate VM or Container"] StepF["STEP F
Deliver Service to User"] StepG["STEP G
Monitor and Meter Usage"] StepH["STEP H
Auto Scale or Bill"] StepA --> StepB StepB --> StepC StepC --> StepD StepD --> StepE StepE --> StepF StepF --> StepG StepG --> StepH StepH -->|if load spikes| StepE StepH -->|if user terminates| StepA ``` ### 4.4 Multi-Tenant Virtualization Working ```mermaid flowchart TB HostHW["PHYSICAL HOST SERVER
128 CPU Cores 512 GB RAM 10 TB Storage"] Hyp["HYPERVISOR LAYER
VMware ESXi KVM Xen"] TenantA["TENANT A VM
4 vCPU 16 GB RAM Ubuntu"] TenantB["TENANT B VM
8 vCPU 32 GB RAM Windows Server"] TenantC["TENANT C CONTAINER
2 vCPU 8 GB RAM App Container"] TenantD["TENANT D CONTAINER
2 vCPU 8 GB RAM DB Container"] HostHW --> Hyp Hyp --> TenantA Hyp --> TenantB Hyp --> TenantC Hyp --> TenantD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The hypervisor is the *abstraction engine* that makes multi-tenancy possible. Without it, the "shared resource pool" promise of cloud computing cannot be fulfilled. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *List and briefly explain any three essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST reference model.* **Model Answer:** The NIST definition of cloud computing specifies **five essential characteristics**. Any three are: 1. **On-demand self-service:** A consumer can automatically provision computing capabilities (server time, network storage) as needed without requiring human interaction with the service provider. 2. **Broad network access:** Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms (mobile phones, tablets, laptops, workstations). 3. **Resource pooling:** The provider's computing resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model, with physical and virtual resources dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand. 4. **Rapid elasticity:** Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward commensurate with demand.
