To the consumer, the capabilities appear unlimited. 5. **Measured service:** Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction appropriate to the type of service (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). **(3 marks — one mark per correct characteristic explained)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *Explain the role of a hypervisor in the working of cloud computing.* **Model Answer:** A **hypervisor** (also called Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM) is a critical software layer that enables **virtualization** in cloud computing. **Role in cloud working:** 1. **Hardware Abstraction:** It abstracts the underlying physical hardware (CPU, RAM, storage, network) and presents multiple isolated virtual instances to the cloud users. 2. **Multi-tenancy Enablement:** It allows multiple tenants (cloud users) to share the same physical server securely by creating isolated Virtual Machines (VMs), each with its own OS and applications. 3. **Resource Partitioning:** The hypervisor divides CPU cores, memory, and I/O bandwidth among VMs according to policies defined by the cloud orchestrator. 4. **Two Types:** - **Type-1 (Bare-metal):** Runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM). Used in production cloud data centers. - **Type-2 (Hosted):** Runs as an application on a host OS (e.g., VirtualBox, VMware Workstation). Used for development/testing. 5. **Live Migration:** Advanced hypervisors support live migration of running VMs between physical hosts without downtime — critical for cloud elasticity and maintenance. **(3 marks — full marks awarded for covering abstraction, multi-tenancy, and types)** --- ### Part B — Full-Question Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** *(a)* Explain the complete working of cloud computing with a neat block diagram covering all five functional layers. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Describe the three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with real-world examples and compare who manages what in each model. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) **Working of Cloud Computing — Five Functional Layers:** When a user requests a cloud service (e.g., launching a virtual machine), the request passes through a well-defined pipeline: **Layer 1 — User/Client Layer:** The end user accesses the cloud through a thin client, web browser, mobile app, or REST API. The request $Q = \{v_{type}, OS, R_{ram}, N_{cpu}, S_{storage}\}$ is encoded and sent over **HTTPS**. **Layer 2 — Service/Front-End Layer:** The request hits the **API Gateway**, which performs **load balancing**, **SSL termination**, and routes the request to the appropriate service. Authentication happens here via **OAuth 2.0** or **SAML** tokens. **Layer 3 — Orchestration Layer (Brain of the Cloud):** The **Service Orchestrator** identifies the optimal data center region using a cost function: $$i_{optimal} = \arg\min_{i} \left( w_1 D_{latency,i} + w_2 P_{price,i} + w_3 L_{load,i} \right)$$ The **SLA Manager** checks the request against the user's quota. The **Scheduler** identifies the best-fit physical host. **Layer 4 — Virtualization Layer:** The **hypervisor** on the chosen host creates a new VM by partitioning CPU (via EPT/NPT), memory (via shadow paging), and I/O (via virtio). The VM is given an IP, hostname, and credentials. **Layer 5 — Physical Infrastructure Layer:** The actual **servers, SAN/NAS storage, switches, and data centers** form the foundation. They are managed invisibly by the provider. **Continuous Loop:** After delivery, the **Monitoring Agent** tracks CPU, memory, and network usage. If utilization crosses a threshold (e.g., 80%), **auto-scaling** triggers a new VM. At termination, the **Billing Module** calculates the final cost using: $$B_{final} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times u_i \times t_i)$$ *Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 for visual representation.* **(7 marks — 1.5 marks per layer + 0.5 for continuous loop)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) **Three Cloud Service Models:** | Layer | IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) | PaaS (Platform as a Service) | SaaS (Software as a Service) | |---|---|---|---| | **What user gets** | Raw VMs, storage, networks | Application platform with runtime, middleware, OS | Ready-to-use software applications | | **User manages** | Applications, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS | Applications and Data only | Only the data they input | | **Provider manages** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Virtualization, Servers | Everything except user data | | **Example** | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, Azure VMs | Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox | | **Target user** | System administrators, DevOps | Application developers | End users / business users | | **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | | **Control** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | **Analogy (for KTU answer):** - **IaaS** = Renting a house (you furnish it yourself) - **PaaS** = Renting a furnished house (you only bring your clothes) - **SaaS** = Staying in a hotel (everything is provided; you just use it) **Stack Visualization (ASCII for exam):** ``` +-----------------------------+ <- SaaS (User uses software) | Application (User's data) | +-----------------------------+ | Application (Developer's) | <- PaaS (Developer deploys app) +-----------------------------+ | OS Middleware Runtime | +-----------------------------+ | Virtualization | <- IaaS (Admin manages VMs) +-----------------------------+ | Servers Storage Network | <- Physical (Provider) +-----------------------------+ ``` **(7 marks — 2 marks for table + 2 marks for management responsibilities + 2 marks for examples + 1 mark for analogy/stack)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** *(a)* Describe the four cloud deployment models with neat diagrams. Compare public, private, hybrid, and community clouds. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Explain in detail the role of **virtualization** and **service orchestration** in the working of cloud computing. How do they together enable elasticity? *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) **Four Cloud Deployment Models:** 1. **Public Cloud:** - Owned and operated by **third-party providers** (AWS, Azure, GCP). - Open to the **general public** or large industry groups. - **Advantages:** Low cost, no maintenance, near-infinite scalability. - **Disadvantages:** Less control, data sovereignty concerns, shared resources. - **Example:** AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure. 2. **Private Cloud:** - Operated **exclusively for a single organization**. - Can be **on-premises** (hosted internally) or **hosted by a third party**. - **Advantages:** Full control, better security, compliance-friendly. - **Disadvantages:** High capital expenditure, limited scalability. - **Example:** KTU internal research cloud, bank internal clouds. 3. **Hybrid Cloud:** - Combines **public and private clouds** bound by **standardized technology** (e.g., VPN, API gateways). - Enables **data and application portability**. - **Use case:** Sensitive data stays in private cloud; burst workloads go to public cloud. - **Example:** AWS Outposts, Azure Arc. 4. **Community Cloud:** - Shared by **several organizations** with **common concerns** (security, compliance, jurisdiction). - Managed **internally or by a third party**. - **Example:** Government cloud (GovCloud), medical research consortium cloud. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community | |---|---|---|---|---| | Ownership | Provider | Single org | Both | Multiple orgs | | Access | Public | Restricted | Mixed | Group-restricted | | Cost | Low (OPEX) | High (CAPEX) | Mixed | Shared | | Security | Moderate | High | High | High | | Scalability | Very High | Limited | Very High | Moderate | | Use case | Startups, web apps | Banks, defense | Enterprises | Government, healthcare | **(7 marks — 1.5 per model + 0.5 for comparison table)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) **Role of Virtualization in Cloud Working:** Virtualization is the **foundational technology** that makes cloud computing possible. It uses a **hypervisor** to create multiple **Virtual Machines (VMs)** on a single physical host, each running its own OS and applications. - **Resource Pooling:** Physical resources (CPU, RAM, storage) are abstracted into a logical pool and dynamically assigned to VMs. - **Isolation:** Each VM is isolated — a crash in one tenant's VM does not affect others. - **Live Migration:** VMs can be moved between physical hosts without downtime, enabling maintenance without service interruption. - **Enables Multi-tenancy:** Multiple customers share the same hardware securely. **Role of Service Orchestration in Cloud Working:** Orchestration is the **intelligence layer** that automates the provisioning, scaling, and management of cloud resources. Key components include: - **Workflow Engine:** Defines the sequence of actions (allocate VM → configure network → attach storage → start service). - **Resource Scheduler:** Decides *where* (which host) and *when* (which time) to deploy a VM. - **SLA Manager:** Continuously monitors and ensures Quality of Service. - **Metering & Billing:** Tracks usage for pay-per-use. - **Auto-Scaler:** Reacts to load changes by adding/removing VMs. **How They Together Enable Elasticity:** Elasticity means the cloud can **scale out** (add resources) and **scale in** (remove resources) automatically based on demand. The combined flow is: 1. Monitoring detects load increase (e.g., CPU > 80%). 2. Orchestrator's **Auto-Scaler** decides to add 2 more VMs. 3. **Virtualization layer** is instructed to provision these VMs on available physical hosts. 4. **Load Balancer** redistributes traffic across the expanded pool. 5. When load drops, the same process scales back, releasing VMs. The mathematical elasticity ratio: $$E_r = \dfrac{R_{peak}}{R_{baseline}}$$ For example, if baseline capacity is 10 VMs and peak capacity scales to 50 VMs: $$E_r = \dfrac{50}{10} = 5$$ This means the system can scale to **5x its baseline** — a hallmark of elastic cloud working. **(7 marks — 2 for virtualization role + 2 for orchestration role + 2 for combined elasticity flow + 1 for mathematical example)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the orchestration layer.** Many students describe only "user → VM → data center" and skip the orchestration/SLA/billing modules. This loses 2-3 marks in 14-mark questions. > 2. **Confusing deployment models with service models.** IaaS/PaaS/SaaS are *service* models; public/private/hybrid are *deployment* models. Mixing them up is a **direct 2-mark penalty**. > 3. **Not using block diagrams.** KTU explicitly awards 1-2 marks for neat labeled diagrams in long answers. Always draw one, even if rough. > 4. **Skipping the pay-per-use formula.** This is the only quantitative part of Module 1 and examiners love testing it. > 5. **Writing only textbook definitions.** KTU 2024 Scheme rewards **application and comparison** — always include at least one real-world example (AWS, Azure, GCP). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] Cloud computing is the **on-demand delivery of IT resources over the internet** with pay-per-use pricing. - [x] The **NIST definition** lists **5 essential characteristics**: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service. - [x] The working of cloud involves **5 layers**: User, Front-end, Orchestration, Virtualization, Physical Infrastructure. - [x] The **orchestration layer** is the brain — it schedules, monitors, scales, and bills. - [x] The **virtualization layer** (hypervisor) enables multi-tenancy and resource pooling. - [x] **Three service models**: IaaS (EC2), PaaS (App Engine), SaaS (Gmail). Each shifts management responsibility differently. - [x] **Four deployment models**: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community. Each suits different security/cost needs. - [x] **Pay-per-use formula**: $B = \sum (r_i \times u_i \times t_i)$ — guaranteed 2-mark question. - [x] **Availability SLAs**: 99.9% = 525.6 min/year downtime; 99.99% = 52.56 min/year. - [x] **Elasticity ratio**: $E_r = R_{peak} / R_{baseline}$ — measures scaling capability. - [x] Always draw a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers showing user → gateway → orchestrator → hypervisor → hardware. - [x] Memorize **Type-1 vs Type-2 hypervisors** — KTU asks this frequently. - [x] Real-world examples (AWS, Azure, GCP) earn extra marks in the application section. - [x] The 6-step working lifecycle: **Request → Auth → Discovery → Virtualize → Deliver → Monitor/Bill**.

topics.title: Cloud Computing Architecture and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Architecture and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (NIST Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with **minimal management effort** or service provider interaction.

This definition, standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**, is the universally accepted KTU board definition.

## 1.2 The Five Essential Characteristics (NIST Model)

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **On-demand self-service** | Resource provisioning without human intervention |
| 2 | **Broad network access** | Access via standard protocols (HTTP, REST) over heterogeneous thin/thick clients |
| 3 | **Resource pooling** | Multi-tenant pooling using virtualization |
| 4 | **Rapid elasticity** | Dynamic scaling (in/out) based on workload |
| 5 | **Measured service** | Pay-per-use metering (CPU-hr, GB-month, I/O ops) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity supplied by the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board)** to your home.
> 
> - You **don't build a power plant** at home — you just plug into the grid (No capital expenditure on infrastructure).
> - You **pay only for the units (kWh)** you consume each month (Measured service / Pay-per-use).
> - If you buy a new AC, you don't ask the board to "install more infrastructure" — the grid **scales elastically** to handle it.
> - The grid, the wires, the transformers are all **shared across thousands of homes** (Resource pooling).
> 
> Cloud computing applies this exact "utility computing" model to **servers, storage, databases, and software**.

## 1.4 Why Cloud Architecture Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 1 is the **foundation block** for the entire course. Every later module (virtualization, security, deployment) builds upon the architectural components defined here. Examiner questions frequently test your ability to **map a real-world problem to the correct service/deployment model**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Cloud Architecture

Cloud architecture is broadly divided into three operational layers, each with distinct responsibilities.

### Layer 1 — Front-End (Client Side)
- The **interface** the user/device interacts with.
- Components: Web browser, mobile app, thin client, fat client, terminal emulator.
- **Protocols:** HTTP/HTTPS, REST APIs, WebSockets.

### Layer 2 — Back-End (Cloud Provider Side)
- The **"engine room"** of the cloud.
- Components: Servers, Storage arrays, Virtualization hypervisor, Database management systems, Application logic, Traffic controllers, Load balancers.
- **Protocols:** SOAP, REST, gRPC, RPC.

### Layer 3 — Network (The Glue)
- The **connectivity layer** binding front-end to back-end.
- Components: Internet, Intranet, Intercloud, Routers, Switches, Firewalls, CDN edge nodes.

## 2.2 The Three Service Models (The "SPI" Stack)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examinations **frequently** test the SPI model. Memorize the responsibility split.

| Model | Full Form | Provider Manages | Customer Manages | Example |
|-------|-----------|------------------|------------------|---------|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Hardware, Storage, Network, Hypervisor | OS, Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Hardware, OS, Middleware, Runtime | App, Data | Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Everything (full stack) | Only user data & access | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Dropbox |

## 2.3 The Four Deployment Models

| Model | Ownership | Audience | Security Posture |
|-------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Open to general public | Lowest control, shared tenancy |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Exclusive to one org | Highest control, dedicated |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combination of Public + Private | Org-controlled orchestration | Balanced (data stays private; burst to public) |
| **Community Cloud** | Several orgs with shared concerns | Specific community (govt, banks) | Shared by trusted entities |

## 2.4 Core Cloud Enabling Technologies

1. **Virtualization** — Abstracting physical hardware into logical VMs (Hypervisor Type-1: VMware ESXi, Xen; Type-2: VirtualBox).
2. **Web Services & Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** — SOAP, WSDL, UDDI for interoperable service calls.
3. **Multi-tenancy** — Single software instance serves multiple customers (tenants) with isolation.
4. **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** — Contractual guarantee (e.g., **99.9%** uptime = 8.77 hours downtime/year).
5. **APIs and Web 2.0** — RESTful interfaces enabling mashups and integration.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **most frequently tested equations** in Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize the symbols, units, and conversion factors.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times T_{period}$ | Downtime per period from availability $A$ | hours/year |
| 2 | $A = 1 - \frac{T_{down}}{T_{period}}$ | Availability ratio | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 3 | $C_{TCO} = C_{capex} + C_{opex}$ | Total Cost of Ownership (on-premise) | ₹ / \$ |
| 4 | $C_{cloud} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)$ | Cloud cost = sum of (price × usage) per resource $i$ | ₹ / \$ |
| 5 | $R_{scale} = \frac{\Delta \text{Capacity}}{\Delta \text{Load}}$ | Scalability ratio | vCPU/req, GB/req |
| 6 | $SLA_{annual} = 1 - (1 - A)^{365 \times 24}$ | Compound (sequential) availability | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_{vCPU} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} n_{j} \times c_{j}$ | Total virtual CPUs in pool (consolidation) | vCPUs |
| 8 | $U_{util} = \frac{\text{Active time}}{\text{Total time}} \times 100\%$ | Server utilization | % |
| 9 | $B_{req} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} (S_{k} \times f_{k})$ | Bandwidth requirement (sum over apps) | Mbps |
| 10 | $T_{provision} = T_{request} + T_{queue} + T_{deploy}$ | Total provisioning latency | seconds |

### Boundary Conditions and Conversion Facts (Recall in 2 seconds)

- **1 year = 365 days = 8,760 hours = 525,600 minutes**
- **1 GB-month** is the standard storage billing unit (e.g., ₹0.10/GB-month).
- **SLA tiers:** **99%** = 3.65 days/yr, **99.9%** = 8.77 hr/yr, **99.99%** = 52.6 min/yr, **99.999%** ("five nines") = 5.26 min/yr.
- **vCPU : Core ratio** = typically **1 vCPU = 1 physical thread** (2 vCPU = 1 physical core in most hypervisors).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud architecture principles drive **DevOps pipelines**, **microservices deployment**, **disaster recovery** (geographic redundancy across regions), and **big-data analytics** (Hadoop/Spark on EMR). For KTU students specifically, these concepts underpin final-year projects using AWS Free Tier or Azure for Students credits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from SLA Availability

**Problem Context (typical KTU 3-mark):** A cloud provider advertises **99.95%** availability. Calculate the maximum permissible downtime per year.

**Step 1 — Identify the time period.**

$$T_{period} = 1 \text{ year} = 365 \text{ days} = 365 \times 24 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to hours.**

$$T_{period} = 8{,}760 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the downtime formula.**

$$T_{down} = (1 - A) \times T_{period}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A = 0.9995$.**

$$T_{down} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 8{,}760$$

**Step 5 — Compute the unavailability fraction.**

$$1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

**Step 6 — Multiply.**

$$T_{down} = 0.0005 \times 8{,}760 = 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to minutes for clarity.**

$$T_{down} = 4.38 \times 60 = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}$$

> **Final Answer:** Maximum downtime = **4.38 hours/year** (≈ **262.8 minutes/year**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Cloud Cost (TCO Comparison)

**Problem Context:** A startup needs **2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM, 100 GB storage, 1 TB outbound data transfer per month** for 12 months. Compare cloud cost vs on-premise TCO.

**Given pricing (hypothetical KTU problem):**
- Cloud: vCPU = ₹500/mo, RAM = ₹100/GB-mo, Storage = ₹2/GB-mo, Data = ₹5/GB.
- On-premise: Server = ₹1,50,000 (amortized over 3 years), Power = ₹3,000/mo, IT admin = ₹20,000/mo.

**Step 1 — Cloud monthly cost per resource.**

$$C_{vCPU} = 2 \times 500 = ₹1{,}000$$

$$C_{RAM} = 8 \times 100 = ₹800$$

$$C_{storage} = 100 \times 2 = ₹200$$

$$C_{data} = 1{,}000 \times 5 = ₹5{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Sum for monthly cloud cost.**

$$C_{cloud,mo} = 1{,}000 + 800 + 200 + 5{,}000 = ₹7{,}000/\text{month}$$

**Step 3 — Annualize.**

$$C_{cloud,yr} = 7{,}000 \times 12 = ₹84{,}000$$

**Step 4 — On-premise annual cost.**

Server amortization (straight-line over 36 months):

$$C_{server} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{36} \times 12 = ₹50{,}000/\text{year}$$

Power:

$$C_{power} = 3{,}000 \times 12 = ₹36{,}000$$

Admin:

$$C_{admin} = 20{,}000 \times 12 = ₹2{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Sum on-premise TCO.**

$$C_{TCO} = 50{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 = ₹3{,}26{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Compare.**

$$\Delta C = 3{,}26{,}000 - 84{,}000 = ₹2{,}42{,}000 \text{ saved by cloud}$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{2{,}42{,}000}{3{,}26{,}000} \times 100\% = 74.23\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Cloud is **74.23% cheaper annually** for this workload profile.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scalability Ratio (Elastic Provisioning)

**Problem Context:** A web app receives traffic that grows from **100 RPS to 500 RPS**. Each server instance handles **50 RPS**. How many instances must be provisioned, and what is the achieved utilization?

**Step 1 — Instances required at peak load.**

$$N_{peak} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak}}{C_{instance}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{500}{50} \right\rceil = 10 \text{ instances}$$

**Step 2 — Instances at base load.**

$$N_{base} = \left\lceil \frac{100}{50} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ instances}$$

**Step 3 — Scalability ratio.**

$$R_{scale} = \frac{\Delta \text{Capacity}}{\Delta \text{Load}} = \frac{10 - 2}{500 - 100} = \frac{8}{400} = 0.02 \text{ instances per RPS}$$

**Step 4 — Utilization at peak.**

$$U_{util,peak} = \frac{500}{10 \times 50} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Average utilization (assuming 30% time at peak, 70% at base).**

$$U_{avg} = 0.3 \times 100\% + 0.7 \times \frac{100}{10 \times 50} \times 100\% = 30\% + 14\% = 44\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Provisioning jumps from 2 to 10 instances; the auto-scaler must add 8 instances. Average utilization is 44%.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service Model Selection

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Service Model | Justification |
|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Need custom OS kernel, root access | **IaaS** | Full control over guest OS |
| Develop and deploy web app quickly without infra setup | **PaaS** | Platform abstracts infrastructure |
| End-user productivity (email, docs) | **SaaS** | Pre-built application consumed directly |
| Big data processing with custom Hadoop tuning | **IaaS** | Low-level cluster control needed |
| Microservices with auto-scaling | **PaaS** | Built-in orchestration (Kubernetes, App Engine) |
| Sensitive financial data, full audit | **Private IaaS** | Maximum governance and isolation |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Deployment Model Selection

| Compliance / Workload Constraint | Recommended Deployment |
|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| Public-facing e-commerce site, bursty traffic | **Public** (e.g., AWS) |
| Banking core system, RBI audit mandates | **Private** (on-premise) |
| Seasonal tax filing app (peak in March) | **Hybrid** (private baseline + public burst) |
| Smart City project across 5 municipal bodies | **Community** (shared infrastructure) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (NIST-aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["User Device - Laptop or Mobile"] --> B["Internet or Network Gateway"]
    B --> C["Cloud Service Provider - Front End"]
    C --> D["API Gateway and Identity Management"]
    D --> E["Application Layer - SaaS Apps"]
    D --> F["Platform Layer - PaaS Runtime"]
    D --> G["Infrastructure Layer - IaaS VMs"]
    G --> H["Hypervisor - Virtualization Engine"]
    H --> I["Physical Hardware Pool - Servers, Storage, Network"]
    F --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J["Resource Pool - Multi Tenant"]
```

## 4.2 Service Model Responsibility Stack (SPI Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Physical Hardware - Servers, Storage, Network"]
    L1["Virtualization and Hypervisor"]
    L2["Operating System and System Software"]
    L3["Middleware, Runtime, Development Tools"]
    L4["Application Software and Data"]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    P1["IaaS Manages L0 to L1"] -.-> L1
    P2["PaaS Manages L0 to L3"] -.-> L3
    P3["SaaS Manages L0 to L4"] -.-> L4
```

## 4.3 Deployment Model Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["Cloud Deployment Models"]
    Root --> Pub["Public Cloud - AWS, Azure, GCP - Open to Public"]
    Root --> Priv["Private Cloud - Single Organization - High Control"]
    Root --> Hyb["Hybrid Cloud - Private plus Public with Orchestration"]
    Root --> Com["Community Cloud - Shared by Specific Community"]
    Hyb --> H1["Sensitive Data on Private"]
    Hyb --> H2["Burst Workload on Public"]
```

## 4.4 Cloud Provisioning Workflow (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User or Client App
    participant API as API Gateway
    participant IAM as Identity and Access Management
    participant ORCH as Orchestrator
    participant HV as Hypervisor Layer
    participant RES as Resource Pool

    U->>API: Request VM - 2 vCPU 8 GB
    API->>IAM: Authenticate and Authorize
    IAM-->>API: Token Granted
    API->>ORCH: Submit Provisioning Request
    ORCH->>HV: Select Host - Find Least Loaded
    HV->>RES: Allocate Compute and Storage
    RES-->>HV: Resources Reserved
    HV-->>ORCH: VM Created
    ORCH-->>API: Return VM Endpoint and Credentials
    API-->>U: 200 OK with VM Details
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*List and briefly explain the **five essential characteristics** of cloud computing as defined by NIST.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **On-demand self-service** — A consumer can automatically provision computing resources (server time, storage) without human interaction with the service provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Resources are accessible over the network through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, PDA).
3. **Resource pooling** — The provider's computing resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model, with physical and virtual resources dynamically reassigned.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward with demand.
5. **Measured service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list only 3 or 4 characteristics or mix up the "characteristics" with the "service models" (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). This is an **instant 1-mark loss**. Memorize all five and write them in the order above.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*Differentiate between **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** with one real-world example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|--------|------|------|------|
| **Provider manages** | Hardware, hypervisor, storage, network | + OS, middleware, runtime | + application, data |
| **Customer manages** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | Application and data | Only their data & access |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce |
| **Use case** | Custom OS configuration, lift-and-shift migration | Rapid web app development, API deployment | End-user productivity, CRM |

**One-line takeaway:** "The deeper you go in the SPI stack, the more the provider manages and the less operational burden on the customer."

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **cloud computing reference architecture** with a neat block diagram, clearly identifying the front-end, back-end, and network components. Discuss the role of APIs and the hypervisor in the back-end. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SaaS provider advertises a **Service Level Agreement (SLA) of 99.9% availability** for its service. Calculate:
   1. The **maximum allowable downtime per year** (in hours and minutes).
   2. The **maximum downtime per month** assuming uniform distribution.
   3. If the provider upgrades to **99.99%**, by what factor does the permissible downtime improve? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A cloud computing reference architecture is a layered framework describing the interaction between the user-facing client components and the provider-managed data center resources, connected through a network.

**Front-End (1.5 marks):**
- **Client devices:** Web browser, mobile app, terminal.
- **Client-side technologies:** HTML5, JavaScript, REST clients, thin-client protocols (RDP, ICA).
- **Function:** Issues service requests, consumes cloud-hosted resources.

**Back-End (3 marks):**
- **Servers:** Large pools of commodity x86/ARM machines (often in container form factors).
- **Storage:** SAN/NAS/object storage (S3-compatible).
- **Hypervisor:** Type-1 (bare-metal, e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen) creates and manages VMs. It enables **hardware abstraction, isolation, and live migration**.
- **Application logic:** SaaS apps, PaaS runtimes, microservices.
- **Traffic controller / Load balancer:** Distributes incoming requests across the server pool.
- **Cloud management software:** Orchestrator (OpenStack, CloudStack), monitoring, billing.

**Network / Middleware (1.5 marks):**
- Internet/intranet, routers, switches, firewalls.
- **APIs (REST/SOAP):** Standardized contract allowing front-end to invoke back-end services. They enable **interoperability, automation, and multi-tenancy**.
- CDN edge nodes for content delivery.

**Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.1 of these notes — reproduce neatly in exam).**

---

#### Part (b) — SLA Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Annual downtime for 99.9% SLA (2 marks).**

$$T_{down,yr} = (1 - 0.999) \times 8{,}760 = 0.001 \times 8{,}760 = 8.76 \text{ hours/year}$$

Convert to minutes:

$$T_{down,yr} = 8.76 \times 60 = 525.6 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 2 — Monthly downtime (uniform distribution) (2 marks).**

$$T_{down,mo} = \frac{8.76}{12} = 0.73 \text{ hours/month} = 43.8 \text{ minutes/month}$$

**Step 3 — Improvement factor from 99.9% to 99.99% (3 marks).**

Downtime at 99.9%:

$$T_{1} = 0.001 \times 8{,}760 = 8.76 \text{ hours/year}$$

Downtime at 99.99%:

$$T_{2} = 0.0001 \times 8{,}760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year}$$

Improvement factor:

$$F = \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} = \frac{8.76}{0.876} = 10$$

> **Final Answer:** Downtime = **8.76 hr/yr (525.6 min/yr)**; monthly = **43.8 min/month**; improvement = **10×** reduction in downtime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **four deployment models** of cloud computing — Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community — using a comparison table. Identify **two real-world scenarios** (one each) where (i) a hybrid cloud is the most suitable choice, and (ii) a private cloud is mandated. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup is evaluating two options for hosting a web application:
   - **Option X (IaaS):** 4 vCPUs × ₹600/month, 16 GB RAM × ₹120/GB-month, 500 GB storage × ₹3/GB-month, 2 TB outbound data × ₹6/GB. Bandwidth in is free.
   - **Option Y (PaaS):** A flat **₹12,000/month** subscription covering up to 8 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM, 1 TB storage, and 5 TB outbound.
   
   The startup expects to grow into Option Y's tier within **18 months**. Compute:
   1. The **monthly cost of Option X** and the **18-month cumulative cost**.
   2. The **18-month cumulative cost of Option Y**.
   3. The **break-even month** at which the cumulative cost of Option Y equals that of Option X. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Deployment Models (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
|--------|--------|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Owner** | Third-party provider | Single organization | Mix of both | Several orgs with shared concerns |
| **Tenants** | Open to all | Single org | Org-controlled | Trusted community |
| **Cost model** | Pay-per-use, low CapEx | High CapEx, predictable OpEx | Mixed | Shared CapEx |
| **Control** | Lowest | Highest | Tunable | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by own infrastructure | High (burst to public) | Moderate |
| **Security** | Shared responsibility | Maximum | Customizable | Shared trust model |
| **Example** | AWS, Azure, GCP | On-premise OpenStack, VMware vSphere | Banking core + cloud analytics | GovCloud, G-Cloud (India) |

**Real-world scenarios (3 marks):**

(i) **Hybrid Cloud Example — E-commerce with seasonal spikes:**
A Kerala-based online retailer runs its **inventory, payment, and order database on a private cloud** (for PCI-DSS compliance and data sovereignty). During the Onam/Vishu festive season, it **bursts into a public cloud** to handle 10× traffic using auto-scaling. This combines security with elastic capacity.

(ii) **Private Cloud Example — Banking / Defence:**
A scheduled commercial bank's core banking system is mandated by **RBI** to reside on-premise in a private cloud to satisfy data localization, audit, and regulatory requirements. Public exposure is restricted to front-end web servers.

---

#### Part (b) — Cost Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Option X monthly cost (2 marks).**

$$C_{X,\text{vCPU}} = 4 \times 600 = ₹2{,}400$$

$$C_{X,\text{RAM}} = 16 \times 120 = ₹1{,}920$$

$$C_{X,\text{storage}} = 500 \times 3 = ₹1{,}500$$

$$C_{X,\text{data}} = 2{,}000 \times 6 = ₹12{,}000$$

$$C_{X,\text{monthly}} = 2{,}400 + 1{,}920 + 1{,}500 + 12{,}000 = ₹17{,}820/\text{month}$$

**Step 2 — 18-month cumulative for Option X (1 mark).**

$$C_{X,18} = 17{,}820 \times 18 = ₹3{,}20{,}760$$

**Step 3 — 18-month cumulative for Option Y (1 mark).**

$$C_{Y,18} = 12{,}000 \times 18 = ₹2{,}16{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Break-even month calculation (3 marks).**

Set cumulative cost of Option Y equal to Option X:

$$12{,}000 \times n = 17{,}820 \times n - C_{0}$$

where $C_{0}$ is the upfront advantage (none here, both start at $n = 0$). Re-arranging differently — let's directly solve:

$$12{,}000 \cdot n = 17{,}820 \cdot n \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; n(12{,}000 - 17{,}820) = 0$$

This yields $n = 0$ trivially because Option Y is **always cheaper** under the given consumption. So strictly, there is **no break-even** in the standard sense — Option Y is the economically dominant choice for this workload.

**However**, the break-even interpretation often asked is: *at what month does the cumulative savings become substantial?* Cumulative savings at month $n$:

$$S_{n} = 17{,}820 \cdot n - 12{,}000 \cdot n = 5{,}820 \cdot n$$

At $n = 12$ months: $S_{12} = ₹69{,}840$.

> **Final Answer:** Option X = **₹17,820/month** → **₹3,20,760 over 18 months**; Option Y = **₹2,16,000 over 18 months**; **Option Y is always cheaper** (saves **₹5,820/month**). No break-even — Option Y dominates from day 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is treating "break-even" as a literal crossover. In a flat-subscription vs pay-per-use model, if the subscription is always cheaper at the actual usage, the correct answer is "**Option Y dominates — no crossover**". State this clearly; partial credit is awarded for recognizing the structure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- [ ] **NIST definition** of cloud computing must be quoted verbatim in 2-mark definition questions.
- [ ] **Five essential characteristics**: on-demand, broad access, pooling, elasticity, measured.
- [ ] **Three service models (SPI):** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — know who manages what layer.
- [ ] **Four deployment models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [ ] **Three-layer architecture:** Front-end ↔ Network ↔ Back-end (with hypervisor, orchestrator, storage, APIs).
- [ ] **Availability → Downtime conversion:** Use $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8{,}760$ hours/year.
- [ ] **SLA tiers:** 99% = 3.65 d/yr; 99.9% = 8.77 h/yr; 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr; 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr.
- [ ] **TCO formula:** $C_{TCO} = C_{capex} + C_{opex}$ vs $C_{cloud} = \sum P_i \times U_i$.
- [ ] **Scalability ratio:** $R_{scale} = \Delta\text{Capacity} / \Delta\text{Load}$.
- [ ] **Hypervisor types:** Type-1 (bare-metal, ESXi/Xen/KVM) vs Type-2 (hosted, VirtualBox).
- [ ] **Multi-tenancy** + **virtualization** are the dual pillars enabling cloud pooling.
- [ ] **APIs (REST/SOAP)** are the contract surface between front-end and back-end.
- [ ] **KTU hot topics** likely in 2025 exams: SLA numerical, TCO comparison, deployment model selection with justification, hybrid cloud burst scenarios.
- [ ] **Don't confuse** "characteristics" (5 NIST pillars) with "service models" (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) or "deployment models" (Public/Private/Hybrid/Community) — they are **three distinct categorization axes**.
- [ ] Always **label diagrams** with full terms (e.g., "Hypervisor" not just "layer").
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Cloud Architecture** is the systematic blueprint that defines the structural and logical composition of cloud computing resources, encompassing the integration of front-end platforms (client-side interfaces), back-end platforms (servers, storage, networks), a cloud-based delivery model, and a network (typically the Internet) that connects them. It defines how components are organized, interconnected, and orchestrated to deliver on-demand, scalable, elastic, and metered services.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum**, Cloud Architecture is studied as the **foundational design framework** that unifies:

- **Virtualization layer** (abstraction of physical resources)
- **Service layer** (delivery models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS, DaaS)
- **Application layer** (workloads, microservices, containers)
- **Management & orchestration layer** (provisioning, monitoring, SLA enforcement)

> [!NOTE] > **Key Architectural Goals (per KTU CO1 – Module 1):** > 1. **On-demand self-service** > 2. **Broad network access** > 3. **Resource pooling & multi-tenancy** > 4. **Rapid elasticity & scalability** > 5. **Measured/metered service**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Smart Apartment Building"

Imagine Cloud Architecture as the blueprint of a **high-rise smart apartment building**:

| Building Element | Cloud Equivalent |
|------------------|------------------|
| Building skeleton (steel, concrete) | **Physical Infrastructure** (servers, storage, network) |
| Electrical wiring & plumbing | **Virtualization & Middleware** |
| Modular apartments (1BHK, 2BHK, Penthouse) | **Virtual Machines & Containers** |
| Building management office | **Orchestration & Resource Manager** |
| Concierge (delivers services on demand) | **Cloud Service Broker / API Gateway** |
| Tenants (different families sharing building) | **Multi-tenants (cloud users)** |
| Utility meters (electricity, water) | **Metering & Pay-per-use billing** |

Just as the architect decides the number of floors, plumbing layout, and lift systems, the **Cloud Architect** decides how compute, storage, and networking are abstracted, pooled, and exposed to users.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Cloud Architecture (Highlighted Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Service Level Agreement (SLA) availability tiers (industry-standard):**
> - **99.9% ("three nines")** = 8.77 hours downtime/year
> - **99.99% ("four nines")** = 52.6 minutes downtime/year
> - **99.999% ("five nines")** = 5.26 minutes downtime/year
>
> **The "Five Essential Characteristics" of cloud (NIST SP 800-145):**
> 1. On-demand self-service
> 2. Broad network access
> 3. Resource pooling
> 4. Rapid elasticity
> 5. Measured service

---

### 1.4 Why Cloud Architecture Matters in Engineering

A well-designed Cloud Architecture ensures:

- **Cost efficiency** through resource consolidation
- **Fault tolerance** through redundancy and replication
- **Scalability** through horizontal and vertical scaling
- **Security** through isolation, encryption, and identity management
- **Portability** through standardized APIs and containerization

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Layer Cloud Architecture Stack (Decomposed)

Cloud Architecture is typically organized into **five logical layers**, each performing a distinct function:

#### **Layer 1 – Physical Layer (Hardware Infrastructure)**
- Comprises **data center servers**, **storage arrays (SAN/NAS)**, **network switches/routers**, **cooling systems**, and **power backup (UPS/generators)**.
- These are geographically distributed across **availability zones** and **regions** for disaster recovery.

#### **Layer 2 – Virtualization Layer**
- Uses **hypervisors (Type 1: VMware ESXi, Xen; Type 2: VirtualBox)** to partition one physical server into multiple Virtual Machines (VMs).
- Provides **abstraction**, enabling multiple OS instances to run on shared hardware.
- **Key metrics:** vCPU-to-pCPU ratio, memory overcommit ratio, VM density.

#### **Layer 3 – Resource Pool Layer**
- Aggregates virtualized resources into **pools** of compute, storage, and network.
- Enables **multi-tenancy** – many users share the same physical infrastructure securely via isolation (VLANs, namespaces, security groups).

#### **Layer 4 – Platform / Service Layer**
- Exposes pooled resources as **programmable APIs** and **managed services**.
- Includes **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS** delivery models.
- Provides **auto-scaling, load balancing, container orchestration (Kubernetes), and serverless runtimes**.

#### **Layer 5 – Application / Workload Layer**
- Hosts **end-user applications**, **microservices**, **AI/ML models**, and **data analytics workloads**.
- Communicates with lower layers via **REST/gRPC APIs**, **SDKs**, and **message brokers**.

---

### 2.2 Components of Cloud Architecture – Structured Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "FABRIC" Components Model (memorization aid):**
>
> **F** – Front-end (Client/UI devices)
> **A** – Application Services
> **B** – Back-end (Servers, Storage, Network)
> **R** – Resource Pooling & Virtualization
> **I** – Integration (APIs, Middleware, Orchestration)
> **C** – Cloud Service Management (Monitoring, Billing, Security)

Detailed breakdown:

1. **Client Infrastructure (Front-end):** Web browsers, mobile apps, thin clients, IoT devices.
2. **Application Services:** SaaS apps, microservices, containerized workloads.
3. **Back-end Platforms:** Servers (x86, ARM, GPU), object storage (S3-like), block storage (EBS-like), relational/NoSQL databases.
4. **Network Fabric:** VPCs, subnets, software-defined networking (SDN), CDNs, load balancers, firewalls.
5. **Cloud Service Management:**
   - **Provisioning** (auto-deployment via Terraform, CloudFormation)
   - **Monitoring** (Prometheus, Grafana, CloudWatch)
   - **Security** (IAM, KMS, WAF, DDoS protection)
   - **Billing/Metering** (pay-per-use, subscription, reserved)
6. **Orchestration Engine:** Coordinates scaling, failover, and workload migration.

---

### 2.3 Cloud Computing Reference Models

#### **A. NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model (SP 800-145)**

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** model defines three service models and four deployment models:

**Service Models:**
- **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** – provides raw compute, storage, network. User manages OS, runtime, apps. *(e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine)*
- **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** – provides platform with OS, middleware, runtime. User manages apps and data. *(e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku)*
- **SaaS (Software as a Service)** – provides fully functional apps over the network. *(e.g., Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Zoom)*

**Deployment Models:**
- **Public Cloud** – owned by third-party provider, open to public
- **Private Cloud** – exclusive to one organization
- **Hybrid Cloud** – combination of public + private with orchestration
- **Community Cloud** – shared by organizations with common concerns

#### **B. IBM Cloud Reference Architecture (Layered View)**
IBM defines architecture in **five distinct layers** plus **cross-layer concerns**:
- User/Client layer
- Application layer
- Platform layer
- Infrastructure layer
- Facility layer (datacenter, power, cooling)
- Cross-layer: Security, Management, Integration

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **directly testable** in KTU university exams and Module tests.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|------|-------|
| 1 | Annual downtime from availability | $\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes | $A$ = availability as decimal |
| 2 | Monthly downtime (from SLA) | $\text{Downtime}_{\text{month}} = (1 - A) \times 30 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes | Use for monthly SLAs |
| 3 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Higher = more reliable |
| 4 | Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Lower = better |
| 5 | System Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | ratio (0–1) | Direct availability measure |
| 6 | Composite Availability (series) | $A_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | ratio | For components in **series** |
| 7 | Composite Availability (parallel) | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | ratio | For **redundant** components |
| 8 | VM Density | $\text{VM Density} = \frac{\text{Number of VMs}}{\text{Number of Physical Hosts}}$ | ratio | Typical: 10–40:1 |
| 9 | CPU Utilization | $U = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Multiplexing efficiency |
| 10 | Cost-per-use (Pay-as-you-go) | $C_{\text{use}} = P_{\text{hour}} \times t_{\text{use}} + S_{\text{storage}} + T_{\text{egress}}$ | currency | $P$ = price, $S$ = storage, $T$ = transfer |
| 11 | Scalability factor | $S_f = \frac{\text{Throughput}_{\text{scaled}}}{\text{Throughput}_{\text{baseline}}}$ | ratio | $S_f > 1$ ⇒ horizontal scaling effective |
| 12 | Elasticity speed | $E_s = \frac{\Delta \text{Resources}}{\Delta t_{\text{provisioning}}}$ | units/min | Faster = better elasticity |
| 13 | Data center PUE | $\text{PUE} = \frac{\text{Total Facility Energy}}{\text{IT Equipment Energy}}$ | ratio $\geq 1$ | 1.0 is ideal (100% efficient) |
| 14 | Carbon Usage Effectiveness | $\text{CUE} = \frac{\text{Total CO}_2 \text{ Emissions}}{\text{IT Equipment Energy}}$ | kgCO₂/kWh | Green computing metric |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary condition for Availability:**
> $\lim_{A \to 1} \text{Downtime} = 0$ (perfect availability = zero downtime)
> $\lim_{\text{MTTR} \to 0} A = 1$ (instant repair gives perfect availability)

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP)** design architectures to maintain 99.99% SLA across **≥ 30+ regions** and **100+ availability zones** worldwide.
- **Edge computing** architectures push cloud capabilities to the network edge (CDNs, 5G MEC) for sub-**10 ms** latency.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** architectures use **N+1, 2N, 2N+1** redundancy models.
- **Green cloud** designs target **PUE < 1.2** using free-cooling and renewable energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from SLA Availability

**Given:** A cloud provider advertises **99.95%** SLA availability.
**Find:** Maximum permitted downtime per year.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step derivation (each row in a separate math block):**

$$\text{Availability} \ A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$

$$\text{Unavailability} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$

$$\text{Minutes in a year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \ \text{minutes}$$

$$\text{Annual Downtime} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \ \text{minutes}$$

Converting to hours:

$$\text{Annual Downtime in hours} = \frac{262.8}{60} \approx 4.38 \ \text{hours/year}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 99.95% SLA permits ~4.38 hours of downtime per year (per service).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: System Availability (Series vs Parallel)

Consider a 3-tier web application:

- **Web server tier** (availability $A_1 = 0.99$)
- **Application tier** (availability $A_2 = 0.995$)
- **Database tier** (availability $A_3 = 0.999$)

#### **Case 1: Single instances in series (no redundancy)**

$$A_{\text{series}} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.999$$

$$A_{\text{series}} = 0.98404 \approx 98.40\%$$

This corresponds to:

$$\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.98404) \times 525{,}600 \approx 83{,}978 \ \text{min} \approx 58.3 \ \text{days/year}$$

> **Result:** Series architecture is **unacceptable** for production.

#### **Case 2: Database tier with 2 redundant instances in parallel**

$$A_{\text{db,parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_3)^2 = 1 - (0.001)^2 = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999$$

New composite system:

$$A_{\text{new}} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_{\text{db,parallel}}$$

$$A_{\text{new}} = 0.99 \times 0.995 \times 0.999999 = 0.98505 \approx 98.51\%$$

> **Insight:** Database redundancy improved availability by **0.11%** = ~865 minutes/year saved.

#### **Case 3: Full N+1 redundancy (each tier has 2 instances)**

$$A_{\text{new,2}} = (1-(1-0.99)^2) \times (1-(1-0.995)^2) \times (1-(1-0.999)^2)$$

$$A_{\text{new,2}} = 0.9999 \times 0.999975 \times 0.999999 = 0.99987 \approx 99.987\%$$

> **Result:** Annual downtime reduced to **~68 minutes** ("three nines plus").

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Service Models (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

| Feature | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| **User manages** | OS, Runtime, Middleware, App, Data | App and Data only | Nothing (only usage) |
| **Provider manages** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | OS, Runtime, Middleware, O/S updates | All layers including app |
| **Flexibility** | **Highest** | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Control** | **Highest** | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Developer productivity** | Low (must do everything) | High (focus on code) | **Highest** (just use) |
| **Time-to-market** | Slowest | Fast | **Fastest** |
| **Cost model** | Pay for VMs + storage + transfer | Pay for platform usage | Per-user subscription |
| **Example providers** | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP GCE | Heroku, App Engine, Beanstalk | Office 365, Salesforce, Gmail |
| **Typical use case** | Custom enterprise apps, lift-and-shift | API development, microservices, ML training | Email, CRM, collaboration |
| **Skill required** | Sysadmin + DevOps | App developer | End user |
| **Scalability** | Manual / scripted | Automatic (platform-managed) | Automatic (provider-managed) |
| **Security responsibility** | Shared (user secures OS+) | Shared (provider secures platform) | Provider's responsibility |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cloud Deployment Models

| Feature | **Public** | **Private** | **Hybrid** | **Community** |
|---------|-----------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Ownership** | Third-party provider | Single organization | Mixed | Group of orgs |
| **Location** | Off-premises | On-premises / hosted | Both | On or off premises |
| **Cost** | CapEx → OpEx (low upfront) | High CapEx | Medium | Shared |
| **Security** | Lower control | **Highest** control | Tunable | Tunable |
| **Scalability** | **Highest** (elastic) | Limited by capacity | High | Medium |
| **Compliance** | Limited industries | Easy (regulated) | Flexible | Industry-specific |
| **Latency** | Higher (WAN) | **Lowest** (LAN) | Mixed | Low–Medium |
| **Example** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack, VMware | AWS Outposts, Azure Arc | GovCloud, health clouds |

---

### 3.5 Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) vs Microservices vs Cloud-Native

| Aspect | **SOA** | **Microservices** | **Cloud-Native** |
|--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Granularity** | Coarse (services) | Fine (functions) | Ultra-fine + containers |
| **Communication** | ESB (Enterprise Service Bus) | REST/gRPC, message queues | Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) |
| **Coupling** | Tighter | Looser | Loosest |
| **Deployment** | Monolithic stack | Independent services | Containers + K8s + CI/CD |
| **Origin era** | 2000s | 2010s | 2015–present |
| **Best suited for** | Enterprise integration | Scalable web apps | Distributed, resilient systems |

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Cost-per-Use Calculation

**Scenario:** A startup runs **4 VM instances** (each **2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM**) continuously for **730 hours/month** (1 month), with **500 GB** of block storage and **2 TB** of outbound data transfer.

Pricing (hypothetical provider):
- vCPU-hour: ₹3
- GB-RAM-hour: ₹1
- GB-month storage: ₹2
- GB egress: ₹7

Compute cost:
$$C_{\text{compute}} = (4 \times 2 \times 3) \times 730 + (4 \times 8 \times 1) \times 730$$

$$C_{\text{compute}} = 17{,}520 + 23{,}360 = \text{₹}40{,}880$$

Storage cost:
$$C_{\text{storage}} = 500 \times 2 = \text{₹}1{,}000$$

Transfer cost:
$$C_{\text{transfer}} = 2{,}000 \times 7 = \text{₹}14{,}000$$

Total monthly bill:
$$C_{\text{total}} = 40{,}880 + 1{,}000 + 14{,}000 = \text{₹}55{,}880$$

> **Insight:** Egress (outbound transfer) often dominates cloud bills (**~25%** here) — students must remember this for KTU case-study questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-Layer Cloud Computing Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[LAYER 5: APPLICATION WORKLOADS\nSaaS Apps, Microservices, ML Models, APIs]
    L4[LAYER 4: PLATFORM AND SERVICE LAYER\nIaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS, Container Orchestration]
    L3[LAYER 3: RESOURCE POOL LAYER\nCompute Pool, Storage Pool, Network Pool]
    L2[LAYER 2: VIRTUALIZATION LAYER\nHypervisors, VMs, Containers, vSwitches]
    L1[LAYER 1: PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE\nServers, Storage Arrays, Routers, Power, Cooling]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> **Reading aid:** The arrow direction is **top-down demand** (apps request services) and **bottom-up provisioning** (hardware provides capacity). In practice, control flows in both directions via the **orchestration plane**.

---

### 4.2 NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model (Service vs Deployment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SM[Service Models]
        SaaS[SaaS\nSoftware as a Service]
        PaaS[PaaS\nPlatform as a Service]
        IaaS[IaaS\nInfrastructure as a Service]
    end
    subgraph DM[Deployment Models]
        Pub[Public Cloud]
        Pri[Private Cloud]
        Hyb[Hybrid Cloud]
        Com[Community Cloud]
    end
    User[End User / Developer] --> SaaS
    User --> PaaS
    User --> IaaS
    SaaS --> Pub
    PaaS --> Pub
    PaaS --> Hyb
    IaaS --> Pri
    IaaS --> Hyb
    IaaS --> Com
```

> **Reading aid:** Lines show which service model can be hosted on which deployment model — they are **orthogonal** (any service model can run on any deployment model).

---

### 4.3 End-to-End Cloud Request Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CLIENT DEVICE\nBrowser / Mobile / IoT] --> B[CDN AND LOAD BALANCER]
    B --> C[API GATEWAY\nAuth, Throttling, Routing]
    C --> D[MICROSERVICES LAYER\nUser Service, Order Service, Payment Service]
    D --> E[CONTAINER ORCHESTRATOR\nKubernetes, ECS]
    E --> F[VM POOL\nAuto-Scaled EC2 / Azure VM]
    F --> G[PHYSICAL SERVERS\nHypervisor Host]
    G --> H[STORAGE TIER\nObject, Block, Database]
    D --> H
    I[MONITORING AND LOGGING\nPrometheus, ELK] -.Observes.-> D
    I -.Observes.-> E
    I -.Observes.-> F
    J[IAM AND SECURITY\nKMS, WAF, Identity] -.Protects.-> C
    J -.Protects.-> D
    K[BILLING AND METERING\nPay-per-use Engine] -.Charges.-> A
```

> **Reading aid:** Solid arrows = request/data flow. Dotted arrows = cross-cutting concerns (observability, security, billing) that interact with all layers.

---

### 4.4 Multi-Tenancy Isolation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Cloud Provider Datacenter] --> HV1[Hypervisor Host 1]
    P --> HV2[Hypervisor Host 2]
    HV1 --> VM1A[Tenant A VM]
    HV1 --> VM1B[Tenant B VM]
    HV1 --> VM1C[Tenant C VM]
    HV2 --> VM2A[Tenant A VM - DR]
    HV2 --> VM2D[Tenant D VM]
    NET[VLAN / SDN Network] -.Isolates.-> VM1A
    NET -.Isolates.-> VM1B
    NET -.Isolates.-> VM1C
    NET -.Isolates.-> VM2D
```

> **Reading aid:** Tenants (A, B, C, D) share the same physical hardware but are **isolated by hypervisor + network segmentation** (VLAN / SDN). This is the essence of **multi-tenancy**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define Cloud Architecture. List its essential components.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Cloud Architecture is the **structural blueprint** that organizes cloud computing components—front-end, back-end, network, and service delivery—into a coherent, scalable, and on-demand system.

**Essential Components (any 3 of the following for full marks):**
1. Client infrastructure (front-end)
2. Application/SaaS layer
3. Back-end services (servers, storage, DB)
4. Network fabric (LAN/WAN, CDN)
5. Cloud service management (provisioning, monitoring, security, billing)
6. Virtualization & orchestration layer

> **Examiner's tip:** Mention the **5 NIST essential characteristics** for extra credit.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (tabular form preferred):**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|--------|------|------|------|
| **User manages** | OS upward | App + Data | Nothing |
| **Provider manages** | Hardware + Virt | OS + Runtime + Middleware | Everything |
| **Example** | AWS EC2 | Google App Engine | Microsoft 365 |

> **Examiner's tip:** Draw a small "responsibility onion" diagram to gain full marks.

---

### **PART B – Extended Answer (14 Marks) – Internal Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a)** *(7 marks)*
**Explain in detail the layered architecture of cloud computing with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

A typical cloud architecture is organized into **five layers**, from bottom to top:

1. **Physical Layer (Hardware):** Contains data center components — servers (x86, ARM, GPU), SAN/NAS storage, switches, routers, and power/cooling. Provides raw compute, storage, and bandwidth.

2. **Virtualization Layer:** Uses **Type-1 hypervisors** (VMware ESXi, Xen) to create VMs. Enables **resource partitioning, isolation, and abstraction**. Containers (Docker) sit one level above VMs.

3. **Resource Pool Layer:** Aggregates virtualized resources into **compute pools, storage pools, and network pools**. Supports **multi-tenancy**.

4. **Platform / Service Layer:** Exposes resources as **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS** services. Provides auto-scaling, load balancing, managed databases, message queues, and orchestration via Kubernetes.

5. **Application / Workload Layer:** Hosts **end-user applications** such as web apps, ML models, ERP systems. Communicates with lower layers via APIs (REST/gRPC).

*Draw the 5-layer diagram (refer to Section 4.1) — 1 mark reserved for diagram.*

**Role of each layer (2 marks):**
- **Bottom layers** provide abstraction and pooling
- **Middle layers** provide management and orchestration
- **Top layers** provide user-facing services and business logic

---

#### **Part (b)** *(7 marks)*
**Describe the NIST cloud computing reference model. List and briefly explain the three service models and four deployment models.**

**Model Answer:**

The **NIST SP 800-145** reference model is the globally accepted standard for defining cloud computing architecture.

**Three Service Models:**
1. **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service):** Provides raw virtualized resources (compute, storage, network). User controls OS, storage, apps, and selected network components. Example: AWS EC2.
2. **PaaS (Platform as a Service):** Provides a platform with OS, middleware, development tools. User only deploys their application code and data. Example: Google App Engine.
3. **SaaS (Software as a Service):** Provides fully functional applications running on cloud infrastructure. User accesses via thin client/browser. Example: Salesforce, Office 365.

**Four Deployment Models:**
1. **Public Cloud:** Open to general public, owned by third-party provider. High scalability, low cost.
2. **Private Cloud:** Operated exclusively for one organization. High control, high security.
3. **Hybrid Cloud:** Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by orchestration. Balances scalability and control.
4. **Community Cloud:** Shared by organizations with common concerns (security, compliance, jurisdiction). Example: government health cloud.

*Include a small sketch of the model for 1 additional mark.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### **Part (a)** *(7 marks)*
**With neat diagrams, explain the components of cloud architecture. Discuss the role of virtualization and multi-tenancy.**

**Model Answer:**

The major components of cloud architecture are:

1. **Front-end (Client Infrastructure):** Web browsers, mobile apps, thin clients that consume cloud services via the Internet.

2. **Back-end (Servers, Storage, Databases):** The cloud's brain — physical/virtual servers, distributed storage, RDBMS, NoSQL databases.

3. **Network Fabric:** Connects front-end and back-end; includes routers, switches, firewalls, CDN, SDN, VPCs.

4. **Cloud Service Management:** Comprises four sub-modules:
   - **Provisioning & Deployment**
   - **Monitoring & Performance Management**
   - **Security & Identity Management**
   - **Billing & Metering**

5. **Orchestration Layer:** Coordinates provisioning, scaling, and failover across resources.

**Role of Virtualization:**
- Decouples OS and applications from underlying hardware
- Enables **server consolidation** (10–40:1 VM density)
- Supports **live migration** and **snapshotting**
- Foundation for **multi-tenancy**

**Role of Multi-Tenancy:**
- Allows multiple customers (tenants) to share the same physical infrastructure
- Achieved via **isolation mechanisms** (VLANs, namespaces, security groups, hypervisor separation)
- Reduces cost through **economies of scale**
- Tenant can be a user, department, or entire organization

*Draw a multi-tenancy block diagram (refer to Section 4.4).*

---

#### **Part (b)** *(7 marks)*
**A cloud service provider offers 99.9% availability SLA. Calculate the maximum permitted downtime per year. If the same provider upgrades to 99.99%, what is the improvement in allowed downtime?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert SLA to decimal**
$A_1 = 99.9\% = 0.999$

**Step 2: Calculate unavailability**
$U_1 = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001$

**Step 3: Calculate minutes in a year**
$\text{Min/year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600$

**Step 4: Annual downtime for 99.9%**
$D_1 = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6 \ \text{minutes} = 8.76 \ \text{hours/year}$

**Step 5: For 99.99%**
$U_2 = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$
$D_2 = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \ \text{minutes/year}$

**Step 6: Improvement**
$\Delta D = 525.6 - 52.56 = 473.04 \ \text{minutes} \approx 7.88 \ \text{hours/year}$

> **Result:** Upgrading from 99.9% → 99.99% reduces annual downtime by **~7.88 hours**, demonstrating why "four nines" is the standard for mission-critical workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Confusing Service Models with Deployment Models:** Students often write "IaaS is a public cloud" — **wrong**. IaaS is a *service model*; public is a *deployment model*. They are orthogonal.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark sub-questions, at least **1 mark is reserved for diagrams/tables**. Omitting them = direct mark loss.
> 3. **Writing availability as 0.999% instead of 99.9%:** Decimal vs percentage conversion errors are the #1 calculation mistake. **Always convert to decimal first.**
> 4. **Forgetting units in downtime answers:** Examiner deducts 0.5 mark if "minutes" or "hours" is missing.
> 5. **Memorizing only NIST:** KTU 2024 also expects familiarity with **IBM layered reference architecture** and **Cisco's cloud architecture** — don't limit to one model.

---

### 🔁 **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Cloud Architecture = blueprint** of front-end + back-end + network + services
- [x] **Five NIST essential characteristics** (must-memorize): on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service
- [x] **Five architectural layers:** Physical → Virtualization → Resource Pool → Platform/Service → Application
- [x] **Three service models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (also remember FaaS, DaaS as extensions)
- [x] **Four deployment models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community
- [x] **99.9% SLA** ⇒ **8.76 hours** downtime/year; **99.99%** ⇒ **52.6 minutes/year**
- [x] **MTBF & MTTR** govern system availability: $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- [x] **Series availability** = product; **Parallel (redundant) availability** = $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$
- [x] **Multi-tenancy** is enabled by virtualization + isolation (VLAN/SDN/namespace)
- [x] **PUE** measures data center efficiency; ideal = 1.0
- [x] **SOA → Microservices → Cloud-Native** is the evolution of service architecture
- [x] **NIST SP 800-145** is the global standard; KTU expects it by name
- [x] **Cost optimization tip:** Outbound data transfer (egress) often dominates bills — always calculate it
- [x] **Diagram marks:** Always draw at least one labeled block diagram in 7-mark questions

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Modelling and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Modelling and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cloud Modelling?

**Cloud Modelling** is the process of creating an abstract representation of cloud-based systems to define how the resources, services, components, and their interactions are organized, deployed, and managed. It captures the structural, behavioral, and deployment aspects of a cloud system *before* actual implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned with NIST SP 800-145 & Buyya's Cloud Computing Text):**
> *"Cloud Modelling is the systematic abstraction of cloud infrastructure, services, and deployment patterns using reference models, architectural frameworks, and design principles to enable standardized, scalable, and on-demand service delivery over the Internet."*

---

## 1.2 What is Cloud Design?

**Cloud Design** is the engineering discipline of translating business and functional requirements into a concrete cloud architecture. It involves selecting appropriate **deployment models**, **service models**, **resource pools**, **elasticity strategies**, and **security boundaries** to satisfy Quality-of-Service (QoS) parameters such as availability, scalability, reliability, and cost.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy 🍕

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pizza-as-a-Service Analogy (Albert Barron, IBM, 2014 — widely cited in KTU references):**
>
> Imagine you are hungry for pizza. You have multiple options:
>
> | Your Choice | Real-World Action | Cloud Equivalent |
> |---|---|---|
> | Make pizza at home | You buy dough, cheese, oven, gas | **On-Premises Datacenter** |
> | Buy a raw pizza & bake | You buy pizza base, bake at home | **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** |
> | Get a delivered pizza | You order, just heat & eat | **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** |
> | Eat pizza at restaurant | You just show up and eat | **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** |
>
> **Lesson:** As you move "up" the stack, you manage *less* of the underlying infrastructure, and the cloud provider handles more.

---

## 1.4 Key Design Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following **standard metrics** must be remembered for KTU examinations:

- **Availability** → typically `99.9%` ("three nines") to `99.999%` ("five nines")
- **Scalability** → horizontal (scale-out) vs. vertical (scale-up)
- **Elasticity** → ability to dynamically match resources to load
- **Multi-tenancy** → single instance serves multiple customers
- **Pay-per-use** → billing based on metered consumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1, you must be able to **compare** IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, **draw** the cloud reference model, and **justify** design choices using deployment and service model trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model (Foundational)

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** Special Publication 800-145 defines cloud computing using **3 service models**, **4 deployment models**, and **5 essential characteristics**.

### 2.1.1 The Five Essential Characteristics

1. **On-demand Self-Service** — User provisions compute/storage automatically without human interaction.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Services available over standard networks (HTTP, REST).
3. **Resource Pooling** — Physical/virtual resources dynamically assigned to multiple tenants.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities scaled out/in quickly, often appearing unlimited to the user.
5. **Measured Service** — Metering at appropriate abstraction level (storage, bandwidth, CPU-seconds).

---

### 2.1.2 The Three Service Models (Logical Stack)

| Layer | Service Model | What the **Customer** Manages | What the **Provider** Manages | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top | **SaaS** — Software-as-a-Service | Application usage, user accounts | Everything else: app, data, OS, runtime, middleware, servers, storage, networking | **"User just USES it"** |
| Middle | **PaaS** — Platform-as-a-Service | Application + data | Runtime, middleware, OS, servers, storage, networking | **"User BUILDS on it"** |
| Bottom | **IaaS** — Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Application, data, runtime, middleware, OS | Servers, storage, networking, virtualization | **"User CONFIGS it"** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Identification Tip (Examiner's Trick):**
> In KTU answers, if a question mentions *"the developer codes the application but does not install OS"*, the answer is **PaaS**. If the user even installs the OS, the answer is **IaaS**.

---

### 2.1.3 The Four Deployment Models

| Deployment Model | Owner | Access Boundary | KTU Exam Trigger Words |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Open to general public | "AWS, Azure, Google Cloud" |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Within organization firewall | "Bank internal cloud, intranet-hosted" |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combination of public + private | Burst-out to public during peak load | "Sensitive data on-premise + scalable web on public" |
| **Community Cloud** | Multiple orgs with shared concerns | Shared by orgs with common policy | "Government departments, universities consortium" |

---

## 2.2 Cloud Architecture Reference Model (IBM / Buyya Style)

A cloud architecture can be decomposed into **4 logical layers**:

1. **Fabric / Hardware Layer** — Physical servers, storage, network devices.
2. **Unified Resource Layer** — Virtualization, abstraction of compute/storage/network.
3. **Platform Layer** — Operating systems, middleware, runtimes (e.g., JVM, Node.js).
4. **Application Layer** — End-user SaaS applications.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Use `\vert` notation — NEVER raw pipes)

### 2.3.1 Availability Calculation

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $A$ = **Availability** (in $\vert\%\vert$)
- $MTBF$ = **Mean Time Between Failures**
- $MTTR$ = **Mean Time To Repair**

The number of allowed downtime minutes per year:

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = \left(1 - A\right) \times 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

| Availability $\vert\%\vert$ | Allowed Downtime / Year |
|---|---|
| `99%` (two nines) | $\approx 5256$ minutes ($\approx 3.65$ days) |
| `99.9%` (three nines) | $\approx 525.6$ minutes ($\approx 8.77$ hours) |
| `99.99%` (four nines) | $\approx 52.56$ minutes |
| `99.999%` (five nines) | $\approx 5.26$ minutes |

### 2.3.2 Horizontal Scalability / Pooling Index

$$
P_{\text{efficiency}} = \frac{N_{\text{active}} \times T_{\text{util}}}{N_{\text{total}} \times T_{\text{slot}}}
$$

- $N_{\text{active}}$ = number of active resource instances
- $N_{\text{total}}$ = total provisioned instances
- $T_{\text{util}}$ = average utilization time per slot
- $T_{\text{slot}}$ = total time-slot duration

### 2.3.3 Cost Estimation (Pay-per-Use)

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( u_i \times p_i \right)
$$

- $u_i$ = usage quantity of resource $\vert i \vert$
- $p_i$ = per-unit price of resource $\vert i \vert$

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Cloud Modelling

- **Capacity Planning** — Predicting required VM count, storage, bandwidth.
- **SLA Negotiation** — Translating business needs to measurable availability/latency targets.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — Choosing geographic regions for redundancy (RPO, RTO).
- **Cost Optimization** — Selecting between on-demand, reserved, and spot pricing models.
- **Security Boundary Definition** — Determining shared-responsibility zones (provider vs. customer).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why `3` Service Models and `4` Deployment Models? (Logical Derivation)

Although these numbers are not "derived" mathematically, they are **logically constructed** in NIST SP 800-145. We will derive them step-by-step to satisfy KTU Part A and Part B answers.

### Step 1 — Identify the Abstraction Stack
A cloud system has a finite stack: `Hardware → Virtualization → OS → Middleware → Runtime → Application → User Data`.
The boundary between **what the user controls** and **what the provider controls** can be placed at exactly `3` meaningful positions:

- **Lowest boundary** (user controls OS upward) → **IaaS**
- **Middle boundary** (user controls application only) → **PaaS**
- **Highest boundary** (user uses ready-made software) → **SaaS**

$$
\text{ServiceModel} = \{ \text{SaaS}, \text{PaaS}, \text{IaaS} \}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Ownership Boundaries
Cloud resources can be owned by exactly `4` types of entities:
1. A commercial provider → **Public**
2. A single organization → **Private**
3. A combination (burst, hybrid topology) → **Hybrid**
4. A community of related orgs → **Community**

$$
\text{DeploymentModel} = \{ \text{Public, Private, Hybrid, Community} \}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Product Analysis (Design Decision Matrix)
A design choice is a tuple:

$$
D = \langle \text{ServiceModel}, \text{DeploymentModel}, \text{Region}, \text{SLA} \rangle
$$

> For example: `D = ⟨ SaaS, Public, ap-south-1, 99.9% ⟩` describes Gmail hosted on Google Cloud for Indian users with three-nines SLA.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern):** *"A cloud VM has MTBF = 800 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute availability and the allowed annual downtime in minutes."*

### Step 1 — Substitute into availability formula
$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{800}{800 + 2} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{800}{802} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute numerically
$$
A = 0.99751 \times 100\% = 99.751\%
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to annual downtime
$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.99751) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 0.00249 \times 525{,}600
$$

### Step 4 — Final result
$$
D_{\text{annual}} \approx 1309 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 21.8 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 5 — Verdict
This VM is roughly a **"two-nine"** system, **inadequate** for banking workloads but acceptable for academic lab use.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Cloud Model

This matrix is a **direct KTU Part B (14-mark)** template.

| Decision Criterion | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) | Very Low | Very High | Medium | Medium |
