| Operational Expenditure (OPEX) | Pay-per-use | In-house team | Mixed | Shared |
| Scalability | Excellent | Limited | Excellent | Limited |
| Security & Compliance | Lower | Highest | Selective | Selective |
| Control | Lowest | Highest | Selective | Medium |
| Time to Deploy | Minutes | Months | Days | Months |
| Suitable For | Startups, web apps | Banks, defence | E-commerce peaks | Govt, universities |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Which model would you choose for an e-commerce company expecting festival-season traffic spikes?"*, the **only** correct answer is **Hybrid Cloud**.

---

## 3.4 Design Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

Cloud design is governed by the **CAP Triangle** analogy (adapted from Brewer's CAP theorem for distributed systems):

$$
\text{DesignGoal} \in \{\text{Consistency}, \text{Availability}, \text{Partition Tolerance}\}
$$

Cloud systems typically **sacrifice strict consistency** to gain **availability and partition tolerance** (AP systems), aligning with eventual-consistency patterns (e.g., DynamoDB, Cassandra).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: NIST Cloud Reference Model (Vertical Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["SaaS - Software as a Service - User just uses the application"]
    layer2["PaaS - Platform as a Service - User builds and deploys apps"]
    layer3["IaaS - Infrastructure as a Service - User configures OS and above"]
    layer4["Virtualization Layer - Hypervisor and VM abstraction"]
    layer5["Physical Fabric - Servers, Storage, Network devices"]

    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1

    pub["Public Cloud"] -. serves .-> layer1
    priv["Private Cloud"] -. serves .-> layer1
    hyb["Hybrid Cloud"] -. serves .-> layer1
    com["Community Cloud"] -. serves .-> layer1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cloud Design Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    req["Step 1 - Gather Business Requirements"] --> ana["Step 2 - Analyse Workload Type"]
    ana --> sm{"Step 3 - Select Service Model"}
    sm --> iaas["IaaS - For low-level control"]
    sm --> paas["PaaS - For developer productivity"]
    sm --> saas["SaaS - For end-user productivity"]

    iaas --> dm{"Step 4 - Select Deployment Model"}
    paas --> dm
    saas --> dm

    dm --> pub["Public"]
    dm --> pri["Private"]
    dm --> hyb["Hybrid"]
    dm --> com["Community"]

    pub --> sla["Step 5 - Define SLA - Availability, Latency, RTO, RPO"]
    pri --> sla
    hyb --> sla
    com --> sla

    sla --> sec["Step 6 - Security Boundary - Identity, Encryption, Compliance"]
    sec --> bill["Step 7 - Cost Model - Pay-per-use, Reserved, Spot"]
    bill --> mon["Step 8 - Monitor and Scale - Auto-scaling groups, Load balancers"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Responsibility Boundary Across Service Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    onprem["On-Premises - Customer manages EVERYTHING"]
    iaasBlock["IaaS Boundary - Provider manages Servers, Storage, Network only"]
    paasBlock["PaaS Boundary - Provider also manages OS, Middleware, Runtime"]
    saasBlock["SaaS Boundary - Provider also manages Application"]

    onprem --> iaasBlock --> paasBlock --> saasBlock
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hybrid Cloud Bursting Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["End Users"] --> lb["Global Load Balancer"]
    lb --> privDC["Private Cloud - Baseline Capacity"]
    lb --> pubDC["Public Cloud - Burst Capacity during Peak"]

    privDC --> db["Centralized Database - Primary"]
    pubDC --> db

    db -. replication .- dr["DR Region - Geo-redundant"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Scalability and Elasticity in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Scalability | Elasticity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability to handle increasing load by adding resources | Ability to automatically match resource supply to demand |
| Trigger | Long-term growth planning | Real-time load fluctuations |
| Direction | Up/Down or Out/In | Always dynamic (auto) |
| Time Scale | Days to weeks | Seconds to minutes |
| Example | Adding 10 new VMs permanently | Auto-scaling group spins up 5 VMs during 8 PM traffic peak |

Scalability is the *capability*; Elasticity is the *automation of that capability*. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**List any three essential characteristics of cloud computing as per NIST.**

**Model Answer:**
According to NIST SP 800-145, the **five** essential characteristics are:

1. **On-demand Self-Service** — Automatic provisioning without human intervention.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Accessible over standard networks via heterogeneous clients.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic resource assignment.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities scaled in/out, appearing unlimited to the user.
5. **Measured Service** — Metered billing at appropriate abstraction levels.

*(Any 3 = 3 marks; Full 5 would gain extra credit but question asked for 3.)*

---

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### 📌 QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model in detail with a neat diagram. List and define the three service models. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cloud service reports MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 1 hour. Calculate the availability in percentage and the allowed annual downtime in minutes. Identify the SLA class ("two nines" / "three nines" / "four nines" / "five nines") of this system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition:**
The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model, defined in **SP 800-145 (2011)**, standardizes cloud concepts using **5 characteristics**, **3 service models**, and **4 deployment models**.

**2. Five Essential Characteristics:**
- On-demand self-service
- Broad network access
- Resource pooling
- Rapid elasticity
- Measured service

**3. Three Service Models:**
- **SaaS** — Provider hosts the application; user only consumes it (e.g., Gmail, Office 365).
- **PaaS** — Provider hosts platform (OS, middleware, runtime); user develops and deploys applications (e.g., Google App Engine, Heroku).
- **IaaS** — Provider hosts raw infrastructure (compute, storage, network); user installs OS and above (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs).

**4. Diagram:**
*(Refer to SECTION 4.1 — Vertical Stack Diagram for 2 marks.)*

**5. Deployment Models:**
Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.

**Conclusion:** The NIST model provides a vendor-neutral vocabulary for cloud design.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 5 for textual explanation.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Availability formula:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting values:**

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5001} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A \approx 99.980\%
$$

**Step 3 — Annual downtime calculation:**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.99980) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 0.00020 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} \approx 105 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Step 4 — SLA classification:**

Since `99.980%` lies between `99.9%` and `99.99%`, the system belongs to the **"three nines"** SLA class with approximately `1 hour 45 minutes` of allowed annual downtime.

**Conclusion:** Suitable for most enterprise web workloads; insufficient for mission-critical banking systems requiring `99.999%`.

*(7 marks — 3 for formula derivation, 2 for numerical answer, 2 for classification.)*

---

---

### 📌 QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the four cloud deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) with respect to cost, security, scalability, and control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce company handles sensitive customer data (regulated by RBI) and experiences 10× traffic spikes during Diwali. Design a cloud architecture justifying your choice of deployment model, service model, and key design decisions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a):

| Criterion | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost (CAPEX)** | Very Low | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| **Cost (OPEX)** | Pay-per-use, predictable | In-house staffing | Mixed | Shared among orgs |
| **Security** | Lowest (multi-tenant) | Highest (single-tenant) | Selective — sensitive workloads on private | High for shared policy orgs |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by in-house infra | Bursts to public during peaks | Limited |
| **Control** | Minimal | Full | Selective | Medium |
| **Examples** | AWS, Azure, GCP | Bank internal cloud | Retail e-commerce | Govt cloud, university consortium |

**Verdict:** Public offers best cost-efficiency; Private offers best control; Hybrid balances both; Community suits regulated-but-shared domains.

*(7 marks — 1 per criterion × 4 = 4 marks + 3 marks for examples and verdict.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Requirement Analysis:**
- **Data Sensitivity:** RBI-regulated → cannot be exposed publicly.
- **Traffic Pattern:** Baseline = 10,000 concurrent users; Peak = 100,000 concurrent users (Diwali).

**Step 2 — Deployment Model Choice → HYBRID**
- **Private Cloud** hosts sensitive payment and PII data (compliance boundary).
- **Public Cloud** hosts the storefront, catalog, and cart (scalable boundary).
- Connected via **VPN / Direct Connect** with encrypted channels.

**Step 3 — Service Model Choice → Combination (IaaS + PaaS)**
- **IaaS** on private cloud for legacy ERP and database (full OS control needed).
- **PaaS** on public cloud for catalog microservice (faster deployment).

**Step 4 — Key Design Decisions:**
- **Auto-scaling groups** triggered at `70%` CPU threshold.
- **Multi-AZ deployment** for `99.99%` SLA.
- **Database replication** from private to public read-replicas for catalog browsing without exposing writes.
- **WAF and DDoS protection** at the public edge.
- **CDN** for static images to offload origin.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The hybrid model satisfies both **compliance** (RBI) and **elasticity** (10× spike) — a textbook KTU answer.

*(7 marks — 2 for model justification, 3 for design components, 2 for conclusion.)*

---

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cloud Modelling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating SaaS with SaaS providers** — Students write "AWS is SaaS" (WRONG; EC2 is IaaS; S3 is IaaS-storage). Only end-user applications like Gmail or Office 365 are SaaS.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** — A 7-mark question on NIST model without a diagram loses 2 marks minimum.
> 3. **Confusing Hybrid with Community** — Hybrid = SAME org using multiple clouds; Community = MULTIPLE orgs sharing one cloud.
> 4. **Skipping MTBF/MTTR units** — Always write "hours" or "minutes"; partial marks are lost otherwise.
> 5. **Writing "three nines" without numerical proof** — Always show the percentage and the downtime calculation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **NIST** defines cloud using `5 characteristics + 3 service models + 4 deployment models`.
- ✅ **Service Stack** (bottom→top): `IaaS → PaaS → SaaS`.
- ✅ **IaaS**: User manages OS and above. **PaaS**: User manages app and data. **SaaS**: User just consumes.
- ✅ **Public** = open; **Private** = org-internal; **Hybrid** = public + private burst; **Community** = shared by similar orgs.
- ✅ **Availability formula**: $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Annual downtime** = `(1 − A) × 525,600` minutes.
- ✅ **SLA Tiers**: `99%` → two nines; `99.9%` → three nines; `99.99%` → four nines; `99.999%` → five nines.
- ✅ **Pay-per-use cost** = `Σ(uᵢ × pᵢ)`.
- ✅ **Hybrid Cloud** is the default answer for sensitive-data + peak-traffic scenarios.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy + Resource Pooling** is the foundation of cloud economics.
- ✅ **Auto-scaling + Load balancing + Multi-AZ** = standard high-availability cloud design triad.
- ✅ **CAP trade-off**: Cloud favors **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) over strict Consistency.

> 📌 **Final Tip:** In KTU answers, always start with a **definition**, then **diagram**, then **comparison table**, then **numerical proof**. This 4-part structure consistently fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization - Foundations, Grid, Cloud and Virtualization, Virtualization and Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtualization - Foundations, Grid, Cloud and Virtualization, Virtualization and Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Virtualization** is an abstraction layer that decouples the physical hardware from the operating system, applications, and services running on it. It enables a single physical machine (the *host*) to be partitioned into multiple, isolated, software-defined environments (the *guests* or *Virtual Machines / VMs*), each capable of running its own operating system and workload as if it possessed the underlying hardware exclusively.

In the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing framework, virtualization is positioned as the **foundational enabling technology** of the modern Cloud Computing paradigm. Without virtualization, the on-demand provisioning, elastic scaling, and multi-tenancy promised by Cloud Service Models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) would be economically and architecturally infeasible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *Virtualization is the technique of creating logical (virtual) representations of physical computing resources (servers, storage, networks, desktops) such that the underlying physical infrastructure can be shared, abstracted, and dynamically allocated to multiple isolated execution environments.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Think of a physical server as a **large, single-family house**. Traditionally, one house could only house one family. If a second family needed a home, you had to **build a second house** (i.e., buy a second physical server). This is the *non-virtualized* world — wasteful, expensive, and rigid.

**Virtualization** transforms that single house into a **multi-storey apartment building**:
- The **land and foundation** = the physical server hardware (CPU, RAM, disk, NIC).
- The **building structure** = the **Hypervisor** (Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM) — the landlord that enforces boundaries.
- Each **apartment unit** = a **Virtual Machine (VM)** — an isolated, self-contained living space.
- Each **apartment's internal furnishings** = the **Guest Operating System + Applications** running inside that VM.
- Each **apartment's key/lock** = strict **isolation** — one tenant cannot enter another's unit, ensuring security and fault containment.

Just as a single building can now serve dozens of families, a single physical server can now run dozens of VMs — each convinced it "owns" the entire machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Pillar Statement:**
> Virtualization is the **enabling technology** of cloud computing. Grid computing preceded the cloud; cloud computing *industrialized* and *productized* the resource-sharing ideas of grid computing by adding virtualization-driven elasticity, self-service provisioning, and pay-per-use metering.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of the Module

| Pillar | One-Line Definition | Role in the Ecosystem |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Foundations** | The core abstraction technique decoupling hardware from workloads. | The *engine* that creates logical resources. |
| **Grid Computing** | A distributed computing paradigm pooling geographically dispersed, heterogeneous resources to solve a single large problem. | The *intellectual precursor* to cloud computing. |
| **Virtualization + Cloud Computing** | The integration of virtualization into service-delivery clouds (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) for elasticity, multi-tenancy, and resource pooling. | The *operational marriage* that defines modern cloud. |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Host Machine**: The underlying physical hardware that runs the hypervisor.
- **Guest Machine**: The virtual machine (VM) running its own OS atop the hypervisor.
- **Hypervisor (VMM)**: The software layer that creates, runs, and manages VMs.
- **Physical Machine → Virtual Machine (PM-to-VM) ratio**: Often referred to as the **consolidation ratio**, typically **1:10 to 1:40** in production clouds.
- **Live Migration**: Moving a running VM from one physical host to another with **zero downtime**.
- **Over-commitment / Oversubscription**: Allocating more virtual resources to VMs than physically exist, exploiting the statistical reality that workloads rarely peak simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Virtualization — A Layered Breakdown

A virtualized environment is constructed as a strict stack of abstraction layers. Each layer shields the layer above it from the physical complexity of the layer below.

**Layer 0 — Physical Hardware (Bare Metal)**
- CPU, RAM, Storage, Network Interface Cards, BIOS/UEFI firmware.
- This is the immutable physical reality.

**Layer 1 — The Hypervisor (VMM)**
- The **Virtual Machine Monitor** — the heart of virtualization.
- Two architectural families:
  - **Type-1 (Bare-Metal / Native) Hypervisor**: Installed directly on hardware. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core), Citrix XenServer, KVM (Linux kernel-integrated).
  - **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor**: Installed as an application inside a host OS. Examples: VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop.
- Performs CPU scheduling, memory allocation, I/O emulation, and VM lifecycle management.

**Layer 2 — Virtual Machines (Guest Domains)**
- Each VM is a fully isolated software container with:
  - Virtual CPU (vCPU)
  - Virtual RAM
  - Virtual Disk (backed by an image file or LUN)
  - Virtual NIC (connected to a virtual switch)
- Each VM runs an unmodified **Guest Operating System** (Windows Server, Linux distro, etc.).

**Layer 3 — Applications / Workloads**
- The end-user services: web servers, databases, ERP systems, ML training jobs.
- These have **zero awareness** that they are virtualized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" Matters:** This layered design is precisely what allows a cloud provider to provision a new customer VM in **seconds** — they only need to instantiate Layer 2 + Layer 3, while the physical hardware and hypervisor (Layers 0 + 1) are amortized across thousands of tenants.

### 2.2 Grid Computing — The Distributed Precursor

**Grid Computing** is a distributed computing architecture in which a coordinated network of geographically dispersed, administratively heterogeneous, and organizationally independent computers collaborate to solve a single, large-scale computational problem or to provide a shared pool of resources.

#### 2.2.1 Defining Characteristics of Grid Computing (Foster's Checklist)

The seminal work by **Ian Foster** (often cited in KTU references) defines a Grid by three converging properties:

1. **Coordination of distributed resources** — resources are not under centralized control.
2. **Standard, open, general-purpose protocols and interfaces** — enabling interoperability across vendors and institutions.
3. **Non-trivial quality of service** — guarantees (latency, throughput, security) beyond best-effort.

#### 2.2.2 Architectural Tiers of a Grid

- **Fabric Layer**: The actual physical resources — CPUs, storage, sensors, instruments.
- **Connectivity Layer**: The communication protocols (TCP/IP, Web Services, security/identity).
- **Resource Layer**: Information protocols for discovering and monitoring resources.
- **Collective Layer**: Coordination of multiple resources (workload management, replication, scheduling).
- **Application Layer**: The user-facing scientific or business application that consumes the grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grid vs. Cloud — The Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - A **Grid** is built to *solve a problem* (tightly coupled scientific workloads — e.g., CERN's LHC data analysis, protein folding).
> - A **Cloud** is built to *deliver a service* (loosely coupled, multi-tenant, on-demand business workloads — e.g., Netflix streaming).
> - Grid emphasizes **throughput and aggregate compute**; Cloud emphasizes **elasticity, self-service, and metering**.

### 2.3 Virtualization and Cloud Computing — The Operational Marriage

Cloud Computing, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus and aligned with the NIST SP 800-145 reference model, is the **on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources** that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

**Virtualization is the technology that physically realizes these abstract cloud properties.** The mapping is direct and examinable:

| Cloud Characteristic (NIST) | Virtualization Mechanism that Enables It |
|---|---|
| **On-demand self-service** | Automated VM instantiation via hypervisor APIs. |
| **Broad network access** | Virtual NICs + virtual switches (vSwitches / OVS) on top of physical NICs. |
| **Resource pooling** | Multi-tenant hypervisor multiplexing physical resources. |
| **Rapid elasticity** | Live VM migration, hot-add of vCPUs/RAM, snapshot/clone. |
| **Measured service** | Hypervisor-level metering of vCPU-hours, GB-RAM-hours, GB-IOPS. |

#### 2.3.1 The Service-Model Layer Cake

- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)**: Users receive **raw VMs** — vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC. They install their own OS and stack. (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine).
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)**: Users receive a **pre-configured runtime** (e.g., App Engine, Azure App Service) — the OS and middleware are virtualized and abstracted away.
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)**: Users receive a **finished application** (e.g., Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce) — the entire virtualization stack is invisible to the end user.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on symbols:** In all tables and equations below, the vertical bar `|` is rendered as `\vert` to comply with markdown table integrity rules.

#### 2.4.1 Virtualization Capacity & Consolidation Formulas

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Consolidation Ratio} = \dfrac{N_{VM}}{N_{PM}}$ | Number of virtual machines per physical host. | dimensionless |
| $\text{CPU Over-commit Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum vCPU_{\text{allocated}}}{N_{\text{physical cores}}}$ | Ratio of virtual to physical CPU cores. Typically 2:1 to 6:1. | dimensionless |
| $\text{Memory Over-commit Ratio} = \dfrac{\sum vRAM_{\text{allocated}}}{RAM_{\text{physical}}}$ | Ratio of virtual to physical memory. Often 1.0 to 1.5. | dimensionless |
| $\text{Utilization}_{\text{effective}} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Actual Load}_i}{\text{Capacity}_{\text{physical}}}$ | Fraction of physical capacity that is *actually* consumed. | unitless fraction (0–1) |
| $\text{VM Density} = \dfrac{N_{VM}}{A_{\text{rack}} \text{ in U}}$ | VMs per rack unit — a data-center efficiency metric. | VMs per U |
| $\text{TCO Savings} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Cost}_{\text{virtualized}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{non-virtualized}}}$ | Total Cost of Ownership reduction. Typical 40–80%. | dimensionless |

#### 2.4.2 Grid Computing Aggregate Throughput

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $T_{\text{grid}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i \cdot (1 - O_i)$ | Aggregate grid throughput = sum of node throughputs minus overhead. $O_i$ is the communication/scheduling overhead fraction. |
| $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Amdahl's Law: speedup of a parallel job with parallel fraction $P$ across $N$ nodes. $S \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ as $N \rightarrow \infty$. |
| $E_{\text{parallel}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{actual}}}{N}$ | Parallel efficiency: actual speedup divided by the number of nodes. $E \in (0, 1]$. |

#### 2.4.3 Cloud Service Availability (Operational SLA Math)

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Where MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. |
| $\text{Availability}_{\text{annual}} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \text{Downtime}_{\text{year}}}{525{,}600 \text{ min}}$ | Annual uptime fraction. "Five nines" = **99.999%** ≈ 5.26 min/yr downtime. |
| $\text{Cloud Downtime}_{\text{serial}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - D_i)$ | Aggregate downtime when failures are independent and recovery is serial. |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Real World

- **For Cloud Architects**: Choosing the right hypervisor (Type-1 vs Type-2), the right over-commit ratio, and the right live-migration technology directly determines hardware ROI, SLA compliance, and operational cost.
- **For DevOps / SRE Teams**: Understanding virtualization layers clarifies why certain monitoring metrics (vCPU steal time, ballooning, ready-queue depth) matter, and how to right-size VMs.
- **For Solution Designers**: Recognizing the Grid → Cloud evolution justifies the architectural choice of burst-to-cloud, hybrid HPC, and federated multi-cloud strategies.
- **For KTU Examiners**: The *Virtualization + Cloud* linkage is the single most-asked concept in Module 2 of PEADT746 — expect it in 2-mark, 7-mark, and 14-mark question variants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Grid Computing

**Premise (KTU 2024 Module 2 expectation):** A scientific workload exhibits a *parallel fraction* $P$ that can be distributed across $N$ grid nodes, while the remaining *serial fraction* $(1-P)$ must execute on a single coordinating node. Derive the maximum achievable speedup.

**Step 1 — Define the work.** Let the total sequential runtime on a single node be $T_1 = 1$ (normalized unit of work).

**Step 2 — Partition the work.**
- Serial work: $T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - P) \cdot T_1 = (1 - P)$.
- Parallel work: $T_{\text{parallel, single}} = P \cdot T_1 = P$.

**Step 3 — Distribute the parallel work across $N$ identical nodes.**
Assuming perfect load balancing and negligible communication, each node performs a share of the parallel work:

$$
T_{\text{parallel, N nodes}} = \frac{P}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the serial + parallel components.**

$$
T_N = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel, N nodes}} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the speedup as the ratio of single-node time to $N$-node time.**

$$
S_N = \frac{T_1}{T_N} = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit as $N \rightarrow \infty$ (infinite grid).**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S_N = \frac{1}{1 - P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation (for the answer sheet):** Even with an *infinite* grid, the speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$. For a workload that is 90% parallel ($P = 0.9$), the theoretical maximum speedup is **10×**, not infinity. This is precisely why identifying and minimizing serial bottlenecks is a core challenge in grid computing.

**Step 7 — Numerical Example.**
Let $P = 0.95$ and $N = 100$ nodes. Then:

$$
S_{100} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{100}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0095} = \frac{1}{0.0595} \approx 16.81
$$

The **parallel efficiency** is:

$$
E = \frac{S_{100}}{N} = \frac{16.81}{100} = 0.1681 \; (\approx 16.8\%)
$$

This illustrates a key grid-design insight: adding the 100th node to an already-saturated workload yields diminishing returns.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cloud Availability from a 3-Tier VM Stack

**Premise:** A cloud customer's IaaS VM is composed of three independent components, each with its own availability:
- Physical host hardware: $A_h = 0.999$ (three nines).
- Hypervisor software: $A_v = 0.9999$.
- Network fabric: $A_n = 0.9995$.

Assuming failures are independent, the **end-to-end availability** of the VM is the product of component availabilities.

**Step 1 — Express availability as a fraction of uptime.**

$$
A_{\text{VM}} = A_h \times A_v \times A_n
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
A_{\text{VM}} = 0.999 \times 0.9999 \times 0.9995
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$
A_{\text{VM}} = 0.99840055
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to annual downtime.**

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - A_{\text{VM}}) \times 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = 0.00159945 \times 525{,}600 \approx 840.65 \text{ minutes} \approx 14.01 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even when each tier has "three-nines" or better, the **multiplicative effect** of cascading them drops end-to-end availability to ~**99.84%** (two nines nine). This is the mathematical reason cloud providers build **redundancy at every tier** (e.g., live migration, multi-AZ replication, redundant hypervisors).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: VM Consolidation Ratio from Real Workload Statistics

**Premise:** A data center has $N_{PM} = 50$ physical servers. Each server has 32 physical CPU cores and 256 GB RAM. Historically, each server's average utilization is **15%** of CPU and **30%** of RAM. After virtualization, the target is to push effective utilization to **75%** of CPU and **80%** of RAM.

**Step 1 — Compute the under-utilized physical resources.**

$$
\text{Total vCPU available} = 50 \times 32 = 1600 \text{ cores}
$$

$$
\text{Total vRAM available} = 50 \times 256 = 12{,}800 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual consumed resource pool.**

$$
\text{Consumed vCPU} = 1600 \times 0.15 = 240 \text{ cores}
$$

$$
\text{Consumed vRAM} = 12{,}800 \times 0.30 = 3840 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new capacity needed to host the same workload at higher utilization.**

$$
N_{\text{new PM, CPU-bound}} = \frac{240}{32 \times 0.75} = \frac{240}{24} = 10 \text{ servers}
$$

$$
N_{\text{new PM, RAM-bound}} = \frac{3840}{256 \times 0.80} = \frac{3840}{204.8} \approx 18.75 \rightarrow 19 \text{ servers}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the binding constraint.**

The **RAM constraint** binds (19 servers needed), so we provision 19 servers.

**Step 5 — Compute the consolidation ratio.**

$$
\text{Consolidation Ratio} = \frac{N_{PM, \text{original}}}{N_{PM, \text{new}}} = \frac{50}{19} \approx 2.63
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Takeaway:** Always check **both** CPU and RAM constraints. The binding constraint is whichever demands *more* physical servers. A common error is to over-allocate CPU while starving RAM.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Grid vs. Cluster vs. Cloud vs. Virtualization

| Dimension | **Grid Computing** | **Cluster Computing** | **Cloud Computing** | **Virtualization Layer** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Scope** | Wide-area, cross-organizational | Single rack or data center | Global, multi-region | Single physical host (or cluster) |
| **Resource Ownership** | Federated, multi-admin | Single admin/owner | Single provider, multi-tenant | Owned by whoever runs the hypervisor |
| **Workload Coupling** | Tightly coupled scientific | Tightly coupled HPC or HA | Loosely coupled, elastic services | N/A — substrate only |
| **Primary Goal** | Aggregate compute for one problem | High throughput / HA for one org | Service delivery with metering | Resource abstraction & isolation |
| **Provisioning Model** | Job submission (batch) | Reserved / static | On-demand, self-service | VM lifecycle APIs |
| **User Awareness of Resources** | Aware (explicit job scheduling) | Aware (cluster topology matters) | Abstracted away | Hidden inside the VM |
| **Typical Examples** | SETI@home, EGEE, LHC Grid | Hadoop YARN clusters, HPC | AWS, Azure, GCP | VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V |
| **Role vis-à-vis the others** | Precursor idea to Cloud | Building block of a private cloud | Productized grid + virtualization | Enabling tech for Cloud |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Hypervisor Type-1 vs. Type-2

| Property | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | **Type-2 (Hosted)** |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | Inside a host OS |
| Performance | Near-native, low overhead | Higher overhead (double scheduling) |
| Use Case | Enterprise data centers, public cloud | Desktop dev/test, education |
| Stability | High (driver-level fault isolation) | Lower (host OS crashes take VMs down) |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| Multi-tenant Production Use | **Yes** | **No** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Virtualization Architecture (Top-Down Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the request-response path from a user application down through the virtualization stack to the physical hardware. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appA[USER APPLICATION LAYER - Web Server Database ERP ML Job]
    gosA[GUEST OS LAYER - Linux Windows Server BSD]
    vresA[VIRTUAL HARDWARE LAYER - vCPU vRAM vDisk vNIC]
    hypA[HYPERVISOR VMM LAYER - CPU Scheduler Memory Mgmt I O Emulation Live Migration]
    drvA[DEVICE DRIVERS AND FIRMWARE LAYER - NIC Driver Disk Controller BIOS UEFI]
    phyA[PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER - CPU Sockets RAM DIMMs SSD NVMe 10GbE NIC]

    appA --> gosA
    gosA --> vresA
    vresA --> hypA
    hypA --> drvA
    drvA --> phyA

    classDef appLayer fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef gosLayer fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef vresLayer fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef hypLayer fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef drvLayer fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#4C1D95
    classDef phyLayer fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D

    class appA appLayer
    class gosA gosLayer
    class vresA vresLayer
    class hypA hypLayer
    class drvA drvLayer
    class phyA phyLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show the *direction of resource requests and control*. The application believes it is talking to real hardware; in reality, the hypervisor intercepts and emulates every privileged instruction.

---

### 4.2 Grid Computing Five-Layer Architecture (Foster's Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appL1[APPLICATION LAYER - Scientific Simulation Weather Modeling Genomics]
    colL1[COLLECTIVE LAYER - Workload Scheduling Data Replication Resource Brokering]
    resL1[RESOURCE LAYER - Resource Discovery Monitoring Accounting]
    conL1[CONNECTIVITY LAYER - TCP IP HTTP SOAP Security Identity]
    fabL1[FABRIC LAYER - Physical CPUs Storage Sensors Instruments]

    appL1 --> colL1
    colL1 --> resL1
    resL1 --> conL1
    conL1 --> fabL1

    classDef appGrid fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef colGrid fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef resGrid fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef conGrid fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef fabGrid fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D

    class appL1 appGrid
    class colL1 colGrid
    class resL1 resGrid
    class conL1 conGrid
    class fabL1 fabGrid
```

---

### 4.3 Virtualization-Enabled Cloud Delivery Model (Block Diagram)

This schematic shows how a single physical data center, through virtualization, simultaneously serves all three cloud service models.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    saasT[SaaS TENANT - Email CRM Office Productivity]
    paasT[PaaS TENANT - Application Runtime Middleware Database Service]
    iaasT[IaaS TENANT - Customer OS Custom Application Stack]

    hyperC[HYPERVISOR CLUSTER - VMware ESXi KVM Hyper-V Xen]
    netV[VIRTUAL NETWORK FABRIC - vSwitch VLAN Overlay VXLAN SDN Controller]
    storV[VIRTUAL STORAGE FABRIC - SAN LUN NFS Object Store Ceph]

    physC[PHYSICAL HARDWARE POOL - Rack Servers TOR Switches Storage Arrays PDU UPS]

    saasT --> hyperC
    paasT --> hyperC
    iaasT --> hyperC

    hyperC --> netV
    hyperC --> storV
    hyperC --> physC

    classDef saasCol fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef paasCol fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E1B4B
    classDef iaasCol fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef hyperCol fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef netCol fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef storCol fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#5B21B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#4C1D95
    classDef physCol fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F1D1D

    class saasT saasCol
    class paasT paasCol
    class iaasT iaasCol
    class hyperC hyperCol
    class netV netCol
    class storV storCol
    class physC physCol
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice how a single hypervisor cluster concurrently hosts SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS tenants. This **multi-tenancy via virtualization** is the *defining capability* that distinguishes a cloud from a traditional hosting provider.

---

### 4.4 Virtualization-to-Cloud Characteristic Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vmEngine[VIRTUALIZATION ENGINE - Hypervisor VM Lifecycle API]

    subgraph cloudChars[ NIST CLOUD CHARACTERISTICS ]
        c1[ON DEMAND SELF SERVICE]
        c2[BROAD NETWORK ACCESS]
        c3[RESOURCE POOLING]
        c4[RAPID ELASTICITY]
        c5[MEASURED SERVICE]
    end

    vmEngine --> c1
    vmEngine --> c2
    vmEngine --> c3
    vmEngine --> c4
    vmEngine --> c5

    classDef engineNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef charNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D

    class vmEngine engineNode
    class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5 charNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each NIST cloud characteristic is *realized* by a specific virtualization capability. Memorize this one-to-one mapping — it is one of the highest-frequency 7-mark questions in KTU Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Define virtualization. Differentiate between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Scoring Format):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Virtualization is the technique of creating logical (virtual) representations of physical computing resources — such as servers, storage, and networks — such that multiple isolated execution environments can share a single physical infrastructure, managed by a software layer called a *hypervisor* (VMM).

**Type-1 Hypervisor (1 mark):** A bare-metal hypervisor installed *directly* on the physical hardware, with no host operating system in between. It directly controls the hardware and hosts guest VMs. **Examples:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V Server, Citrix XenServer, KVM.

**Type-2 Hypervisor (1 mark):** A hosted hypervisor installed as an *application* within a conventional host operating system. The host OS mediates all hardware access, adding extra scheduling overhead. **Examples:** Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop.

**Key Difference:** Type-1 is production-grade with near-native performance and is used in data centers; Type-2 is for development, testing, and desktop use.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*Briefly explain the relationship between Grid Computing and Cloud Computing. Why is virtualization considered a prerequisite for cloud delivery?*

**Model Answer:**

**Relationship (1.5 marks):** Grid computing is the *conceptual predecessor* of cloud computing. Both pool distributed resources, but a Grid is designed to **solve a single large problem** across federated, heterogeneous resources (e.g., scientific HPC), whereas a Cloud is designed to **deliver standardized services** to many independent users with on-demand elasticity, self-service provisioning, and pay-per-use metering. Cloud essentially *productized* and *industrialized* the resource-sharing ideas of the Grid.

**Virtualization as Prerequisite (1.5 marks):** Virtualization is the technology that **physically realizes** the abstract characteristics of cloud. Without virtualization, the cloud provider could not:
- Provision a new customer's environment in seconds (requires VM cloning/instantiation).
- Isolate multiple tenants on shared hardware (requires VM isolation).
- Elastically scale workloads up or down (requires live migration and hot-add).
- Meter per-VM resource consumption (requires hypervisor-level instrumentation).

Hence, every public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP) is built on a virtualization foundation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the foundational concepts of virtualization in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the layered architecture of a virtualized environment, identifying the role of the hypervisor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the Grid Computing architecture as proposed by Ian Foster. Explain its five layers with their functions. Compare and contrast Grid Computing with Cloud Computing in a tabular form (at least 6 points). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Virtualization is the abstraction of physical computing resources into logical, software-defined equivalents. A single physical machine can be partitioned into multiple, isolated virtual machines (VMs), each running its own OS and applications as if it possessed the hardware exclusively.

**Step 2 — Core Concepts (3 marks):**
- **Host Machine**: The physical server (CPU, RAM, storage, NIC) that provides the underlying resources.
- **Guest Machine (VM)**: An isolated software container with virtualized hardware (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC).
- **Hypervisor (VMM)**: The software layer that creates, runs, schedules, and manages VMs. It performs CPU time-slicing, memory mapping (e.g., Extended Page Tables / EPT), I/O device emulation, and VM lifecycle operations (start, stop, pause, snapshot, migrate).
- **VM Image**: A file-based template (e.g., VMDK, QCOW2, VHD) used to instantiate new VMs rapidly.
- **Live Migration**: Moving a running VM from one host to another without service interruption, used for load balancing and hardware maintenance.

**Step 3 — Layered Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**
[Use the diagram from Section 4.1 — Application → Guest OS → Virtual Hardware → Hypervisor → Device Drivers → Physical Hardware.]

**Step 4 — Why It Matters (1 mark):**
This layered design enables hardware consolidation, multi-tenancy, fault isolation, rapid provisioning, and dynamic workload migration — the very foundation of modern cloud computing.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Foster's Five-Layer Grid Architecture (3.5 marks):**

1. **Fabric Layer**: The physical resources — CPUs, storage systems, sensors, scientific instruments, network links. Provides the raw capability.
2. **Connectivity Layer**: The communication substrate — TCP/IP, HTTP, SOAP/REST, plus authentication and security protocols (e.g., PKI, Kerberos, GSI).
3. **Resource Layer**: Information protocols for resource discovery (e.g., MDS, LDAP), monitoring, and accounting. Answers the question: *What resources exist and what is their state?*
4. **Collective Layer**: Coordinates multiple resources — job scheduling, data replication, work-unit distribution, fault tolerance. Examples: Condor, Globus Toolkit.
5. **Application Layer**: The user-facing scientific or business application that decomposes a problem into work units and submits them to the grid (e.g., climate modeling, bioinformatics).

**Step 2 — Grid vs Cloud Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Dimension | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Aggregate compute to solve a problem | Deliver a service on demand |
| **Workload Type** | Tightly coupled, parallel scientific | Loosely coupled, multi-tenant business |
| **User Pool** | Pre-approved research collaborators | Public, anyone with a credit card |
| **Provisioning** | Job submission via scheduler | Self-service portal / API |
| **Virtualization** | Optional, often minimal | Foundational, ubiquitous |
| **Pricing Model** | Grant-funded / institutional | Pay-per-use / subscription |
| **Geographic Scope** | Cross-organizational federation | Single provider, multi-region |
| **Example** | CERN LHC Computing Grid, SETI@home | AWS, Azure, GCP |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain how virtualization enables the three cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Discuss the role of the hypervisor in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A data center has **40 physical servers**, each with **16 CPU cores** and **128 GB RAM**. Currently, average utilization is **12% CPU** and **25% RAM**. After virtualization, the target utilization is **70% CPU** and **75% RAM**. Compute the consolidation ratio and the number of physical servers required. Also, explain two key benefits and one major challenge of server virtualization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — The Common Substrate (1.5 marks):**
All three service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — share a common virtualization substrate: a hypervisor running on physical hardware, providing the ability to instantiate and manage multiple isolated execution environments on demand. The *user-facing abstraction* differs by model.

**Step 2 — IaaS and the Hypervisor (2 marks):**
- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)**: The hypervisor provides raw VMs (vCPU + vRAM + vDisk + vNIC) to the customer. The customer installs their own OS, middleware, and applications.
- **Hypervisor role**: Full VM lifecycle — provisioning, resizing, snapshotting, live migration.
- **Examples**: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine.
- **Diagram reference**: Section 4.3 — VM cluster exposed directly to IaaS tenant.

**Step 3 — PaaS and the Hypervisor (1.5 marks):**
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)**: The cloud provider pre-installs a guest OS, runtime (e.g., Node.js, JVM, Python), and middleware inside the VM. The customer only deploys their application code.
- **Hypervisor role**: Still provides the VM, but the customer has a *higher-level abstraction* (the platform API). The hypervisor is invisible to the application developer.
- **Examples**: Google App Engine, Azure App Service, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.

**Step 4 — SaaS and the Hypervisor (1 mark):**
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)**: The customer accesses a finished application (e.g., Gmail, Office 365). Virtualization is *entirely transparent* — the customer does not see VMs, OS, or middleware.
- **Hypervisor role**: Internal to the provider — used to scale the application fleet elastically, isolate tenant data, and ensure uptime SLAs.
- **Examples**: Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Dropbox.

**Step 5 — Summary Insight (1 mark):**
The deeper the service model in the stack (toward SaaS), the *more* the hypervisor is hidden from the user, but its role in delivering elasticity and isolation becomes *more*, not less, critical.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Given Data (0.5 marks):**
- Number of physical servers: $N_{PM} = 40$
- CPU cores per server: $16$
- RAM per server: $128$ GB
- Current CPU utilization: $U_{CPU, \text{old}} = 0.12$
- Current RAM utilization: $U_{RAM, \text{old}} = 0.25$
- Target CPU utilization: $U_{CPU, \text{new}} = 0.70$
- Target RAM utilization: $U_{RAM, \text{new}} = 0.75$

**Step 2 — Total Physical Resources (0.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Total Cores} = 40 \times 16 = 640 \text{ cores}
$$

$$
\text{Total RAM} = 40 \times 128 = 5120 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Consumed Resources (Effective Workload) (0.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Consumed Cores} = 640 \times 0.12 = 76.8 \text{ cores}
$$

$$
\text{Consumed RAM} = 5120 \times 0.25 = 1280 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Servers Required Under New Utilization (1.5 marks):**

CPU-bound calculation:

$$
N_{\text{new, CPU}} = \frac{76.8}{16 \times 0.70} = \frac{76.8}{11.2} \approx 6.86 \rightarrow 7 \text{ servers}
$$

RAM-bound calculation:

$$
N_{\text{new, RAM}} = \frac{1280}{128 \times 0.75} = \frac{1280}{96} \approx 13.33 \rightarrow 14 \text{ servers}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Binding Constraint (0.5 marks):**
The **RAM** constraint binds (14 servers needed > 7 servers by CPU). Hence the new requirement is **14 physical servers**.

**Step 6 — Compute the Consolidation Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Consolidation Ratio} = \frac{40}{14} \approx 2.86 : 1
$$

**Step 7 — Two Key Benefits of Server Virtualization (1.5 marks):**
1. **Higher Hardware Utilization & Lower TCO**: Idle resources are eliminated, reducing the number of physical servers, power, cooling, and rack-space costs. TCO reductions of **40–80%** are common.
2. **Rapid Provisioning & Agility**: New VMs can be cloned from templates in minutes rather than provisioning physical hardware over days/weeks. Live migration enables zero-downtime hardware maintenance.

**Step 8 — One Major Challenge (1 mark):**
**Resource Contention and "Noisy Neighbor" Effect**: When multiple VMs share the same physical host, an aggressive workload in one VM (the *noisy neighbor*) can starve other VMs of CPU, RAM, disk I/O, or network bandwidth. Mitigation requires careful VM sizing, resource pools, QoS policies (e.g., shares, limits, reservations), and monitoring of metrics such as vCPU *ready time* and *ballooning*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Grid and Cloud**: Many students write "Grid is the same as Cloud" — *incorrect*. Grid is *problem-oriented* (HPC / scientific); Cloud is *service-oriented* (elastic, multi-tenant, metered). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this conflation.
>
> 2. **Confusing Hypervisor Types**: Students often mis-attribute VirtualBox as a Type-1 hypervisor because it is powerful. **Type-1 = bare-metal, no host OS in between**. Type-2 = runs *inside* a host OS. Memorize the example pairs: ESXi/KVM (Type-1), VirtualBox/VMware Workstation (Type-2).
>
> 3. **Ignoring the Binding Constraint in VM Sizing**: Always compute BOTH the CPU-bound and the RAM-bound requirement. The larger of the two is the answer. Failing to do this is a **1.5 mark** deduction in numerical problems.
>
> 4. **Omitting Units and Showing No Work**: KTU examiners require explicit substitution and unit mention. A bare final number with no derivation earns *partial* credit only.
>
> 5. **Writing "Virtualization = Cloud Computing"**: This is the most frequent conceptual error. Virtualization is a *technology*; Cloud is a *service delivery model* that *uses* virtualization. The relationship is *enabling*, not *equivalence*.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Live Migration / Multi-tenancy Angle**: When asked "how does virtualization enable cloud?", at least **two** of: *on-demand provisioning*, *multi-tenancy isolation*, *elastic scaling*, *live migration*, and *metering* must be mentioned to score full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Virtualization: One-Page Rapid Recap**

- **Virtualization** = logical abstraction of physical resources, managed by a **hypervisor (VMM)**.
- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor** = runs directly on hardware; production-grade; ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, Xen.
- **Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor** = runs as an app inside a host OS; desktop/dev use; VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.
- A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is an isolated software container with vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC running its own Guest OS.
- **Live Migration** = moving a running VM between hosts with zero downtime.
- **Over-commitment** = allocating more virtual resources than physical, exploiting statistical multiplexing (typical ratios 2:1 to 6:1).
- **Grid Computing** = Federated, geographically dispersed, heterogeneous resources cooperating to solve a single problem; Foster's 5 layers: Fabric → Connectivity → Resource → Collective → Application.
- **Cloud Computing** = NIST-defined on-demand, multi-tenant, elastic, metered service delivery model.
- **The Five NIST Cloud Characteristics**: On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service — *all* enabled by virtualization.
- **The Three Service Models**: IaaS (raw VMs) → PaaS (runtime + middleware) → SaaS (finished application). Virtualization is *most visible* in IaaS and *most hidden* (but most leveraged) in SaaS.
- **Consolidation Ratio Formula**: $N_{PM,\text{old}} \div N_{PM,\text{new}}$. Always check both CPU-bound and RAM-bound.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S_N = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \frac{P}{N}}$; maximum theoretical speedup as $N \to \infty$ is $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR); component availabilities **multiply**, not add.
- **Key Distinction (Board-Exam Gold)**: Virtualization is a **technology**; Cloud is a **delivery model** that *uses* virtualization; Grid is a **precursor paradigm** that *inspires* the cloud's resource-sharing ethos.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Storage and Cloud Computing - Data Storage, Cloud Storage, Cloud Storage from LANs to WANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Storage and Cloud Computing - Data Storage, Cloud Storage, Cloud Storage from WANs to LANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Storage — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Storage** in the context of cloud computing refers to the persistent retention of digital information using physical or virtual storage media, managed through hardware (HDDs, SSDs, tape), software (file systems, databases), and networking protocols such that data remains available, durable, recoverable, and secure across the compute infrastructure of an enterprise or cloud environment.

From the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cloud Computing syllabus perspective**, data storage is treated as a foundational pillar of the **Cloud Stack** — without scalable, durable, and elastic storage, the promises of IaaS/PaaS/SaaS cannot be delivered. Storage is what makes the cloud *stateful*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Warehouse vs. Personal Locker" Model

Imagine you are a small shopkeeper in Kerala:
- **Personal Locker (DAS / Local Disk)**: You keep your money in a small drawer next to your cash register. Fast, but if the shop burns down, everything is lost. *(This is Direct Attached Storage.)*
- **Office Almirah (NAS)**: You rent a steel almirah in the same building. Multiple shopkeepers can share it over an internal corridor (LAN). Safer, but still in one building.
- **Bank Vault (SAN)**: A dedicated, high-security vault room is built and connected to your shop via a private underground tunnel (Fibre Channel). High speed, very safe.
- **Public Bank Locker (Cloud Storage)**: You rent a locker in a bank branch across the city, accessed over the public road (Internet WAN). You do not know *which* vault holds your locker — the bank manages that abstraction. You can access it from any branch in the world.

This is exactly how storage has evolved from **local → LAN → SAN → Cloud (WAN)**.

---

## 1.2 Cloud Storage — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage** is a service model in which digital data is stored, managed, and backed up remotely on a multi-tenant pool of network-accessible storage infrastructure, owned and operated by a Cloud Service Provider (CSP), and delivered to consumers over a public or private network (typically the Internet / WAN) on a metered, elastic, pay-per-use basis.

### Key Properties of Cloud Storage (Must Memorize for KTU)

1. **Elasticity** — capacity scales up/down on demand.
2. **Durability** — typically **99.999999999 % (11 nines)** for object stores (e.g., AWS S3 Standard).
3. **Availability** — typically **99.9 % to 99.99 %** SLA.
4. **Multi-tenancy** — multiple users share the same physical substrate.
5. **Pay-per-use** — billed per GB stored + per I/O operation + per GB transferred.
6. **Geographic Distribution** — replicated across **Availability Zones (AZs)** and **Regions**.
7. **Self-service provisioning** — no human operator needed to allocate a "disk."

---

## 1.3 Cloud Storage from LANs to WANs — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **"From LANs to WANs"** in the KTU 2024 syllabus refers to the **historical and architectural evolution** of how storage is networked — beginning with **Local Area Network (LAN) attached file servers**, progressing through **Storage Area Networks (SAN)** and **Network Attached Storage (NAS)** in campus/enterprise networks, and finally converging into the **Wide Area Network (WAN)/Internet-scale Cloud Storage** we use today (S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage).

The journey is essentially a story of **decreasing coupling between data and physical location**.

### The Evolution Timeline (KTU High-Yield)

| Era | Topology | Range | Typical Protocol | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1980s | **DAS** (Direct Attached) | 0 m (inside CPU) | ATA, SCSI | Internal HDD |
| 1990s | **NAS** over LAN | 0–2 km | NFS, SMB/CIFS | Office file server |
| Late 1990s | **SAN** | 0–10 km (campus) | Fibre Channel, iSCSI | Enterprise datacenter |
| 2000s | **IP-SAN / WAN SAN** | Metro / National | FCIP, iFCP | Disaster-recovery site |
| 2006+ | **Cloud Storage over WAN** | Global (Internet) | REST/HTTPS, S3 API | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS |
| 2015+ | **Edge / CDN** | Global, last-mile | HTTP/3, QUIC | Cloudflare R2, AWS S3+CF |

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Summary of Three Core Ideas

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this triplet — it appears almost verbatim in KTU Module 2 questions:**
> 1. **Data Storage** = the *physical + logical* substrate holding bits.
> 2. **Cloud Storage** = storage *delivered as a service* over a network.
> 3. **LANs → WANs** = the *networking evolution* that made cloud storage possible by extending the reach of storage from a single room to the entire planet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Storage Hierarchy (The "Pyramid" You Must Draw)

Storage is arranged in a hierarchy where **speed, cost-per-GB, and capacity** trade off. From top (fastest, smallest, most expensive) to bottom (slowest, largest, cheapest):

1. **CPU Registers** — ~ 1 ns, bytes
2. **CPU Cache (L1/L2/L3)** — ~ 1–10 ns, KB–MB
3. **Main Memory (RAM)** — ~ 100 ns, GB
4. **SSD (NVMe)** — ~ 50–150 µs, TB
5. **HDD (Spinning Disk)** — ~ 5–10 ms, TB
6. **Network Attached Storage / SAN** — ~ 1–10 ms, PB
7. **Cloud Object Storage** — ~ 50–200 ms (WAN RTT), EB
8. **Cold / Archival Tape** — seconds to minutes, EB

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question asks "Where should data X be stored?", your answer must justify using this hierarchy. Hot data → RAM/SSD; warm data → HDD/SAN; cold data → S3 Glacier / Azure Archive.

---

## 2.2 Three Traditional Storage Architectures (Before Cloud)

### (a) Direct Attached Storage (DAS)
- Storage device is physically attached to the server (cable or internal bus).
- Examples: internal HDD, USB drive, internal RAID.
- **Pros:** Cheap, low latency, simple.
- **Cons:** Cannot be shared; no central management; tied to one server.

### (b) Network Attached Storage (NAS)
- A **file-level** storage server connected to a LAN.
- Uses file-sharing protocols such as **NFS** (UNIX/Linux) and **SMB/CIFS** (Windows).
- Appears to clients as a "shared folder."
- **Pros:** Cheap, easy, multi-OS.
- **Cons:** Limited throughput (constrained by LAN), file-level granularity only.

### (c) Storage Area Network (SAN)
- A **dedicated, high-speed** network of disks using **block-level** access.
- Protocols: **Fibre Channel (FC)**, **iSCSI** (SCSI over TCP/IP), **FCoE**.
- The server sees the remote disk as if it were local.
- **Pros:** Very high throughput (8/16/32 Gbps FC), low latency, block-level.
- **Cons:** Expensive, complex, requires specialized switches/ HBAs.

### (d) Object Storage (Cloud-Native)
- Data is stored as **objects** inside **buckets**, identified by a unique key/URL.
- Accessed via **RESTful HTTP APIs** (e.g., S3 API: `PUT /bucket/key`).
- **Pros:** Virtually unlimited scale, metadata-rich, HTTP-based, geo-replicated.
- **Cons:** Higher latency than block storage, no random block writes, eventually consistent.

---

## 2.3 Why Cloud Storage Wins: The Five Pillars

1. **Elasticity** — Capacity grows/shrinks automatically (no procurement delays).
2. **Pay-per-use** — Pay only for the GB-months and I/Os you consume.
3. **Durability via replication** — Data is replicated across **≥ 3 AZs**; object stores offer **11 nines** durability.
4. **Global accessibility** — A bucket in `us-east-1` is reachable from Kerala in ~250 ms.
5. **Managed services** — No patching of OS, no RAID rebuilds, no disk replacement.

---

## 2.4 Cloud Storage Service Models (Storage-as-a-Service Hierarchy)

> [!TIP]
> This is a **direct KTU 2024 syllabus mapping** — learn the four layers top-to-bottom.

| Layer | What you manage | What CSP manages | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw Infrastructure** (IaaS storage) | OS, filesystem, app | Disks, replication | AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks, GCP Persistent Disk |
| **Storage-as-a-Service** (object/file/block) | App, data, permissions | Everything else | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS, Azure Files |
| **Database-as-a-Service** | Schema, queries | Patching, backups | Amazon RDS, Azure SQL, DynamoDB |
| **Application-level storage** | Nothing | Entire app + data | Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive |

---

## 2.5 Deployment Models of Cloud Storage

- **Public Cloud Storage** — owned by hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP), shared by all tenants.
- **Private Cloud Storage** — owned by a single org on-premises (e.g., OpenStack Swift).
- **Hybrid Cloud Storage** — on-prem + public, with bursting/archival to cloud (e.g., AWS Storage Gateway).
- **Community Cloud Storage** — shared by orgs with a common concern (e.g., government health records).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are fair game for KTU 14-mark numerical/derivation questions.

### (i) Storage Capacity Formulas (RAID)

For a RAID group with $N$ disks each of capacity $C$ (in bytes):

$$
\text{Usable Capacity} = (N - P) \times C
$$

where $P$ is the number of disks "lost" to parity/mirroring.

| RAID Level | $P$ | Min disks | Fault tolerance | Usable capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RAID 0 | 0 | 2 | 0 disks | $N \cdot C$ |
| RAID 1 (Mirror) | $N/2$ | 2 | $N/2$ disks | $(N/2) \cdot C$ |
| RAID 5 | 1 | 3 | 1 disk | $(N-1) \cdot C$ |
| RAID 6 | 2 | 4 | 2 disks | $(N-2) \cdot C$ |
| RAID 10 (1+0) | $N/2$ | 4 | 1 per mirror | $(N/2) \cdot C$ |

### (ii) Availability & Durability Metrics (Cloud SLAs)

**Availability** is typically expressed as a percentage of *uptime* over a year:

$$
\text{Annual Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \quad \text{[minutes]}
$$

For example, **99.9 %** availability $\Rightarrow$ 525.6 minutes/year (≈ 8.76 hours) of allowed downtime.

**Durability** is the probability that data is *not* lost over a year, expressed in "nines":

$$
\text{Annual Loss Probability} = 1 - D
$$

where $D$ is durability. For 11 nines:

$$
1 - 0.99999999999 = 1 \times 10^{-11}
$$

i.e., you could lose **one object in 10,000 years** for every 10 million objects stored.

### (iii) Network Bandwidth–Delay Product (Storage over WAN)

When moving data over a WAN link of bandwidth $B$ (bits/s) and round-trip time $RTT$ (seconds), the **bandwidth–delay product** tells you how much data is "in flight":

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

This is critical for tuning TCP window size when streaming data from on-prem to S3, Azure, etc.

### (iv) Replication Factor & Effective Durability

If you store an object with replication factor $r$ across $r$ independent failure domains (e.g., AZs), the per-domain failure probability is $p$, and assuming independence:

$$
D_{\text{object}} = 1 - p^{r}
$$

For $p = 0.01$ (1 % per-AZ loss probability) and $r = 3$:

$$
D = 1 - (0.01)^{3} = 1 - 10^{-6} = 0.999999
$$

i.e., **6 nines** with triple-replication across AZs.

### (v) Storage Cost (TCO over $T$ years)

$$
\text{TCO} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( C_{\text{store}} \cdot S_t + C_{\text{IO}} \cdot I_t + C_{\text{xfer}} \cdot X_t \right)
$$

where $S_t$ is GB stored in year $t$, $I_t$ is I/O operations, $X_t$ is GB transferred out. Egress ($X_t$) is usually the dominant cost — a classic KTU pitfall.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of This Theory

- **Data engineers** size EBS volumes using RAID formulas.
- **Solution architects** calculate SLA-based downtime budgets for multi-region deployments.
- **Network engineers** tune TCP windows using BDP for cloud migrations.
- **FinOps teams** model TCO to decide on-prem vs. cloud.
- **CDN/edge designers** push BDP optimization to the last mile.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **availability** (uptime, measured in %) with **durability** (data-loss probability, measured in nines). They are completely different. Examiners will specifically test this distinction.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Storage Capacity of a RAID 5 Array

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** — be ready to write every line.

**Problem statement:** Given a RAID 5 array with $N$ disks, each of size $C$ GB, derive the usable capacity and the fault-tolerance properties.

### Step 1 — Understand the parity distribution

In RAID 5, **one** disk's worth of capacity is used for **distributed parity** (not stored on a single disk, but spread across all disks in rotating stripes).

Therefore, the number of disks "lost" to parity is:

$$
P = 1
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the general capacity formula

From the formula sheet (Section 2.6):

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = (N - P) \times C = (N - 1) \cdot C
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example (which KTU loves to set)

Suppose $N = 5$ disks, each $C = 2$ TB. Then:

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = (5 - 1) \times 2 \text{ TB} = 4 \times 2 \text{ TB} = 8 \text{ TB}
$$

**Logical reasoning to write in the exam:** Out of 5 physical disks, one disk's worth of space (2 TB) is used for parity. The remaining 4 disks (8 TB) are usable for data.

### Step 4 — Fault tolerance analysis

A single disk failure can be tolerated because the missing data can be reconstructed by XOR-ing the surviving $N-1$ disks:

$$
D_{\text{reconstructed}} = D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \oplus D_4 \oplus P
$$

where $P$ is the parity stripe. This is why $P = 1$ disk is sufficient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Downtime from a 99.99 % SLA

> [!TIP]
> A staple of KTU 14-mark problems.

**Given:** A cloud storage service guarantees availability $A = 99.99 \,\%$ ("4 nines").
**Find:** Maximum permitted annual downtime in minutes and seconds.

### Step 1 — Convert percentage to fraction

$$
1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by total minutes in a year

$$
T_{\text{down}} = 0.0001 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the product

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{down}} &= 0.0001 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
&= 0.0001 \times 525600 \\
&= 52.56 \text{ minutes per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to seconds

$$
52.56 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 3153.6 \text{ seconds} \approx 52 \text{ min } 34 \text{ s}
$$

**Interpretation:** A "4-nines" service is allowed to be down for **less than 53 minutes per year** — this is why S3 Standard is priced at a premium over S3 One Zone-IA.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth–Delay Product for a WAN Backup

**Scenario:** You are uploading a 1 TB database from a KTU campus server in Thiruvananthapuram to a cloud bucket in `ap-south-1` (Mumbai). The link has $B = 100$ Mbps, and measured RTT $= 60$ ms.

**Find:** (a) BDP, (b) minimum TCP window size to fully utilize the link.

### Step 1 — Convert units

$$
B = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}, \quad \text{RTT} = 60 \text{ ms} = 0.06 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute BDP

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 0.06 = 6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to bytes

$$
6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits} \div 8 = 750{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 750 \text{ KB}
$$

### Step 4 — TCP window conclusion

The sender's TCP receive window must be **at least 750 KB** to keep the pipe full. Default Windows TCP window is often only 64 KB — a major bottleneck for cloud migrations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world fix:** Use **multi-stream uploads** (e.g., S3 multipart upload), **AWS Snowball/Snowmobile** for petabyte-scale moves, or **AWS Direct Connect** for dedicated bandwidth.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cloud Object Durability with Erasure Coding

Modern cloud object stores (Azure Blob, GCS Coldline) use **erasure coding** instead of simple replication. Suppose we encode data into $k = 10$ data chunks and $n - k = 4$ parity chunks, distributed across $n = 14$ disks.

### Step 1 — Per-disk failure probability

Assume $p = 0.001$ (99.9 % disk reliability per year).

### Step 2 — Probability of data loss

Data is lost only if **more than 4 disks** fail simultaneously. Using the binomial tail:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = \sum_{i=5}^{14} \binom{14}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{14-i}
$$

For small $p$, the dominant term is $i = 5$:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} \approx \binom{14}{5} \cdot (0.001)^{5} = 2002 \cdot 10^{-15} = 2.002 \times 10^{-12}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to durability

$$
D = 1 - P_{\text{loss}} \approx 1 - 2 \times 10^{-12} \approx 0.999999999998
$$

i.e., **~ 12 nines** durability — better than 3× replication (which gives only 6 nines)!

This is why hyperscale object stores use erasure coding, not plain replication.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DAS vs NAS vs SAN vs Object

| Attribute | DAS | NAS | SAN | Cloud Object |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topology | Direct bus | LAN | Dedicated FC / iSCSI | WAN / Internet |
| Granularity | Block | File | Block | Object (key-value) |
| Protocol | ATA, SCSI | NFS, SMB | FC, iSCSI | HTTPS, S3 API |
| Typical latency | ~ 0.1 ms | ~ 1–5 ms | ~ 0.5–2 ms | ~ 50–200 ms |
| Max practical scale | TB | ~ PB | ~ 10 PB | Exabytes |
| Cost per GB | Low | Medium | High | Pay-per-use |
| Multi-tenant | No | Limited | Possible | Native |
| KTU keyword | "Server-local" | "File server" | "Block fabric" | "Bucket + REST" |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Evolution of Storage: From DAS → LAN → SAN → Cloud (WAN)

This diagram captures the **full KTU 2024 Module 2 narrative arc** of how storage moved from a single machine to global cloud scale.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ERA1["Era 1 - 1980s - Direct Attached"]
        CPU1[Server CPU] -- ATA or SCSI Bus --> HDD1[Internal HDD or RAID]
    end

    subgraph ERA2["Era 2 - 1990s - NAS over LAN"]
        CPU2[Office Workstation A] -- NFS or SMB --> NAS1[NAS File Server]
        CPU3[Office Workstation B] -- NFS or SMB --> NAS1
        CPU4[Office Workstation C] -- NFS or SMB --> NAS1
    end

    subgraph ERA3["Era 3 - Late 1990s - SAN"]
        APP1[Application Server 1] -- FC or iSCSI --> FSW[Fibre Channel Switch]
        APP2[Application Server 2] -- FC or iSCSI --> FSW
        FSW -- Block-level --> DSK1[Disk Array 1]
        FSW -- Block-level --> DSK2[Disk Array 2]
    end

    subgraph ERA4["Era 4 - 2006 to Now - Cloud over WAN"]
        CLIENT1[Client in Kerala] -- HTTPS or REST --> EDGE[Cloud Edge or CDN]
        CLIENT2[Client in Delhi] -- HTTPS or REST --> EDGE
        EDGE -- Internal Backbone --> OBJS1[Object Store S3 or Azure Blob]
        OBJS1 -- Replicates --> AZ1[Availability Zone A]
        OBJS1 -- Replicates --> AZ2[Availability Zone B]
        OBJS1 -- Replicates --> AZ3[Availability Zone C]
    end

    HDD1 -.evolves to.-> NAS1
    NAS1 -.evolves to.-> FSW
    FSW -.evolves to.-> OBJS1

    classDef eraStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91;
    class ERA1,ERA2,ERA3,ERA4 eraStyle;
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFF,stroke:#202124,stroke-width:1px,color:#202124;
    class CPU1,HDD1,CPU2,CPU3,CPU4,NAS1,APP1,APP2,FSW,DSK1,DSK2,CLIENT1,CLIENT2,EDGE,OBJS1,AZ1,AZ2,AZ3 nodeStyle;
```

**Reading guide for the exam:**

- The **leftmost sub-graph** shows DAS — the storage is *inside* the server.
- The **second sub-graph** shows NAS — storage is a *file server* shared over a LAN.
- The **third sub-graph** shows SAN — storage is on a *separate, dedicated* high-speed network.
- The **rightmost sub-graph** shows cloud object storage — clients on the Internet (WAN) access a logical bucket that is physically replicated across multiple data centers.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Cloud Storage Service Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer - SaaS - Google Drive Dropbox OneDrive] --> L2
    L2[Data Platform Layer - DBaaS - RDS DynamoDB BigQuery] --> L3
    L3[Storage Service Layer - S3 Azure Blob GCS - Object or File or Block API] --> L4
    L4[Virtualization Layer - Hypervisor or Container Runtime] --> L5
    L5[Physical Storage Layer - HDD SSD Tape in Datacenter]

    L1 -.user manages only data.-> L1
    L2 -.user manages schema.-> L2
    L3 -.user manages buckets and keys.-> L3
    L4 -.CSP manages.-> L4
    L5 -.CSP manages.-> L5

    classDef userLayer fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5F370E;
    classDef cspLayer fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D652D;
    class L1,L2,L3 userLayer;
    class L4,L5 cspLayer;
```

**Exam tip:** KTU questions on "Who manages what in cloud storage?" expect you to draw this exact layered diagram and label the boundary between **user responsibility** and **CSP responsibility**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hybrid Cloud Storage with Caching Tier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP[Enterprise Application] -- Hot Data --> LSSD[Local SSD Cache Tier]
    APP -- Warm Data --> NAS2[On-Premises NAS or SAN]
    APP -- Cold Data --> CLD[(Public Cloud Object Store - S3 Glacier)]
    NAS2 -- Archive via WAN --> CLD
    CLD -- Replicates --> REG2[Region 2 Backup]

    classDef hotStyle fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#A50E0E;
    classDef warmStyle fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#5F370E;
    classDef coldStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91;
    class LSSD hotStyle;
    class NAS2 warmStyle;
    class CLD,REG2 coldStyle;
```

**Why this matters for KTU:** Hybrid storage is the *practical* deployment of cloud storage in Indian enterprises — a common 14-mark question topic.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Object Storage Internals (Bucket, Key, Metadata)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BUCKET["Bucket - mycompany-documents - in ap-south-1"]
        OBJ1["Object Key - invoices-2024-001.pdf - Data 5MB - Metadata"]
        OBJ2["Object Key - photo-2024.jpg - Data 8MB - Metadata"]
        OBJ3["Object Key - log-2024-12-31.txt - Data 2MB - Metadata"]
    end

    IDX[Metadata Index Service] --- BUCKET
    IDX -.search.-> OBJ1
    IDX -.search.-> OBJ2
    IDX -.search.-> OBJ3

    REPL[Cross-Region Replication] --- BUCKET
    REPL -.async copy.-> DR[(DR Bucket in ap-south-2)]
```

**Exam takeaway:** An "object" in cloud storage is **not just bytes** — it is the tuple **(key, value, metadata, version ID)**. This distinguishes object storage from raw block or file storage.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and expects a crisp, 3-4 sentence answer plus a diagram/table if relevant.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEADT746]

**Differentiate between Direct Attached Storage (DAS), Network Attached Storage (NAS), and Storage Area Network (SAN). Mention the typical protocol used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DAS | NAS | SAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coupling | Tight (internal bus) | Loose (LAN) | Loose (dedicated network) |
| Granularity | Block | File | Block |
| Protocol | ATA, SCSI | NFS, SMB/CIFS | Fibre Channel, iSCSI |
| Access scope | Single server | Many clients on LAN | Many servers on dedicated fabric |
| Typical use | OS disk | Office shares | Databases, VMs |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

DAS is the oldest, simplest, and lowest-latency option. NAS operates at file level over a LAN, making it ideal for shared folders. SAN provides block-level access over a high-speed dedicated fabric, making it the choice for performance-critical workloads like databases. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEADT746]

**List and briefly explain any three characteristics of cloud storage that distinguish it from traditional enterprise storage.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Elasticity** — capacity scales up/down automatically to match demand, unlike a fixed-capacity SAN.
2. **Pay-per-use billing** — costs are metered per GB-month, per I/O, and per GB transferred out, replacing large CapEx with OpEx.
3. **Geographic durability via replication** — data is automatically replicated across multiple geographically separated data centers, achieving 11-nines durability in object stores like S3.
4. **Multi-tenancy with logical isolation** — many customers share the same physical infrastructure, isolated via software-defined policies.

*(Any 3 characteristics × 1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Within a module, students answer **one** of two alternative 14-mark questions. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)**. We provide both alternatives below so you can practice both.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks  `[KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the evolution of cloud storage from LAN-based architectures to WAN-based architectures. With a neat diagram, describe how storage migrated from Direct Attached Storage (DAS) → Network Attached Storage (NAS) → Storage Area Network (SAN) → Cloud Object Storage over WAN.

**Model Solution:**

1. **DAS Era (1980s):** Storage was directly attached to a server via ATA or SCSI buses. Each server had its own disks. Limitation: no sharing, no centralization, no failover across machines.

2. **NAS Era (1990s):** A dedicated file server was added to the LAN, exposing shared folders via NFS (UNIX) and SMB/CIFS (Windows). Many office PCs could now read/write the same files. Limitation: limited by LAN bandwidth; only file-level (not block-level) access.
