3. **SAN Era (late 1990s):** To support high-performance workloads (databases, mail servers), a dedicated, lossless network — Fibre Channel or iSCSI over Gigabit Ethernet — was deployed. Servers accessed remote LUNs as if they were local disks. Limitation: expensive, complex, geographically limited to a campus or metro.

4. **Cloud/WAN Era (2006 onwards):** With broadband Internet and hyperscale data centers, storage moved onto the public WAN. Providers like AWS (S3, 2006), Azure (Blob, 2010), and Google Cloud Storage abstracted the physical substrate and exposed data via REST/HTTPS APIs. Data is replicated across geographically separated data centers for durability. New benefits: elastic capacity, pay-per-use, global accessibility, 11-nines durability.

   *(Evolutionary narrative — 4 marks; diagram — 3 marks.)*

   **Diagram to draw (in the answer sheet):**
   ```
   [Server]--ATA/SCSI-->[Internal HDD]            (DAS)
   [PCs]--NFS/SMB-->[NAS File Server]              (NAS over LAN)
   [Servers]--FC/iSCSI-->[FC Switch]-->[Disks]     (SAN)
   [Clients]--HTTPS-->[Cloud Object Store]         (WAN / Cloud)
   ```

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the three cloud storage service models: Object Storage, Block Storage, and File Storage. For each, give one real-world AWS service as an example and a use-case.

**Model Solution:**

| Attribute | Object Storage | Block Storage | File Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data unit | Object (key + data + metadata) | Block (fixed-size sectors) | File (hierarchical) |
| Access | REST/HTTPS API | Mounted as a block device | SMB/NFS mount |
| Latency | 50–200 ms | < 1 ms | 1–10 ms |
| Scalability | Virtually unlimited | Limited per volume | Limited per share |
| AWS example | Amazon S3 | Amazon EBS | Amazon EFS / FSx |
| Ideal for | Backups, big data, media, logs | Databases, boot volumes | Lift-and-shift of legacy apps, content mgmt |

**Use-case examples (write any one for each):**
- **Object (S3):** Static website hosting, data lake for analytics, backup of VMs.
- **Block (EBS):** Boot volume of an EC2 instance running a MySQL database.
- **File (EFS):** Shared home directories for a fleet of EC2 web servers.

*(3 marks for comparison table, 4 marks for use-cases and AWS examples.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks  `[KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2 – Alternative]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the term "Cloud Storage" and discuss the four deployment models of cloud storage with one advantage and one limitation each.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Cloud storage is a model of networked, multi-tenant storage infrastructure in which digital data is stored on remote servers accessed via a network (typically the public Internet), with elastic capacity, pay-per-use billing, and high durability delivered as a managed service.

**Four Deployment Models:**

1. **Public Cloud Storage**
   - *Advantage:* Massive economies of scale, lowest per-GB cost, global availability.
   - *Limitation:* Data resides in third-party infrastructure; regulatory concerns for sensitive data (e.g., DPDP Act in India).

2. **Private Cloud Storage**
   - *Advantage:* Full control over data location, hardware, and security policies.
   - *Limitation:* High CapEx and operational overhead; benefits of hyperscale economics are lost.

3. **Hybrid Cloud Storage**
   - *Advantage:* Combines on-prem control for hot/sensitive data with cloud elasticity for cold/bursty workloads.
   - *Limitation:* Complex networking (VPN/Direct Connect), data-tiering policies, and Egress costs.

4. **Community Cloud Storage**
   - *Advantage:* Shared cost among organizations with a common regulatory or mission profile.
   - *Limitation:* Governance disputes; smaller scale than public; less mature tooling.

*(Definition — 2 marks; four deployment models — 4 marks; advantages/limitations — 1 mark.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** A startup wants to back up 500 TB of data from their on-prem datacenter in Bengaluru to AWS S3 in the `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) region. The available Internet bandwidth is 200 Mbps and the measured RTT is 40 ms. Calculate:
- (i) the bandwidth–delay product (BDP),
- (ii) the minimum TCP window size in KB,
- (iii) the **theoretical minimum time** to transfer the data, and
- (iv) suggest **two** real-world techniques to speed up the transfer.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bandwidth–Delay Product**

$$
B = 200 \text{ Mbps} = 200 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}
$$

$$
\text{RTT} = 40 \text{ ms} = 0.04 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT} = 200 \times 10^{6} \times 0.04 = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Minimum TCP window in bytes**

$$
\text{Window} = \frac{8 \times 10^{6}}{8} = 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes} = 1000 \text{ KB} \approx 1 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iii) Theoretical minimum transfer time**

Total data $= 500 \text{ TB} = 500 \times 8 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits} = 4 \times 10^{15} \text{ bits}$

$$
T_{\min} = \frac{4 \times 10^{15}}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} \approx 231.5 \text{ days}
$$

So in theory, this single-stream transfer would take over **7 months** — clearly impractical.

**(iv) Two real-world speed-up techniques:**

1. **AWS Snowball Edge (Physical Appliance):** AWS ships a rugged 80 TB storage device to the Bengaluru office; data is copied locally at gigabit speeds and shipped back to AWS. Recommended for > 10 TB transfers when network is slow.

2. **S3 Multi-Part Upload with Parallel Streams:** Split the 500 TB into millions of objects and upload them in parallel from multiple machines, saturating available uplink.

3. **AWS Direct Connect:** Establish a dedicated 1 Gbps / 10 Gbps private fiber link from the datacenter to AWS, bypassing the public Internet.

4. **S3 Transfer Acceleration:** Use CloudFront edge locations to route uploads over AWS's optimized backbone.

*(Each part — 1.5 to 2 marks; totals 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing availability with durability.** 99.99 % availability ≠ 99.99 % durability. Always state units clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units in BDP calculations.** A frequent 1-mark loss: leaving bandwidth in Mbps but expressing BDP in bytes without dividing by 8.
> 3. **Drawing a NAS diagram with a switch labelled "SAN".** Examiner *will* deduct a mark. Memorize the protocols: NFS/SMB = NAS; FC/iSCSI = SAN.
> 4. **Stating "cloud storage is unlimited"** — it is not. Object stores have per-account soft limits (e.g., 100 buckets per account in S3 by default) and hard limits per object (5 TB in S3).
> 5. **Ignoring the "From LANs to WANs" evolution** in answers. Always narrate the *transition* — that is the heart of Module 2.
> 6. **Writing "S3 stores files"** — wrong. S3 stores *objects* consisting of **key + data + metadata + version ID**. Marker's pet peeve.
> 7. **Forgetting Egress costs in TCO** — examiners will deduct if you only mention storage and ignore data transfer out.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Core Concepts
- **Data Storage** = persistent retention of digital information.
- **Cloud Storage** = storage delivered as a managed service over a network.
- **"LANs to WANs"** = the historical evolution extending storage's reach.

### Three Pre-Cloud Architectures
- **DAS** → block, internal, single server.
- **NAS** → file, NFS/SMB, LAN-shared.
- **SAN** → block, FC/iSCSI, dedicated fabric.

### Cloud-Native Storage
- **Object Storage** = S3, Azure Blob, GCS — key-value over HTTPS.
- **Block Storage** = EBS, Azure Managed Disks — mounted as a device.
- **File Storage** = EFS, Azure Files — NFS/SMB over cloud.

### Five Pillars of Cloud Storage
Elasticity, Pay-per-use, Durability, Global Access, Managed Service.

### Four Deployment Models
Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.

### Formulas to Memorize
- RAID capacity: $C_{\text{usable}} = (N - P) \cdot C$
- Annual downtime: $(1 - A) \times 525600$ minutes
- BDP: $B \times \text{RTT}$
- 11 nines durability $= 1 - 10^{-11}$ annual loss
- Triple-replication durability: $D = 1 - p^{3}$

### Key Acronyms (KTU loves them)
**DAS, NAS, SAN, FC, iSCSI, NFS, SMB, REST, API, AZ, SLA, TCO, BDP, CDN, IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, DBaaS, STaaS.**

### Common KTU Exam Patterns
- 3-mark: differentiate two storage types OR list 3 cloud storage characteristics.
- 7-mark (a): narrative + diagram of LAN → WAN evolution.
- 7-mark (b): numerical on RAID capacity OR BDP OR SLA downtime.

### One-Liner Mnemonics
- **"**D**irect = **D**rive next to CPU."
- **"N**etwork = **N**FS or SMB."
- **"**S**torage Area = **S**pecial fibre."
- **"**O**bject = **O**ver HTTP."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing Services - Cloud Computing Elements, Understanding Services and Applications by Type, Cloud Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Services - Cloud Computing Elements, Understanding Services and Applications by Type, Cloud Services ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Cloud Computing Services > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cloud Computing Services** are the on-demand, network-accessible, elastic, and metered delivery of configurable computing resources (servers, storage, networks, applications, and services) over the internet using a **pay-per-use** consumption model, as defined by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Special Publication 800-145**. Cloud services abstract the underlying physical infrastructure from the end-user, allowing individuals and enterprises to consume IT capabilities without owning, managing, or maintaining the hardware and software stack themselves. The services are delivered through three well-defined models — **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)**, **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)**, and **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** — collectively called the **SPI Model** (Software, Platform, Infrastructure). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model Imagine your home does not have its own power generator. Instead, you draw electricity from a national grid through a socket in your wall. You: - Do not care **how** the electricity is generated (coal, solar, nuclear, hydro). - Pay only for the **units (kWh)** you consume each month. - Can instantly **scale up** consumption (turn on AC) or **scale down** (switch off lights). - The provider guarantees **uptime, voltage stability, and safety** through a Service Level Agreement (SLA). > [!NOTE] > **Cloud Computing works exactly the same way.** Instead of building your own data center, you "plug into" a cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) and consume **Compute (vCPUs)**, **Storage (GB)**, and **Bandwidth (GB transferred)** on a metered basis. The provider handles fault tolerance, scaling, patching, and security. ### 1.3 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Services (NIST SP 800-145) | # | Characteristic | Definition | Metered Metric | |---|----------------|------------|----------------| | 1 | **On-Demand Self-Service** | User provisions resources automatically without human interaction | Provisioning time (seconds) | | 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Services are accessible over the network via standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST APIs) | Latency (ms) | | 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Multi-tenant model pooling physical/virtual resources dynamically | Utilization (%) | | 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities scaled out/in quickly, often automatically | Scale-out time (sec) | | 5 | **Measured Service** | Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering | Billing per unit | ### 1.4 Cloud Computing Elements — The Foundational Pillars > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Cloud Computing Elements** are the building blocks that compose a complete cloud service. Mastering these is mandatory for KTU Module 3. 1. **Clients** — The user-facing interfaces (Web browsers, Mobile apps, Thin clients, Thick clients, Terminals). 2. **Datacenter (Server Farm)** — The physical facility housing **racks of blade servers, SAN storage, top-of-rack (ToR) switches**, and environmental controls (CRAC units, UPS, generators). 3. **Distributed Servers** — Geographically dispersed servers that deliver content via load balancers and CDN (Content Delivery Network) edge nodes to minimize latency. 4. **Cloud Platforms & Virtualization Layer** — The **Hypervisor (Type-1: Xen, ESXi, KVM; Type-2: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation)** that abstracts the hardware into isolated **Virtual Machines (VMs)** or **containers (Docker, Kubernetes pods)**. ### 1.5 Understanding Services and Applications by Type > [!NOTE] > **Application by Type** refers to categorizing cloud-delivered software based on its functional class — CRM, ERP, Productivity, Communication, Analytics, DevOps, AI/ML, etc. The four primary delivery archetypes of cloud applications are: | Application Type | Description | Industry Example | |------------------|-------------|------------------| | **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** | Ready-to-use end-user applications | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce | | **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** | Application development & deployment platform | Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service | | **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** | Raw virtualized hardware resources | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine | | **FaaS (Function-as-a-Service)** | Event-driven, serverless compute units | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, GCP Cloud Functions | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Cloud Service Stack — Layered Architecture A cloud service is delivered through **logical abstraction layers**, where each upper layer consumes the services of the layer immediately below it. This is known as the **Cloud Service Stack** or **SPI Stack**. - **Layer 0 — Physical Infrastructure (Hardware)** - Raw compute servers (x86, ARM, GPUs, TPUs), storage arrays (HDD, SSD, NVMe), and networking gear (Switches, Routers, Firewalls). Owned and maintained by the cloud provider. - **Layer 1 — Virtualization & Orchestration** - The hypervisor abstracts hardware into Virtual Machines (VMs). An **orchestrator** (e.g., OpenStack, Kubernetes) automates the lifecycle of VMs, containers, and networks. - **Layer 2 — IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** - Users rent VMs, block storage (EBS, Azure Disks), virtual private clouds (VPCs), and load balancers. They control the **OS, middleware, runtime, and application**. - **Layer 3 — PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** - Provider supplies the OS, middleware (e.g., Tomcat, Node.js runtime), database (e.g., AWS RDS), and CI/CD pipeline. User only deploys **application code and data**. - **Layer 4 — SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** - Provider delivers the entire stack including the application. User simply logs in via a browser. Examples: Google Workspace, Microsoft 365. - **Layer 5 — FaaS / Serverless** - The user supplies only a stateless **function** triggered by an event. The cloud provider dynamically allocates compute, runs the function, and scales to zero when idle. ### 2.2 Cloud Deployment Models > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently ask the difference between **Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community** clouds. This is a high-weightage conceptual question. - **Public Cloud** — Owned by a third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) and delivered over the public internet. Multi-tenant. Lowest cost. - **Private Cloud** — Used exclusively by a single organization. Can be on-premises (OpenStack) or hosted by a third party (VMware Cloud on AWS). Higher security and control. - **Hybrid Cloud** — A coordinated composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (public + private) bound by standardized or proprietary technology enabling data and application portability. - **Community Cloud** — Shared by several organizations with shared concerns (e.g., security, compliance, jurisdiction). Common in healthcare and government. ### 2.3 The Shared Responsibility Model > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students often answer that "the cloud provider is responsible for all security." This is **false**. Security in the cloud is a **shared responsibility**. - **Provider's Responsibility (Security *OF* the Cloud):** Physical security of data centers, hardware, network infrastructure, hypervisor, and host OS. - **Customer's Responsibility (Security *IN* the Cloud):** Identity & Access Management (IAM), data encryption, OS patching (in IaaS), application code security, network configuration, and firewall rules. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **must-memorize formula sheet** for KTU Module 3 numerical and SLA problems. | # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Constraint | |---|---------|---------|------|------------------------| | 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{CapEx} + C_{OpEx} + C_{Migration} + C_{Downtime}$ | USD ($) | $C_{CapEx} \geq 0$; $C_{Downtime} \geq 0$ | | 2 | **Pay-Per-Use Cost** | $Cost_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)$ | USD ($) | $U_i$ = units consumed; $P_i$ = price per unit | | 3 | **Storage Cost (Tiered)** | $Cost_{storage} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} (GB_t \times Rate_t)$ | USD ($) | Tier $t$ has rate $Rate_t$ in USD/GB/month | | 4 | **Egress Data Cost** | $Cost_{egress} = D_{out} \times R_{egress}$ | USD ($) | Often **$0.09/GB** (AWS standard rate) | | 5 | **Cloud Availability (SLA)** | $Availability = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | Percent (\%) | $MTBF > 0$; $MTTR \geq 0$ | | 6 | **Downtime per Year** | $Downtime_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | Minutes/Year | $525{,}600 = 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | | 7 | **Composite SLA** | $SLA_{composite} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} SLA_i$ | Fraction (0–1) | Multiplicative; e.g., $0.999 \times 0.999 = 0.998001$ | | 8 | **Serverless Cost** | $Cost = Invocations \times Rate_{inv} + (GB\text{-}s \times Rate_{compute})$ | USD ($) | AWS Lambda: **\$0.20 per 1M requests** | | 9 | **Cost per vCPU-hour** | $Cost = N_{vCPU} \times T_{hours} \times R_{vCPU}$ | USD ($) | Typical $R_{vCPU} \approx \$0.04$/hour | | 10 | **Storage Provisioned IOPS Cost** | $Cost = IOPS_{prov} \times R_{IOPS}$ | USD ($) | AWS: **\$0.065 per provisioned IOPS-month** | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IaaS** is used by Netflix (encoding farm on AWS), Dropbox (storage backend on S3), and Airbnb (ElasticSearch clusters on EC2). - **PaaS** is used by Pinterest (Heroku), and by most enterprise Java/Python dev teams on **AWS Elastic Beanstalk** or **Azure App Service**. - **SaaS** is the dominant delivery model for CRM (**Salesforce**), HR (**Workday**), and collaboration (**Slack, Teams**). - **FaaS** powers event-driven pipelines at iRobot (IoT vacuum telemetry processing on AWS Lambda) and Coca-Cola (vending machine data ingestion). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from Availability Percentage > [!NOTE] > This is a **guaranteed KTU Part A / Part B question**. You must derive downtime from the SLA given by a provider. **Given:** A cloud provider advertises an availability of **$A = 99.95\%$**. **Step 1 — Convert the percentage to a probability fraction.** $$ A_{frac} = \frac{A}{100} = \frac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the unavailability (the probability that the service is DOWN).** $$ U_{frac} = 1 - A_{frac} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005 $$ **Step 3 — Convert unavailability to annual minutes.** The total minutes in a non-leap year is $365 \times 24 \times 60$. $$ Total_{min} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 4 — Multiply unavailability by total minutes.** $$ Downtime_{yr} = U_{frac} \times Total_{min} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ Downtime_{yr} = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to hours for intuition.** $$ Downtime_{hr} = \frac{262.8}{60} = 4.38 \text{ hours/year} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A 99.95% SLA allows approximately **4.38 hours of downtime per year**, often scheduled during off-peak maintenance windows. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Composite SLA of a Multi-Tier Web Application > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU asks: *"An application uses an EC2 instance (99.95%) and an RDS database (99.95%). Both must be UP for the app to work. What is the composite SLA?"* **Step 1 — Identify the dependency structure.** This is a **serial/dependent** architecture. If **either** EC2 OR RDS is down, the application is unavailable. **Step 2 — Apply the product rule for serial availability.** $$ SLA_{composite} = SLA_{EC2} \times SLA_{RDS} $$ $$ SLA_{composite} = 0.9995 \times 0.9995 $$ **Step 3 — Expand the product.** $$ SLA_{composite} = (1 - 0.0005)^2 $$ $$ SLA_{composite} = 1 - 2(0.0005) + (0.0005)^2 $$ $$ SLA_{composite} = 1 - 0.001 + 0.00000025 $$ $$ SLA_{composite} = 0.99900025 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percentage.** $$ SLA_{composite} \% \approx 99.90\% $$ **Step 5 — Compute the new annual downtime.** $$ Downtime_{yr} = (1 - 0.99900025) \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ Downtime_{yr} = 0.00099975 \times 525{,}600 $$ $$ Downtime_{yr} \approx 525.46 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 8.76 \text{ hours/year} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly add the SLAs (e.g., $99.95 + 99.95$). SLAs multiply, they do **not** add. This is the single most common mistake in cloud SLA problems. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Pay-Per-Use Monthly Bill > [!NOTE] > KTU numerical: *"A team uses 720 hours of a t3.medium instance at \$0.0416/hour, transfers 2 TB (2048 GB) of egress data at \$0.09/GB, and stores 500 GB in S3 Standard at \$0.023/GB-month. Calculate the monthly bill."* **Step 1 — Compute Compute Cost.** $$ Cost_{compute} = 720 \times 0.0416 = 29.952 \text{ USD} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Egress Cost.** $$ Cost_{egress} = 2048 \times 0.09 = 184.32 \text{ USD} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Storage Cost.** $$ Cost_{storage} = 500 \times 0.023 = 11.50 \text{ USD} $$ **Step 4 — Sum all components.** $$ Cost_{total} = Cost_{compute} + Cost_{egress} + Cost_{storage} $$ $$ Cost_{total} = 29.952 + 184.32 + 11.50 $$ $$ Cost_{total} = 225.772 \text{ USD} $$ **Step 5 — Apply tax/VAT (assume 18% GST, a KTU-style Indian context).** $$ Cost_{final} = 225.772 \times 1.18 \approx 266.41 \text{ USD} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cloud Service Comparison > [!NOTE] > This matrix is the **single most diagrammed comparison in KTU 2024 exams**. Memorize the "You manage" column. | Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | FaaS | |-------|------------|------|------|------|------| | **Application** | You | You | You | Provider | You (code only) | | **Data** | You | You | You | Provider | You | | **Runtime** | You | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Middleware** | You | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **OS** | You | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Virtualization** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Servers** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Storage** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Networking** | You | Provider | Provider | Provider | Provider | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cloud Service Use-Case Mapping | Use Case | Best Service | Why? | |----------|--------------|------| | Host a static website | **SaaS / IaaS (S3 + CloudFront)** | Lowest cost, no server management | | Train an ML model on 1 TB of data | **PaaS (SageMaker) / IaaS (P3 instances)** | Elastic GPUs, no framework setup | | Send 10 million transactional emails/day | **SaaS (SendGrid, SES)** | Provider handles deliverability, DKIM, throttling | | Build a REST API for a mobile app | **PaaS (App Engine, Elastic Beanstalk)** | Auto-scaling, managed TLS, zero-downtime deploys | | Process 100 million image uploads/day | **FaaS (Lambda + S3 triggers)** | Pay only for execution milliseconds | | Migrate legacy Windows SQL Server | **IaaS (EC2, Azure VMs)** | Lift-and-shift with full OS control | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Cloud Service Stack (Layered Architecture) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **five-layer cloud service stack** with the management-responsibility boundary between **Customer** and **Provider**. ```mermaid graph TD clientA[Client Browser] clientB[Mobile App] clientC[Thin Client Terminal] saasL[SaaS - Google Workspace - Salesforce - Office 365] faasL[FaaS - AWS Lambda - Azure Functions - GCP Cloud Functions] paasL[PaaS - App Engine - Heroku - Azure App Service] iaasL[IaaS - EC2 - Azure VM - Compute Engine] virtL[Virtualization - Hypervisor KVM - ESXi - Xen] physL[Physical Layer - Servers - SAN - Switches - CRAC - UPS] secC[Customer Manages - App Code - Data - IAM - OS in IaaS] secP[Provider Manages - Hardware - Network - Hypervisor - Host OS] clientA --> saasL clientB --> paasL clientC --> faasL saasL --> paasL faasL --> paasL paasL --> iaasL iaasL --> virtL virtL --> physL secC -.governs.-> saasL secP -.governs.-> physL ``` ### 4.2 Cloud Service Selection Decision Flow > [!IMPORTANT] > This diagram is a **frequently repeated KTU question**: *"Given a workload, which cloud service model should be chosen?"* ```mermaid graph TD startA[New IT Workload] q1{Is the workload
a standard end-user app
like Email or CRM?} q2{Does the team want to
write custom code
without managing servers?} q3{Is the workload event-driven
with bursts and idle periods?} q4{None of the above -
need full OS and infra control?} outSaaS[Choose SaaS] outPaaS[Choose PaaS] outFaaS[Choose FaaS] outIaaS[Choose IaaS] startA --> q1 q1 -- Yes --> outSaaS q1 -- No --> q2 q2 -- Yes --> outPaaS q2 -- No --> q3 q3 -- Yes --> outFaaS q3 -- No --> q4 q4 -- Yes --> outIaaS ``` ### 4.3 Shared Responsibility Model Diagram > [!NOTE] > This block diagram clarifies **who manages what** in a public cloud (AWS as the canonical example). ```mermaid graph LR subgraph custZone[Customer Responsibility] appC[Application Code] dataC[Data and Encryption] osC[Guest OS Patching - IaaS only] iamC[Identity and Access Mgmt IAM] end subgraph provZone[Provider Responsibility - AWS] physC[Physical Data Center Security] servC[Server and Storage Hardware] netC[Networking Infrastructure] hypC[Hypervisor and Host OS] end appC -.in the cloud.-> custZone physC -.of the cloud.-> provZone ``` ### 4.4 Multi-Region High-Availability Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **canonical KTU diagram for SLAs and elasticity**. ```mermaid graph TD userA[End User] cdnA[CloudFront CDN - Edge Locations] elbA[Elastic Load Balancer] az1a[EC2 in Availability Zone 1] az2a[EC2 in Availability Zone 2] rdsPrimary[RDS Primary DB] rdsStandby[RDS Standby DB - Multi-AZ] s3a[S3 Bucket - Cross-Region Replication] userA --> cdnA cdnA --> elbA elbA --> az1a elbA --> az2a az1a --> rdsPrimary az2a --> rdsPrimary rdsPrimary -.replicates synchronously.-> rdsStandby az1a -.async backup.-> s3a ``` ### 4.5 Cloud Deployment Model Comparison Block ```mermaid graph TD cloudA[Cloud Deployment Models] pubC[Public Cloud - AWS - Azure - GCP - Open to all tenants] priC[Private Cloud - On-Prem OpenStack - Single tenant - High control] hybC[Hybrid Cloud - Public + Private linked via VPN or Direct Connect] comC[Community Cloud - Shared by organizations with common policy - e.g. GovCloud] cloudA --> pubC cloudA --> priC cloudA --> hybC cloudA --> comC ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]** **Q1. Define Cloud Computing. List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as per NIST.** > **Model Answer:** > Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. (NIST SP 800-145) > > The five essential characteristics are: > 1. **On-demand self-service** > 2. **Broad network access** > 3. **Resource pooling** > 4. **Rapid elasticity** > 5. **Measured service** --- **Q2. Differentiate between SaaS and PaaS with one example each.** > **Model Answer:** > > | Parameter | SaaS | PaaS | > |-----------|------|------| > | **Full Form** | Software-as-a-Service | Platform-as-a-Service | > | **User Manages** | Only data and user access | Application code and data | > | **Provider Manages** | Everything else (OS, runtime, app, infra) | OS, runtime, middleware, infra | > | **Example** | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce | Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk | > | **Target User** | End-user / business user | Application developer | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Adapted Style]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three main cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — with a neat diagram showing the layers managed by the provider versus the customer. > **Model Answer:** > > The three primary cloud service models are the **SPI Model** (Software, Platform, Infrastructure). > > **1. Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS):** > - Provider supplies raw virtualized resources: virtual machines, block/object storage, virtual networks, and load balancers. > - Customer has **full control over the OS, middleware, runtime, and application** stack. > - Examples: **Amazon EC2**, **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines**, **Google Compute Engine**. > - Use case: lift-and-shift migrations, custom network topologies, dev/test environments. > > **2. Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS):** > - Provider manages the OS, runtime (Java/Python/Node), middleware, and database. > - Customer focuses only on **application code and data**. > - Examples: **Google App Engine**, **Heroku**, **AWS Elastic Beanstalk**, **Azure App Service**. > - Use case: rapid application development, microservices deployment, API backends. > > **3. Software-as-a-Service (SaaS):** > - Provider delivers the entire stack including the application. Customer uses it via a web browser or API. > - No local installation; multi-tenant by default. > - Examples: **Google Workspace**, **Microsoft Office 365**, **Salesforce CRM**, **Slack**. > - Use case: email, CRM, collaboration, ERP, productivity. > > **Responsibility Diagram (textual representation for the answer script):** > ``` > +-------------------------+--------+--------+--------+ > | Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | > +-------------------------+--------+--------+--------+ > | Application | Cus | Cus | Prov | > | Data | Cus | Cus | Cus | > | Runtime | Cus | Prov | Prov | > | Middleware | Cus | Prov | Prov | > | Operating System | Cus | Prov | Prov | > | Virtualization | Prov | Prov | Prov | > | Servers/Storage/Network | Prov | Prov | Prov | > +-------------------------+--------+--------+--------+ > (Cus = Customer manages; Prov = Provider manages) > ``` **(b) [7 Marks]** A startup uses an AWS architecture with an Application Load Balancer (SLA 99.99%), two EC2 instances in different AZs (each SLA 99.95%), and an RDS Multi-AZ database (SLA 99.95%). Compute the **composite SLA** and the **expected annual downtime in minutes**. > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1 — Convert all SLAs to fractional form.** > > $$ > SLA_{ALB} = 0.9999, \quad SLA_{EC2} = 0.9995, \quad SLA_{RDS} = 0.9995 > $$ > > **Step 2 — Identify the topology.** > > The ALB is in series with the EC2 layer (which is in series with RDS). The two EC2s are in **parallel** (one survives, the system stays up). > > **Step 3 — Compute EC2 parallel availability.** > > The system fails only when **both** EC2s fail simultaneously. > > $$ > P_{EC2_{fail}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times (1 - 0.9995) = 0.0005 \times 0.0005 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} > $$ > > $$ > SLA_{EC2_{parallel}} = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999975 > $$ > > **Step 4 — Compute the composite serial SLA.** > > $$ > SLA_{composite} = SLA_{ALB} \times SLA_{EC2_{parallel}} \times SLA_{RDS} > $$ > > $$ > SLA_{composite} = 0.9999 \times 0.99999975 \times 0.9995 > $$ > > $$ > SLA_{composite} \approx 0.9984 > $$ > > $$ > SLA_{composite} \approx 99.84\% > $$ > > **Step 5 — Compute the annual downtime.** > > $$ > Downtime_{yr} = (1 - 0.9984) \times 525{,}600 > $$ > > $$ > Downtime_{yr} = 0.0016 \times 525{,}600 > $$ > > $$ > Downtime_{yr} \approx 840.96 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 14.02 \text{ hours/year} > $$ > > **Conclusion:** The composite SLA of **99.84%** allows approximately **14 hours of annual downtime**, which is why redundant AZs and Multi-AZ databases are critical for high-availability workloads. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four cloud deployment models** with one real-world example for each. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. > **Model Answer:** > > | Deployment Model | Description | Example | Advantage | Disadvantage | > |------------------|-------------|---------|-----------|--------------| > | **Public Cloud** | Provider-owned, open to general public over the internet | AWS, Azure, GCP | Lowest cost, no CapEx | Shared tenancy, less control | > | **Private Cloud** | Dedicated to a single org, can be on-prem or hosted | OpenStack on corporate datacenter, VMware Cloud on AWS | Full control, compliance | High CapEx, skilled staff needed | > | **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of public + private linked by VPN/Direct Connect | Bank keeping customer data in private cloud, web tier in AWS | Burst capacity, data sovereignty | Complex networking, integration cost | > | **Community Cloud** | Shared by orgs with common policy/regulatory needs | GovCloud (US government), healthcare consortium cloud | Shared compliance costs | Limited vendor choice, governance overhead | **(b) [7 Marks]** A B.Tech project team runs a serverless workload on **AWS Lambda**. The function is invoked **5 million times per month**, each invocation runs for **200 ms**, allocates **512 MB** of RAM, and the data transfer is **100 GB** per month. Calculate the monthly bill. Use the AWS pricing: **\$0.20 per 1M requests**, **\$0.0000166667 per GB-second**, and **\$0.09 per GB egress**. > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1 — Compute the request cost.** > > $$ > Cost_{requests} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 0.20 = 5 \times 0.20 = 1.00 \text{ USD} > $$ > > **Step 2 — Compute the compute duration in GB-seconds.** > > Convert milliseconds to seconds: > > $$ > T_{total} = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.200 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ seconds} > $$ > > Convert memory to GB: > > $$ > M = \frac{512}{1024} = 0.5 \text{ GB} > $$ > > Compute GB-seconds: > > $$ > GB\text{-}s = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 = 500{,}000 \text{ GB-seconds} > $$ > > **Step 3 — Compute the compute cost.** > > $$ > Cost_{compute} = 500{,}000 \times 0.0000166667 > $$ > > $$ > Cost_{compute} \approx 8.33 \text{ USD} > $$ > > **Step 4 — Compute the egress cost.** > > $$ > Cost_{egress} = 100 \times 0.09 = 9.00 \text{ USD} > $$ > > **Step 5 — Sum the total bill.** > > $$ > Cost_{total} = 1.00 + 8.33 + 9.00 = 18.33 \text{ USD/month} > $$ > > **Step 6 — Add 18% GST (Indian context).** > > $$ > Cost_{final} = 18.33 \times 1.18 \approx 21.63 \text{ USD/month} > $$ > > **Conclusion:** The serverless architecture is **highly cost-efficient** for this workload — the same workload on a 24/7 EC2 t3.medium instance would cost approximately \$30/month for compute alone, even when idle. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points in Module 3:** > 1. **Confusing the Shared Responsibility Model** — Students say *"AWS is responsible for security."* This is **wrong**. AWS is responsible for security *of* the cloud; the customer is responsible for security *in* the cloud. (Loses 2–3 marks) > 2. **Adding SLAs instead of multiplying** — Composite SLAs are multiplicative for serial dependencies, not additive. (Loses 4 marks) > 3. **Mixing up the FaaS / PaaS / IaaS management layers** — Always memorize the "you manage" column in the responsibility table. > 4. **Forgetting to convert units** — GB-month vs GiB-month, milliseconds vs seconds, MB vs MiB cause silent calculation errors. (Loses 2–3 marks) > 5. **Ignoring data egress costs** — Many students compute compute and storage but forget that cloud providers charge **\$0.09/GB** for data leaving the cloud. This is a frequent trap in numerical questions. > 6. **Writing deployment model instead of service model** — A classic question asks *"List the service models"* and the student answers *"Public, Private, Hybrid."* This is a **4-mark immediate loss**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Cloud Computing Services:** - **NIST Definition**: Cloud computing is on-demand, network-accessible, pooled, elastic, and measured. (Memorize verbatim for 2-mark questions.) - **Five Characteristics**: On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service. (Acronym: **O-B-R-R-M**.) - **Three Service Models (SPI)**: SaaS, PaaS, IaaS. The higher you go, the more the provider manages. - **Emerging Models**: FaaS (serverless) and CaaS (Container-as-a-Service, e.g., AWS EKS) are also tested. - **Four Deployment Models**: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community. - **Shared Responsibility**: Security *of* the cloud = provider; security *in* the cloud = customer. - **Composite SLA**: Multiply for serial (dependent) components; use complement rule for parallel (redundant) components. - **Cost Components**: Compute (per vCPU-hour), Storage (per GB-month), Egress (per GB out), Requests (per million). - **FaaS Pricing**: $Cost = Requests \times Rate_{req} + GB\text{-}s \times Rate_{compute}$. - **Cloud Providers**: AWS (market leader ~32%), Microsoft Azure (~23%), Google Cloud (~10%), plus Alibaba, IBM, Oracle. - **Key Formulas**: $Downtime = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ and $SLA_{composite} = \prod SLA_i$. - **Avoid Common Traps**: Egress charges, region-specific pricing, GST in numericals, multiplicative (not additive) SLAs. - **KTU 2024 Focus**: Module 3 emphasizes the **service model decision matrix**, **SLA math**, and **cost calculations** — expect a 14-mark question to combine two of these. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** Draw the **Cloud Service Stack diagram** and the **Shared Responsibility diagram** at least three times before the exam. KTU examiners award 1–2 extra marks for neat, labeled diagrams in Part B answers.

topics.title: Cloud Computing and Security - Risks in Cloud Computing, Data Security in Cloud, Cloud Security Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Security - Risks in Cloud Computing, Data Security in Cloud, Cloud Security Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Risks in Cloud Computing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Cloud Computing Risk** refers to the combined probability and impact of an adverse event that exploits one or more vulnerabilities in a cloud-based system, resulting in unauthorized access, data loss, service disruption, or compliance violation. Formally, risk is a function of **Threat**, **Vulnerability**, and **Asset Value**. The **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** categorizes risks into the *Notorious Nine* / *Top Threats to Cloud Computing*, which include data breaches, data loss, account hijacking, insecure APIs, denial of service, malicious insiders, abuse of cloud services, insufficient due diligence, and shared technology vulnerabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, students must be able to **enumerate the top cloud security risks** (per CSA) and **map them to the CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability). Examiners frequently ask for a 7-mark "list and explain" question on CSA's Top Threats.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you have shifted your most valuable jewelry from a personal locker in your house to a **bank's safety deposit vault**. While the vault is far more secure than your locker in terms of physical protection, you have now introduced new risks:
- The **bank's staff** might mishandle your locker (Insider Threat).
- A **clever thief** might forge your signature and open the locker (Account Hijacking).
- The bank itself might have a **faulty vault door** (Shared Technology Vulnerability).
- The bank might **lose your key** or the vault might be destroyed in a natural disaster (Data Loss).
- You have **no control** over who enters the vault room (Insecure API).

This is precisely what happens in cloud computing — by outsourcing your IT infrastructure, you trade off some **direct control** for **scalability and cost-efficiency**, but inherit a new taxonomy of risks that must be actively managed.

---

## 1.2 Data Security in Cloud

### Formal Academic Definition
**Data Security in the Cloud** is the practice of protecting digital data — at rest, in transit, and in use — from unauthorized access, corruption, or theft throughout its entire lifecycle in a cloud environment. It encompasses cryptographic controls (encryption, hashing), access controls (RBAC, ABAC), data masking, tokenization, key management, and data loss prevention (DLP) mechanisms. The three fundamental states of data that must be secured are: **Data-at-Rest** (stored on disk), **Data-in-Transit** (moving across networks), and **Data-in-Use** (being processed in memory/CPU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** The **CIA Triad** underpins all data security: **C**onfidentiality (only authorized access), **I**ntegrity (data not altered), **A**vailability (data accessible when needed). Every cloud security control maps to one or more of these three pillars.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of your data as a **diary** that you want to send to a friend through a courier service:
- **Data-at-Rest** (in the locker): The diary sits in a sealed envelope inside the courier's warehouse → protected by the warehouse lock (encryption at rest).
- **Data-in-Transit** (on the road): The courier truck is moving → protected by an armed escort and tamper-proof GPS (TLS/SSL).
- **Data-in-Use** (being read): Your friend opens the diary to read it → protected by the friend's personal privacy setting (memory encryption, secure enclaves).

If any of these three layers fail, the diary leaks. Cloud data security must defend all three states simultaneously.

---

## 1.3 Cloud Security Services

### Formal Academic Definition
**Cloud Security Services** are a portfolio of managed security offerings — delivered via **IaaS**, **PaaS**, or **SaaS** models — that provide on-demand security capabilities such as Identity and Access Management (IAM), Data Loss Prevention (DLP), Security Information and Event Management (SIEM), encryption, vulnerability scanning, Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS), and Compliance Monitoring. These services follow the **shared responsibility model**, in which the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** secures the *of* the cloud (infrastructure, hardware, hypervisor), while the **customer** secures the *in* the cloud (data, applications, OS, access policies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shared Responsibility Model (Must-Know for KTU):**
> - **IaaS:** CSP secures physical infra, network, host OS/hypervisor; Customer secures guest OS, apps, data.
> - **PaaS:** CSP secures runtime, middleware, OS; Customer secures applications and data.
> - **SaaS:** CSP secures everything except the customer's data and access credentials.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a **rented fully-furnished apartment**:
- The **landlord (CSP)** is responsible for the building's structural safety, fire alarms, elevator maintenance, and parking lot lighting.
- The **tenant (Customer)** is responsible for locking their own apartment door, not leaving valuables in the open, and choosing who gets a spare key.
- A **cloud security service** is like hiring a **professional security agency** (ADT, for example) that installs cameras, motion sensors, and alarm systems — all managed from a central control room — which both landlord and tenant can subscribe to.

The agency does not own the building or your apartment, but it provides the **policies, tools, and monitoring** that protect both parties.

---

## 1.4 Summary Snapshot

| Concept | One-line Essence | KTU Weightage Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Cloud Risk** | Probability × Impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | High (frequently asked 7–14 marks) |
| **Data Security** | Protect data at rest, in transit, in use via CIA Triad | High (mechanism-based questions) |
| **Security Services** | Shared responsibility via IaaS/PaaS/SaaS security stacks | Medium-High (comparison questions) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Cloud Computing Risk

A risk is not a single entity — it is a **composite of three variables**. The classical risk formula, also used in NIST SP 800-30 and ISO 27005, is:

$$
\text{Risk} = f(\text{Threat}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Asset})
$$

In simpler operational terms used by KTU examiners:

$$
R = P \times I
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Probability of threat occurrence (0 to 1, dimensionless)
- $I$ = Impact (asset value in monetary or reputational units, e.g., ₹ or normalized 1–5 scale)
- $R$ = Risk score

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The KTU examiner often gives a scenario-based question asking you to *compute* the risk for two assets and recommend which one to mitigate first. Always pick the **higher R value** as the priority.

### 2.1.1 CSA's Top Threats (Notorious Nine) — Structured Decomposition

The Cloud Security Alliance publishes a periodic ranking of the most significant cloud security threats. Below is a **bullet-level logical breakdown** that KTU expects you to reproduce:

1. **Data Breach** — Unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data (PII, PHI, IP).
   - *Why it happens:* Misconfigured storage (S3 buckets), weak access controls, credential theft.
   - *Maps to:* Confidentiality failure.
2. **Data Loss** — Permanent destruction of data due to accidental deletion, natural disaster, or malicious action.
   - *Maps to:* Availability + Integrity failure.
3. **Account Hijacking** — Attacker steals credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or session hijacking.
   - *Mitigation:* MFA, conditional access, zero-trust.
4. **Insecure APIs** — Cloud provider APIs lack authentication, rate limiting, or input validation.
   - *Mitigation:* OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, API gateways.
5. **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)** — Flooding the cloud service with traffic to exhaust resources.
   - *Maps to:* Availability failure.
6. **Malicious Insider** — Disgruntled employee or contractor with legitimate access abuses privileges.
7. **Abuse of Cloud Services** — Misuse of free-tier resources for crypto-mining, botnet C2, or password cracking.
8. **Insufficient Due Diligence** — Customer fails to understand the CSP's SLA, jurisdiction, or compliance posture before migration.
9. **Shared Technology Vulnerabilities** — Flaws in hypervisor, shared kernel, or multi-tenant isolation (VM escape, side-channel attacks like Spectre/Meltdown).

### 2.1.2 Risk Categories — Three Pillars

| Risk Category | Examples | Affected Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|
| **Policy & Organizational** | Insufficient due diligence, vendor lock-in, legal/jurisdiction issues | Customer's management + Legal team |
| **Technical** | Data breach, insecure APIs, DoS, shared tech vulnerabilities | Cloud architect + Security engineer |
| **Legal & Compliance** | GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023, PCI-DSS violations | DPO + Compliance officer |

---

## 2.2 Data Security in Cloud — Deep Mechanics

### 2.2.1 The Three States of Data (MUST Memorize)

| State | Definition | Primary Defense Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Data-at-Rest** | Data stored on persistent media (HDD, SSD, object storage) | AES-256 encryption, access-controlled storage, backups |
| **Data-in-Transit** | Data moving across networks (LAN, WAN, internet) | TLS 1.3, IPsec, VPN, mTLS |
| **Data-in-Use** | Data being processed in RAM or CPU registers | Secure enclaves (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), confidential computing, homomorphic encryption |

### 2.2.2 Cryptographic Toolkit

$$
E_k(P) = C \quad \text{(Encryption function: plaintext → ciphertext using key k)}
$$

$$
D_k(C) = P \quad \text{(Decryption function: ciphertext → plaintext using key k)}
$$

For **symmetric** (e.g., AES):
$$
E_{k_{\text{shared}}}(P) = C \quad ; \quad D_{k_{\text{shared}}}(C) = P
$$
Same key for encrypt and decrypt. Fast, suitable for bulk data-at-rest.

For **asymmetric** (e.g., RSA, ECC):
$$
E_{k_{\text{pub}}}(P) = C \quad ; \quad D_{k_{\text{priv}}}(C) = P
$$
Public key encrypts, private key decrypts. Used for key exchange, digital signatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid encryption (used in TLS 1.3):** Asymmetric cryptography is used only to securely exchange a **symmetric session key**, which is then used for bulk data transfer. This combines the **key distribution advantage** of asymmetric with the **speed advantage** of symmetric.

### 2.2.3 Advanced Data Protection Techniques

- **Tokenization:** Replaces sensitive data (e.g., PAN: 4532-1234-5678-9010) with a non-reversible token (e.g., `tok_a1b2c3`). The original-to-token mapping is stored in a **token vault**.
- **Data Masking:** Hides original data with fictitious but realistic data (e.g., `XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-9010` showing only last 4 digits).
- **Homomorphic Encryption:** Allows computation on encrypted data without decrypting it first — useful for privacy-preserving analytics.
- **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) / HYOK (Hold Your Own Key):** Customer manages the encryption keys externally so CSPs cannot decrypt customer data at rest.

---

## 2.3 Cloud Security Services — Architecture & Categories

### 2.3.1 Identity and Access Management (IAM)

The **first line of defense**. IAM services provide:
- **Authentication** (AuthN) — "Are you who you say you are?" (Username/password, MFA, biometrics).
- **Authorization** (AuthZ) — "What are you allowed to do?" (RBAC, ABAC, policies).

The **AAA Framework**:
$$
\text{AAA} = \text{Authentication} + \text{Authorization} + \text{Accounting (Audit Logging)}
$$

### 2.3.2 Other Security Services

| Service | Full Form | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **DLP** | Data Loss Prevention | Detects and blocks sensitive data exfiltration |
| **SIEM** | Security Information & Event Management | Aggregates logs, correlates events, triggers alerts |
| **IDS/IPS** | Intrusion Detection/Prevention System | Detects (IDS) and blocks (IPS) malicious traffic |
| **CASB** | Cloud Access Security Broker | Sits between user and CSP, enforces security policies |
| **WAF** | Web Application Firewall | Filters malicious HTTP/S traffic to web apps |
| **KMS** | Key Management Service | Centralized creation, rotation, and destruction of encryption keys |
| **HSM** | Hardware Security Module | Tamper-resistant physical device for key storage |

### 2.3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These services are used in:
- **Healthcare** for HIPAA-compliant patient data storage.
- **Banking** for PCI-DSS compliant payment data handling.
- **Government** for sovereign data residency (data must stay within national borders).
- **E-commerce** for DDoS protection during festive sales (AWS Shield, Cloudflare).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE WARNING:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, the absolute value bar $\vert x \vert$ is rendered using `\vert` (NOT the pipe character) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Term | Symbolic Form | Unit / Type | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Score** | $R = P \times I$ | Probability (0–1) × Impact (₹ or 1–5) | Risk prioritization question |
| 2 | **Encryption** | $E_k(P) = C$ | Function notation | "Define encryption" theory question |
| 3 | **Decryption** | $D_k(C) = P$ | Function notation | "Define decryption" theory question |
| 4 | **AES Key Length** | $L = 128/192/256$ | bits | "Compare AES-128 vs AES-256" |
| 5 | **RSA Security** | $n = p \times q$ | Integer (modulus) | Optional: RSA key generation steps |
| 6 | **CIA Triad** | $\text{C} \cap \text{I} \cap \text{A}$ | Conceptual | Universal — every security question |
| 7 | **AAA Framework** | $\text{AuthN} + \text{AuthZ} + \text{Accounting}$ | Conceptual | IAM-related questions |
| 8 | **Shared Responsibility** | $\text{CSP} = \text{of cloud} ; \text{Cust} = \text{in cloud}$ | Conceptual | "Explain shared responsibility" |
| 9 | **TLS 1.3 Handshake** | 1-RTT (Round-Trip Time) | Latency measure | Optional: TLS upgrade discussion |
| 10 | **Entropy (key strength)** | $H = \log_2(K)$ | bits | Optional: Key strength comparison |

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters — Engineering Rationale

A 2024 IBM Cost of a Data Breach report shows the **average cost of a cloud data breach is ~USD 4.88 million**, with breaches involving stolen credentials taking the longest to identify (~292 days). Understanding risk quantification helps:
- **Cloud architects** prioritize which control to invest in.
- **CISOs** justify security budgets to the board.
- **Auditors** verify compliance posture for ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR.
- **Developers** choose between symmetric vs. asymmetric crypto for their use case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Risk Quantification — Worked Derivation

### 3.1.1 Scenario
A KTU-style numerical question (commonly seen in 7-mark sub-parts):

> *"A cloud-hosted e-commerce application has an estimated 30% chance of suffering a SQL injection attack in a year. If successful, the estimated impact is ₹50,00,000 in revenue loss and reputational damage. Another vulnerability — a misconfigured S3 bucket — has a 60% chance of occurrence with an impact of ₹10,00,000. Compute the risk score for each and recommend prioritization."*

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the variables for vulnerability A (SQL Injection).

$$
P_A = 0.30 \quad ; \quad I_A = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the risk score for vulnerability A.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A &= P_A \times I_A \\
&= 0.30 \times 50{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the variables for vulnerability B (Misconfigured S3 bucket).

$$
P_B = 0.60 \quad ; \quad I_B = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the risk score for vulnerability B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= P_B \times I_B \\
&= 0.60 \times 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare and prioritize.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A > R_B \quad &\Rightarrow \quad 15{,}00{,}000 > 6{,}00{,}000 \\
&\Rightarrow \quad \text{mitigate SQL Injection FIRST}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Even though vulnerability B has a *higher probability* (60% > 30%), vulnerability A has a *higher impact*. Always compute and compare the **product**, not just one factor in isolation. This is a classic KTU trap to test if students truly understand risk = P × I.

### 3.1.3 Risk Heat Map (Common 14-Mark Question)

After computing $R$, the typical classification used in board exams is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \in [0, 3 \times 10^5) &\Rightarrow \text{LOW (accept risk)} \\
R \in [3 \times 10^5, 1 \times 10^7) &\Rightarrow \text{MEDIUM (apply controls)} \\
R \in [1 \times 10^7, \infty) &\Rightarrow \text{HIGH (immediate mitigation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So in our scenario: $R_A = 1.5 \times 10^7$ → **HIGH**; $R_B = 6 \times 10^5$ → **MEDIUM**.

---

## 3.2 Hybrid Encryption — Conceptual Derivation (TLS 1.3 Style)

### 3.2.1 The Problem

Symmetric encryption is **fast** (AES-256 runs at multi-GB/s in hardware) but suffers from a **key distribution problem** — how do two parties share the key securely over an insecure channel?

Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECC) **solves key distribution** but is **slow** (1000× slower than AES).

### 3.2.2 The Solution — Hybrid Encryption

Let:
- $K_S$ = the symmetric session key (e.g., 256-bit AES key)
- $K_{pub}^{B}, K_{priv}^{B}$ = Bob's asymmetric public and private key pair
- $P$ = plaintext message
- $C$ = ciphertext

**Step 1 — Key Exchange (Asymmetric phase):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{K_{pub}^{B}}(K_S) &= C_{\text{wrapped}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alice encrypts the session key using Bob's public key. Only Bob can decrypt it with his private key.

**Step 2 — Bulk Data Transfer (Symmetric phase):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{K_S}(P) &= C_{\text{bulk}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alice encrypts the actual message using the symmetric session key $K_S$.

**Step 3 — Decryption by Bob:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{K_{priv}^{B}}(C_{\text{wrapped}}) &= K_S \\
D_{K_S}(C_{\text{bulk}}) &= P
\end{aligned}
$$

Bob first unwraps the session key, then decrypts the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is genius:** The expensive asymmetric crypto is used only **once** (to protect a tiny 256-bit key), while the cheap symmetric crypto handles the multi-gigabyte bulk data. This is the basis of **TLS 1.3, SSH, PGP, and HTTPS**.

---

## 3.3 Key Management Lifecycle (KMS Workflow)

A 14-mark KTU question often asks: *"Explain the key management lifecycle in cloud."* Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step breakdown.

**Step 1 — Key Generation:**
$$
K = \text{Random}(L \text{ bits})
$$
Where $L \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ for AES. Use a **CSPRNG** (Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator) like `/dev/urandom` or `Fortuna`.

**Step 2 — Key Storage:**
Keys are stored in a **tamper-resistant HSM** (Hardware Security Module) or in a **KMS** that uses envelope encryption (key-encrypting-key encrypts the data-encrypting-key).

**Step 3 — Key Distribution:**
Delivered via secure channels (TLS, IPsec). In cloud, the **Key Management Service (KMS)** acts as the trusted broker.

**Step 4 — Key Rotation:**
Performed **periodically** (e.g., every 90 days) or **on-demand** (e.g., after suspected compromise). Old keys are kept for decryption of legacy data.

**Step 5 — Key Revocation:**
A key is **revoked** (added to a CRL — Certificate Revocation List) when it is compromised or no longer trusted.

**Step 6 — Key Destruction:**
After the retention period, the key is **cryptographically erased** (deleted from HSM/KMS). All data encrypted with it becomes permanently unrecoverable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rotation Interval (KTU-ready):** Industry standard is **90 days for data keys** and **1 year for Key Encryption Keys (KEKs)**. AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud KMS automate this.

---

## 3.4 Data Security Matrix — Exhaustive Cross-Tabulation

This table directly answers the frequently asked KTU question: *"Explain data security in cloud with respect to data at rest, in transit, and in use."*

| Aspect | Data-at-Rest | Data-in-Transit | Data-in-Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Stored persistently | Moving across network | Being processed (RAM/CPU) |
| **Threat** | Disk theft, unauthorized DB access, backup theft | MITM, eavesdropping, session hijack | Memory dump, cold-boot attack, kernel exploit |
| **Primary Control** | AES-256 encryption at rest | TLS 1.3, IPsec VPN | Intel SGX, AMD SEV, homomorphic encryption |
| **Cloud Service** | AWS S3 SSE, Azure Storage SSE, GCS CME | AWS ALB/ELB TLS termination, Azure Front Door | Azure Confidential Compute, AWS Nitro Enclaves |
| **Key Mgmt** | KMS / BYOK / HSM | Per-session ephemeral keys (PFS) | Sealing keys to enclave identity |
| **Compliance** | PCI-DSS req. 3, GDPR Art. 32 | PCI-DSS req. 4, HIPAA §164.312(e) | ISO 27018, SOC 2 CC6.1 |
| **Performance Cost** | ~2–5% CPU overhead | ~1–3% latency overhead | ~10–30% CPU overhead (enclaves) |

---

## 3.5 Shared Responsibility — Step-by-Step Allocation

The KTU 2024 module-3 question bank repeatedly asks: *"What is the shared responsibility model in cloud security?"* The exhaustive breakdown:

**Step 1 — Identify the service model (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS).**

**Step 2 — For IaaS (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs):**
$$
\text{CSP Responsibilities} = \{\text{Physical}, \text{Network}, \text{Hypervisor}, \text{Host OS}\}
$$
$$
\text{Customer Responsibilities} = \{\text{Guest OS}, \text{Apps}, \text{Data}, \text{Access}, \text{Network Config}\}
$$

**Step 3 — For PaaS (e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service):**
$$
\text{CSP Responsibilities} = \{\text{OS}, \text{Middleware}, \text{Runtime}, \text{Hardware}, \text{Network}\}
$$
$$
\text{Customer Responsibilities} = \{\text{App Code}, \text{Data}, \text{Access}\}
$$

**Step 4 — For SaaS (e.g., Microsoft 365, Google Workspace):**
$$
\text{CSP Responsibilities} = \{\text{Application}, \text{OS}, \text{Middleware}, \text{Hardware}, \text{Network}\}
$$
$$
\text{Customer Responsibilities} = \{\text{Data}, \text{User Access}, \text{Device Security}\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Rule of Thumb (frequently asked):** *As you move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the responsibility shifts from the customer to the CSP. In SaaS, the customer is responsible only for "data and people".*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSA Top Threats — Risk Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Cloud Computing Risks]
    rootB[CSA Top Threats]
    rootA --> rootB
    rootB --> thr1[Data Breach]
    rootB --> thr2[Data Loss]
    rootB --> thr3[Account Hijacking]
    rootB --> thr4[Insecure APIs]
    rootB --> thr5[Denial of Service]
    rootB --> thr6[Malicious Insider]
    rootB --> thr7[Abuse of Cloud]
    rootB --> thr8[Insufficient Due Diligence]
    rootB --> thr9[Shared Tech Vulnerabilities]
    thr1 --> cia1[Confidentiality Fail]
    thr2 --> cia2[Availability and Integrity Fail]
    thr3 --> cia1
    thr5 --> cia2
    thr6 --> cia1
    thr9 --> cia2
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This tree maps each CSA-notorious threat to which pillar of the CIA Triad it primarily violates. KTU examiners reward students who explicitly link each threat to CIA.

---

## 4.2 Data Security — Three-State Defense Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA[Data at Rest]
    stateB[Data in Transit]
    stateC[Data in Use]
    ctrlA[AES-256 KMS HSM]
    ctrlB[TLS 1.3 IPsec mTLS]
    ctrlC[Intel SGX Confidential Compute]
    stateA --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> stateA
    stateB --> ctrlB
    ctrlB --> stateB
    stateC --> ctrlC
    ctrlC --> stateC
    stateA -->|user reads| stateB
    stateB -->|server processes| stateC
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** Shows the **bidirectional flow** of data through the three states and the **dedicated cryptographic control** for each state. Note that data does not stay in one state — it transitions, and each transition needs the appropriate control.

---

## 4.3 Shared Responsibility Model — Visual Allocation Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IaaS_Layer
        iaasCust1[Customer: Guest OS Apps Data Access]
        iaasCsp1[CSP: Physical Network Hypervisor]
    end
    subgraph PaaS_Layer
        paasCust2[Customer: Apps Data Access]
        paasCsp2[CSP: OS Middleware Runtime]
    end
    subgraph SaaS_Layer
        saasCust3[Customer: Data Access Devices]
        saasCsp3[CSP: Application OS Hardware]
    end
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** Each layer is a **separate sub-graph (boundary)**. The customer's responsibility shrinks as you move down the stack (IaaS → SaaS), while the CSP's responsibility grows. This is the most-asked visual concept in the KTU 2024 module-3 question bank.

---

## 4.4 Identity and Access Management (IAM) — AuthN / AuthZ / Audit Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[End User]
    idp1[Identity Provider IdP]
    auth1{AuthN: Who are you}
    mfa1[MFA Token Check]
    authz1{AuthZ: Are you allowed}
    policy1[IAM Policy Evaluation]
    resource1[Cloud Resource S3 EC2 DB]
    log1[Audit Log CloudTrail]
    userA --> auth1
    auth1 --> mfa1
    mfa1 -->|verified| authz1
    auth1 -.->|via SAML OIDC| idp1
    idp1 --> auth1
    authz1 --> policy1
    policy1 -->|Allow| resource1
    policy1 -->|Deny| deny1[Access Denied 403]
    resource1 --> log1
    policy1 --> log1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is the **AAA pipeline**. Every access decision produces an audit log (mandatory for compliance). The "deny" branch is equally important — it must be logged and alerted on.

---

## 4.5 Key Management Service (KMS) — Lifecycle Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step1 Key Generation CSPRNG]
    s2[Step2 Key Storage HSM]
    s3[Step3 Key Distribution TLS Channel]
    s4[Step4 Key Usage Envelope Encryption]
    s5[Step5 Key Rotation 90 days]
    s6[Step6 Key Revocation Compromised]
    s7[Step7 Key Destruction Crypto Erase]
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 -.->|periodic| s1
    s4 -.->|compromise| s6
    s5 -.->|end of life| s7
    s6 --> s7
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The lifecycle is a **loop** — once a key is rotated, the new key goes through the same pipeline. The dashed lines indicate **conditional paths** (rotation is periodic, revocation is conditional on compromise, destruction is conditional on end of retention period).

---

## 4.6 Cloud Security Services — Layered Defense-in-Depth Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    layer1[Layer 1 Perimeter: WAF DDoS Shield CDN]
    layer2[Layer 2 Network: VPC Security Groups NACL TLS]
    layer3[Layer 3 Identity: IAM MFA SSO RBAC]
    layer4[Layer 4 Data: Encryption KMS Tokenization DLP]
    layer5[Layer 5 Application: SAST DAST API Gateway]
    layer6[Layer 6 Monitoring: SIEM SOAR Audit Logs]
    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer6
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is **Defense-in-Depth** — even if one layer fails, the next one catches the attack. KTU frequently tests whether students can list the layers in the correct order (perimeter → network → identity → data → app → monitoring).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Risk" in cloud computing and list any four risks from the CSA Top Threats list.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Risk in cloud computing is the product of the probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability and the resulting impact on the asset. It is formally expressed as:

$$
R = P \times I
$$

where $P$ is the probability of occurrence and $I$ is the impact magnitude.

**Four CSA Risks (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Data Breach** — Unauthorized access and exfiltration of sensitive customer data.
2. **Account Hijacking** — Theft of user credentials via phishing or credential stuffing.
3. **Insecure APIs** — Cloud provider APIs lacking proper authentication, encryption, or rate limiting.
4. **Denial of Service (DDoS)** — Overwhelming the cloud service with traffic to disrupt availability.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Data-at-Rest, Data-in-Transit, and Data-in-Use with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per state):**

| State | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data-at-Rest** | Data stored on persistent storage media | A file in an AWS S3 bucket |
| **Data-in-Transit** | Data moving across a network between two endpoints | An HTTPS request from a browser to a web server |
| **Data-in-Use** | Data currently being processed in CPU registers or RAM | A SQL query being executed on a decrypted row in memory |

The primary control for each is **AES-256 at rest**, **TLS 1.3 in transit**, and **Intel SGX / confidential computing in use**, respectively.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided — answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the various security risks in cloud computing with reference to the CSA's Top Threats. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Cloud Security Alliance (CSA) publishes a periodic list of the most critical threats to cloud computing. The following are the major risks every cloud architect must address:

1. **Data Breach (1 mark):** A data breach is the unauthorized access and exfiltration of sensitive, protected, or confidential information. In cloud environments, common causes include misconfigured storage buckets (e.g., publicly open AWS S3 buckets), weak access control policies, and stolen credentials. The 2017 Equifax breach (affecting 147 million consumers) and the 2019 Capital One breach are classic examples. It violates the **Confidentiality** pillar of the CIA Triad.

2. **Data Loss (1 mark):** Permanent destruction of data due to accidental deletion, hardware failure, or natural disaster. Unlike a breach, data loss is typically unintentional. Mitigation requires **3-2-1 backup rule** (3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite) and geographically redundant storage.

3. **Account Hijacking (1 mark):** Attackers use phishing, credential stuffing, or session hijacking to gain control of legitimate user accounts. Once hijacked, they can pivot across cloud services, exfiltrate data, or launch further attacks. Mitigation: enforce **MFA**, conditional access, and zero-trust principles.

4. **Insecure APIs (1 mark):** Cloud providers expose APIs (REST, SOAP) for customers to manage resources. If these APIs have weak authentication, lack rate limiting, or suffer from injection flaws, they become attack vectors. Mitigation: use **OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, API gateways, and rigorous input validation**.

5. **Denial of Service (1 mark):** DDoS attacks flood the cloud service with traffic from botnets, exhausting compute, memory, or bandwidth resources, making the service unavailable to legitimate users. Mitigation: **AWS Shield, Cloudflare, Azure DDoS Protection**, auto-scaling, and rate limiting.

6. **Malicious Insider (1 mark):** A current or former employee, contractor, or partner with authorized access intentionally misuses that access. This is particularly dangerous in cloud because insider can access multiple tenants' data. Mitigation: **separation of duties, audit logging, least privilege, employee screening**.

7. **Shared Technology Vulnerabilities (1 mark):** Multi-tenancy means multiple customers share the same hypervisor, kernel, or physical hardware. A flaw like **VM escape, Spectre, or Meltdown** could allow one tenant to read another's data. Mitigation: **patching, hypervisor hardening, confidential computing**.

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud security across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** defines the division of security duties between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the customer. The principle is: **CSP secures the *of* the cloud; customer secures the *in* the cloud.**

**IaaS — Infrastructure-as-a-Service (2 marks):**
- *CSP handles:* Physical data center security, host hardware, network infrastructure, hypervisor.
- *Customer handles:* Guest operating system, applications, middleware, runtime, data, access management, network security groups.
- *Examples:* AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.

**PaaS — Platform-as-a-Service (2 marks):**
- *CSP handles:* OS, middleware, runtime, virtualization, servers, storage, networking.
- *Customer handles:* Application code, data, access policies, identity.
- *Examples:* AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine.

**SaaS — Software-as-a-Service (2 marks):**
- *CSP handles:* Almost everything — application, OS, middleware, runtime, servers, storage.
- *Customer handles:* User access (IAM), data, endpoint security, configuration of SaaS settings.
- *Examples:* Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IaaS_Layer
        iaasCust1[Customer: Guest OS Apps Data Access]
        iaasCsp1[CSP: Physical Network Hypervisor]
    end
    subgraph PaaS_Layer
        paasCust2[Customer: Apps Data Access]
        paasCsp2[CSP: OS Middleware Runtime]
    end
    subgraph SaaS_Layer
        saasCust3[Customer: Data Access Devices]
        saasCsp3[CSP: Application OS Hardware]
    end
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** As the abstraction level increases (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS), the customer has progressively fewer security responsibilities but also less direct control. The choice of service model is a trade-off between **control and operational overhead**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the three states of data in the cloud. Discuss the cryptographic controls used to protect each state. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Data in a cloud environment exists in one of three states at any given time, and each state requires a different cryptographic control. This model is widely adopted by NIST SP 800-144 and is fundamental to cloud data security.

**1. Data-at-Rest (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Data that is stored on persistent media such as hard drives, SSDs, object storage (S3, Azure Blob), or databases.
- **Threats:** Physical theft of disk, unauthorized file access, backup theft, stolen database dumps.
- **Controls:**
  - **AES-256 encryption** of storage volumes and objects.
  - Server-side encryption (SSE) in object stores — AWS S3 SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C.
  - Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) in databases (SQL Server, Oracle).
  - Encryption keys stored in **HSM** or **KMS**.
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS requirement 3, GDPR Article 32, HIPAA §164.312(a)(2)(iv).

**2. Data-in-Transit (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Data moving across a network — between client and server, between two cloud services, or between data centers.
- **Threats:** Man-in-the-middle (MITM), eavesdropping, packet sniffing, session hijacking.
- **Controls:**
  - **TLS 1.3** (latest, with 1-RTT handshake) for HTTP-based traffic.
  - **IPsec VPN** for site-to-site or remote-access tunnels.
  - **mTLS (mutual TLS)** for service-to-service communication in microservices.
  - **SSH** for administrative access to VMs.
- **Compliance:** PCI-DSS requirement 4, HIPAA §164.312(e).

**3. Data-in-Use (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Data currently being processed in CPU registers, caches, or RAM.
- **Threats:** Memory dump attacks, cold-boot attacks, kernel-level exploits, malicious hypervisor.
- **Controls:**
  - **Intel SGX (Software Guard Extensions)** — creates hardware-isolated "enclaves" where code and data are encrypted even during execution.
  - **AMD SEV (Secure Encrypted Virtualization)** — encrypts VM memory with per-VM keys.
  - **Confidential Computing** (Azure Confidential VMs, AWS Nitro Enclaves) — extends protection to the in-use state.
  - **Homomorphic Encryption** — allows computation on encrypted data without ever decrypting it.

**Conclusion:** Modern cloud security requires a **layered approach** — every state of data must be defended by an appropriate cryptographic primitive, and these primitives must be coordinated through a centralized key management strategy.

**(b) Describe the architecture and components of a Key Management Service (KMS) in cloud. Explain the key lifecycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Key Management Service (KMS)** is a centralized system that generates, stores, distributes, rotates, and destroys cryptographic keys used to protect cloud data. AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud KMS are industry-standard examples.

**Architecture (3.5 marks):**

1. **Key Generation Module:** Uses a **CSPRNG** (Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator) compliant with NIST SP 800-90A to generate keys of 128/192/256 bits for AES or 2048/4096 bits for RSA.

2. **HSM Backend:** The actual key material is stored in a **Hardware Security Module** — a FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified tamper-resistant physical device. Software-level keys in the KMS API are never exposed in plaintext to the user.

3. **Envelope Encryption:** The KMS uses a two-tier model:
   - **Customer Master Key (CMK)** — the top-level key, stored in HSM, never leaves the HSM.
   - **Data Encryption Key (DEK)** — generated per-data-object, encrypted by the CMK, stored alongside the data.

4. **Access Control:** IAM policies control **who can use which key for which action** (encrypt, decrypt, generate). Separation of duties: key users vs. key administrators.

5. **Audit Logging:** All API calls (GenerateDataKey, Encrypt, Decrypt) are logged to **AWS CloudTrail / Azure Monitor** for compliance and forensic analysis.

**Key Lifecycle (3.5 marks):**

1. **Generation** — Key is created via CSPRNG.
2. **Storage** — Key is placed in HSM; CMK remains there permanently.
3. **Activation** — Key is marked active and available for use.
4. **Usage** — Application calls KMS API to encrypt/decrypt data.
5. **Rotation** — Performed automatically (every 90 days for AWS KMS CMKs) or manually. Old key versions are retained to decrypt legacy data.
6. **Revocation** — Key is disabled or scheduled for deletion if compromised.
7. **Destruction** — After the mandatory waiting period (7–30 days), the key is cryptographically erased. All data encrypted with it becomes permanently unrecoverable.

**Conclusion:** A robust KMS is the **backbone of cloud data security**. Without it, encryption offers no real protection, because the keys themselves become the new attack target. Customers should prefer services that support **BYOK** or **HYOK** for sensitive workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "in the cloud" vs "of the cloud":** Many students mix up shared responsibility. Memorize: **CSP = of the cloud (infra), Customer = in the cloud (data + access).**
> 2. **Forgetting one of the three data states:** A 7-mark question on data security MUST cover all three (rest, transit, use) — leaving out "in-use" loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Listing CSA threats without mapping to CIA:** Examiners want the *Why* — explicitly say "this threat violates **Confidentiality**" or "this affects **Availability**" to fetch full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring lifecycle stages in KMS:** A 7-mark KMS question expects you to list all **7 stages** (generate → store → distribute → use → rotate → revoke → destroy).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** KTU 2024 scheme has a strong emphasis on **diagrams** — a 7-mark question without a labeled diagram can lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Risk Formula:** $R = P \times I$ — always compute, never guess. Higher R = higher priority.
- ✅ **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — every cloud security control maps to one of these.
- ✅ **CSA Top Threats (Notorious Nine):** Data Breach, Data Loss, Account Hijacking, Insecure APIs, DoS, Malicious Insider, Abuse, Insufficient Due Diligence, Shared Tech Vulnerabilities.
- ✅ **Three Data States:** At Rest (AES-256), In Transit (TLS 1.3), In Use (Intel SGX / Confidential Computing).
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility:** As you move **IaaS → PaaS → SaaS**, the customer responsibility shrinks and CSP responsibility grows.
- ✅ **AAA Framework:** Authentication (who you are) + Authorization (what you can do) + Accounting (audit logs).
