- ✅ **KMS Lifecycle (7 stages):** Generate → Store → Distribute → Use → Rotate → Revoke → Destroy.
- ✅ **Key Rotation Interval:** 90 days for data keys (industry standard).
- ✅ **Hybrid Encryption:** Asymmetric (RSA/ECC) is used **once** to wrap a symmetric (AES) session key, which then encrypts the bulk data — the foundation of TLS 1.3.
- ✅ **Tokenization vs Masking:** Tokenization replaces with a non-reversible token (vault-mapped); Masking hides with realistic but fake data (no vault).
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Perimeter → Network → Identity → Data → Application → Monitoring — 6 layers, each independently catching what the previous one missed.
- ✅ **BYOK / HYOK:** Bring Your Own Key / Hold Your Own Key — customer-controlled key management for sensitive workloads (sovereign cloud, regulated industries).
- ✅ **CASB:** Cloud Access Security Broker — sits between user and CSP to enforce security policies across multiple cloud services.
- ✅ **DPDP Act 2023 (India):** Data Protection Digital Personal Data Act — mandates that personal data of Indian citizens must be protected with similar rigor to GDPR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing Tools - Tools and Technologies for Cloud, Apache Hadoop, Cloud Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Tools - Tools and Technologies for Cloud, Apache Hadoop, Cloud Tools # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Cloud Computing Tools — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > *Cloud Computing Tools* refer to a curated set of software frameworks, platforms, services, and utilities that enable the **development, deployment, monitoring, management, and scaling** of applications and data infrastructure over distributed cloud environments. They span categories such as **data processing engines, storage managers, orchestration systems, virtualization layers, DevOps pipelines, and analytics platforms**. According to the **NIST SP 800-145** reference model, cloud tools are the enablers of the five essential characteristics — *On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, and Measured service*. ## 1.2 Apache Hadoop — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > *Apache Hadoop* is an **open-source, distributed, fault-tolerant framework** that allows for the **storage (HDFS)** and **batch processing (MapReduce / YARN)** of extremely large datasets (Big Data) across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is the **de-facto reference implementation** for distributed data processing on commodity hardware and forms the foundation of nearly every modern cloud data lake. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "City Library System":** > Imagine a city with **millions of books** that no single library can hold. > - The books are split into chapters and stored in **many small local libraries** (HDFS DataNodes). > - Each library keeps **photocopies of rare books** in partner libraries so that if one burns down, the knowledge survives (**replication factor = 3**). > - A **central librarian (NameNode)** keeps the index — *"which book is in which library"*. > - When a researcher asks *"Count the occurrences of the word 'Eclipse' in every astronomy book"*, the central librarian dispatches **junior assistants (Map tasks)** to every local library to count in parallel. The counts are then **shuffled and combined (Reduce tasks)** at a sorting centre. > > This is **exactly** how Hadoop splits, distributes, and processes petabytes of data in parallel across a cluster. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bold-Weighted) - **DataNode** — Worker node that stores actual data blocks. - **NameNode** — Master node that holds filesystem metadata. - **JobTracker / ApplicationMaster** — Coordinator of MapReduce jobs. - **Block Size (default 128 MB in HDFS v2.x, 256 MB in v3.x)**. - **Replication Factor (default = 3)**. - **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) — Cluster resource manager. - **Throughput** measured in MB/s, IOPS, or records/sec. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Apache Hadoop Apache Hadoop is built on **four core modules** that together enable distributed data processing: ### 2.1.1 HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System - A **write-once, read-many** filesystem designed for streaming access to large files. - Files are split into **fixed-size blocks** (default **128 MB**) and distributed across the cluster. - Each block is **replicated** across multiple DataNodes (default **RF = 3**) for fault tolerance. - A single **NameNode** maintains the namespace metadata (`fsimage` + `edit logs`). ### 2.1.2 MapReduce — The Programming Paradigm The data processing model operates in **two functional phases**: 1. **Map Phase** — Input is partitioned, and a user-defined `map()` function emits `(key, value)` pairs in parallel on each split. 2. **Reduce Phase** — All values for a given key are shuffled, sorted, and aggregated by a user-defined `reduce()` function. ### 2.1.3 YARN — Cluster Resource Manager - Decouples **resource management** from **job scheduling/monitoring**. - Comprises: **ResourceManager (RM)**, **NodeManager (NM)**, and **ApplicationMaster (AM)** per application. ### 2.1.4 Hadoop Common - The set of utilities and libraries that support the other Hadoop modules (e.g., RPC, serialization, file I/O). ## 2.2 Other Prominent Cloud Computing Tools | Category | Tool / Technology | Primary Function | |----------|-------------------|------------------| | **Container Orchestration** | Kubernetes (K8s), Docker Swarm | Automated deployment, scaling & management of containers | | **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** | Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, Ansible | Declarative provisioning of cloud resources | | **Monitoring & Logging** | Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack | Metrics, dashboards, log aggregation | | **Big Data Processing** | Apache Spark, Apache Flink, Apache Storm | In-memory / stream processing on YARN or K8s | | **Serverless Frameworks** | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions | Event-driven, FaaS execution | | **DevOps / CI-CD** | Jenkins, GitLab CI, ArgoCD, Spinnaker | Continuous integration and delivery pipelines | | **Data Storage** | HDFS, Amazon S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage | Object / distributed storage | | **NoSQL Databases** | HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, DynamoDB | Schema-less, horizontally scalable data stores | | **Message Queues** | Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS | Asynchronous event streaming | | **Security & IAM** | Keycloak, Vault, AWS IAM, Azure AD | Identity, secrets, and access management | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes the **most important quantitative relationships** tested in KTU ESE for this topic. **Memorize** the units and boundary conditions. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit / Value | Notes | |---------|---------------------|----------------------|-------| | **HDFS Block Count** | $\text{Number of Blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | integer | Always round **UP** | | **Total Storage with Replication** | $S_{total} = S_{logical} \times RF$ | GB, TB, PB | $RF$ = Replication Factor | | **HDFS Storage Overhead** | $\text{Overhead \%} = (RF - 1) \times 100$ | percent | For $RF=3$: **200% overhead** | | **Cluster Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Total Data Processed}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | MB/s, GB/min | Job-level metric | | **MapReduce Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | ratio | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of mappers | | **Data Locality Cost** | $C_{local} \ll C_{cross-rack} \ll C_{cross-DC}$ | dimensionless ratio | DataNode proximity reduces I/O latency | | **Hadoop Rack Awareness** | Replicas on **2 racks, 1/rack, 1/rack** | placement policy | Minimizes cross-rack traffic | | **YARN Container Memory** | $M_{container} \leq M_{node} - M_{OS} - M_{hadoop\,services}$ | MB / GB | Per-container allocation | | **Cost per GB-month (Cloud Storage)** | $C_{storage} = \dfrac{\text{Monthly \$ Bill}}{\text{Total GB Stored}}$ | \$/GB-month | Tiered pricing applies | | **Effective Cost (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{infra} + C_{ops} + C_{power} + C_{license}$ | \$/year | Capital + Operational | > [!NOTE] > **NEVER** write the absolute-value symbol as a single pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell. Use `$\lvert x \rvert$` or `$x_{abs}$` in LaTeX instead. The formula $\lvert \log_2 N \rvert$ is a common KTU value. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Healthcare**: Hadoop + Spark on AWS EMR processes **genomic sequences** (terabytes per patient) for precision medicine. - **Finance**: Kafka + Flink on Azure HDInsight performs **real-time fraud detection** on millions of credit-card transactions per second. - **Retail**: Spark MLlib on GCP Dataproc generates **personalized recommendations** from clickstream logs. - **IoT & Smart Cities**: HBase + Storm ingest **sensor telemetry** for traffic and pollution analytics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — HDFS Block Count and Storage Consumption > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** A file of size **F = 1.7 GB** is stored in HDFS with block size **B = 128 MB** and replication factor **RF = 3**. Compute the (a) number of blocks, and (b) total physical storage consumed. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Number of Blocks $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil $$ Substitute $F = 1.7 \text{ GB} = 1700 \text{ MB}$ and $B = 128 \text{ MB}$: $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{1700}{128} \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil 13.28125 \right\rceil = 14 $$ > **Interpretation:** 13 full blocks × 128 MB + 1 partial block of 44 MB = 14 blocks. ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Total Physical Storage with Replication $$ S_{total} = F \times RF $$ $$ S_{total} = 1.7 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 5.1 \text{ GB} $$ > **Insight:** The **storage overhead** = $S_{total} - F = 5.1 - 1.7 = 3.4$ GB, i.e. **200% overhead**, which exactly matches $(RF - 1) \times 100\%$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Applied to MapReduce > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** A MapReduce job spends **P = 90%** of its execution in parallelizable `map()` tasks. If we increase the cluster mapper count from **N = 1** to **N = 100**, what is the maximum theoretical speedup? ### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law $$ S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}} $$ ### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Substitute N = 1 (Sequential Baseline) $$ S(1) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \frac{0.9}{1}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.9} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 $$ > **Interpretation:** With 1 mapper, no speedup — this is the baseline. ### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Substitute N = 100 $$ S(100) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \frac{0.9}{100}} $$ $$ S(100) = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.009} = \frac{1}{0.109} $$ $$ S(100) \approx 9.17 $$ > **Interpretation:** Even with infinite mappers, the theoretical limit is $\frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$. The 10% sequential portion (shuffle/reduce/setup) becomes the bottleneck. ### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Practical Inference The job can never scale beyond **10×** no matter how large the cluster grows. This motivates **partitioning, combiners, and speculative execution** in Hadoop tuning. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — YARN Container Memory Allocation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** A DataNode has **64 GB RAM**. The OS reserves 4 GB, Hadoop daemons (DataNode + NodeManager) consume 6 GB, and we want to run containers of size **8 GB each**. How many containers can the node host? ### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Available Memory for Containers $$ M_{avail} = M_{node} - M_{OS} - M_{daemons} $$ $$ M_{avail} = 64 - 4 - 6 = 54 \text{ GB} $$ ### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Maximum Concurrent Containers $$ N_{containers} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{avail}}{M_{container}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{54}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 6.75 \right\rfloor = 6 $$ > **Interpretation:** Only **6 containers** can run simultaneously; the remaining **6 GB** is reserved by YARN's `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` overhead buffer. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Decision Table | Use Case | Best Tool | Justification | |----------|-----------|---------------| | **Batch log analytics (TB scale)** | Apache Hadoop (HDFS + MapReduce) | Mature, fault-tolerant, scales linearly | | **Iterative ML (in-memory)** | Apache Spark on YARN/K8s | 100× faster than MapReduce for iterative jobs | | **Real-time event streaming** | Apache Kafka + Apache Flink | Sub-second latency, exactly-once semantics | | **Containerized microservices** | Kubernetes + Docker | Auto-scaling, self-healing, declarative | | **Configuration management** | Ansible / Terraform | Agentless IaC, idempotent playbooks | | **Monitoring & alerting** | Prometheus + Grafana | Pull-based metrics, powerful dashboards | | **Secret management** | HashiCorp Vault | Dynamic secrets, fine-grained ACLs | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hadoop Cluster — High-Level Architecture ```mermaid graph TB Client[Client Application] subgraph MasterNode["Master Node Layer"] NN["NameNode
HDFS Metadata + fsimage + edit logs"] RM["ResourceManager
YARN Global Scheduler"] AM["ApplicationMaster
Per-Job Coordinator"] end subgraph SlaveNode1["Slave Node 1"] DN1["DataNode 1
Stores Blocks"] NM1["NodeManager 1
Container Manager"] end subgraph SlaveNode2["Slave Node 2"] DN2["DataNode 2
Stores Blocks"] NM2["NodeManager 2
Container Manager"] end subgraph SlaveNode3["Slave Node 3"] DN3["DataNode 3
Stores Blocks"] NM3["NodeManager 3
Container Manager"] end Client -->|"Submit Job"| RM Client -->|"Read/Write"| NN NN -->|"Block Reports"| DN1 NN -->|"Block Reports"| DN2 NN -->|"Block Reports"| DN3 RM -->|"Allocate Containers"| AM AM -->|"Launch Containers"| NM1 AM -->|"Launch Containers"| NM2 AM -->|"Launch Containers"| NM3 DN1 -.->|"Replicate Blocks"| DN2 DN2 -.->|"Replicate Blocks"| DN3 DN3 -.->|"Replicate Blocks"| DN1 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** represent control-plane traffic (metadata, scheduling), while the **dashed arrows** represent data-plane replication traffic. ## 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow — End-to-End Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR InputFile["Input File
e.g. log.txt"] Split1["Input Split 1"] Split2["Input Split 2"] Split3["Input Split 3"] Map1["Map Task 1
emit k,v pairs"] Map2["Map Task 2
emit k,v pairs"] Map3["Map Task 3
emit k,v pairs"] Shuffle["Shuffle and Sort
Group by Key"] Reduce1["Reduce Task 1
Aggregate per key"] Reduce2["Reduce Task 2
Aggregate per key"] Output1["Output Part 1
HDFS File"] Output2["Output Part 2
HDFS File"] InputFile --> Split1 InputFile --> Split2 InputFile --> Split3 Split1 --> Map1 Split2 --> Map2 Split3 --> Map3 Map1 --> Shuffle Map2 --> Shuffle Map3 --> Shuffle Shuffle --> Reduce1 Shuffle --> Reduce2 Reduce1 --> Output1 Reduce2 --> Output2 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline progresses through four canonical stages — **Input → Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce → Output**. Each stage is a **barrier**, meaning the next stage cannot begin until all upstream tasks complete. ## 4.3 HDFS Block Replication & Rack-Aware Placement ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Rack1["Rack 1"] DN1a["DataNode A
Local Replica"] DN1b["DataNode B
2nd Replica"] end subgraph Rack2["Rack 2"] DN2a["DataNode C
3rd Replica on Different Rack"] end Writer["Client Writer"] -->|"Block Write 1"| DN1a DN1a -->|"Pipeline Replicate"| DN1b DN1a -->|"Cross-Rack Replicate"| DN2a ``` > **Placement Policy (HDFS default):** The first replica goes on the **local** node (or a random node if the writer is off-cluster). The second replica goes on a **different node on the same rack**. The third replica goes on a **different rack**. Additional replicas are placed randomly subject to constraints. ## 4.4 Cloud Tool Ecosystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Presentation["Presentation & User Layer"] UI1["Web Console / CLI / API"] end subgraph Orchestration["Orchestration and IaC Layer"] T1["Terraform"] T2["Ansible"] T3["Kubernetes Controller"] end subgraph Application["Application and Runtime Layer"] A1["Microservices in Containers"] A2["Serverless Functions FaaS"] A3["Spark / Hadoop Jobs"] end subgraph DataPlane["Data Plane Layer"] D1["Object Storage S3 / Blob"] D2["NoSQL Database HBase / Cassandra"] D3["Streaming Queue Kafka"] end subgraph Observability["Observability Layer"] O1["Prometheus Metrics"] O2["Grafana Dashboards"] O3["ELK Logging"] end subgraph Security["Security and Governance Layer"] S1["IAM / RBAC"] S2["Vault Secrets"] S3["Keycloak SSO"] end UI1 --> Orchestration Orchestration --> Application Application --> DataPlane Application --> Observability Orchestration --> Security DataPlane --> Security Observability --> Security ``` > **Reading the diagram:** This is a **layered cloud-native reference architecture** where every layer is independently scalable and replaces the monolithic traditional 3-tier design. The **Observability** and **Security** planes cut **horizontally** across all other layers (a "sidecar" pattern). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *List any three core components of Apache Hadoop and briefly state the role of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Provides distributed, fault-tolerant storage by splitting files into blocks and replicating them across DataNodes. *(1 mark)* 2. **MapReduce** — A programming model for parallel batch processing consisting of `Map` and `Reduce` phases. *(1 mark)* 3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — Performs cluster resource management and job scheduling, decoupling resource allocation from application logic. *(1 mark)* > *(Acceptable alternative: Hadoop Common as the third component — utilities and libraries supporting the other modules.)* --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *Differentiate between *On-premise* infrastructure and *Cloud* infrastructure with two points relevant to cloud-tools adoption.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | On-Premise | Cloud | |--------|-----------|-------| | **Provisioning Time** | Weeks/months (hardware procurement) | Minutes (API/portal click) | | **Capital vs Operational Expense** | High CapEx (upfront hardware) | Pure OpEx (pay-as-you-go) | | **Elasticity** | Fixed capacity, manual scaling | Auto-scaling via cloud tools (K8s, ASG) | | **Tool Ecosystem** | Limited proprietary tools | Rich: Terraform, Spark, Kafka, Prometheus | *(2 marks for any two crisp distinguishing points; 1 mark for relevant tool-name example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Explain the HDFS architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss block replication and rack-awareness policy.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) **HDFS Architecture (4 marks):** HDFS follows a **master-slave (master-worker) architecture**: - **NameNode (Master)**: Maintains the filesystem tree, metadata, and the mapping of file → block → DataNode. It stores two files: `fsimage` (checkpoint) and `edit logs` (recent changes). The NameNode does **not** store actual data. - **DataNode (Slave/Worker)**: Stores the actual data blocks. Periodically sends a **block report** and **heartbeat** (default every 3 seconds) to the NameNode. - **Secondary NameNode**: Periodically merges `fsimage` and `edit logs` to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded. It is **not** a hot standby. **Block Replication (2 marks):** - Default **block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); default **replication factor = 3**. - When a client writes a block, replication is handled by a **replication pipeline** — the first DataNode forwards the block to the second, which forwards to the third. - If any replica is lost (heartbeat failure), the NameNode triggers **re-replication** to restore the desired RF. **Rack-Awareness Policy (1 mark):** - Replica 1 → **Local node** (or random node if writer is off-cluster) - Replica 2 → **Different node, same rack** - Replica 3 → **Different rack** - *Benefit*: Minimizes cross-rack network traffic (inter-rack bandwidth is ~10× costlier than intra-rack). *(Full marks include a labelled HDFS diagram showing NameNode, DataNodes, and a client.)* --- **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of the MapReduce programming model. Use a Word-Count example to illustrate the Map and Reduce phases.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **MapReduce Overview (2 marks):** MapReduce is a **distributed data-processing paradigm** that splits a job into two functional stages executed in parallel across a cluster. **Map Phase (2 marks):** - Input file is split into **input splits** (typically 64 MB or 128 MB). - Each split is processed by a **Mapper**, which invokes `map(key, value)` for every record. - The mapper emits zero or more intermediate `(key, value)` pairs. - **Word-Count Map output**: `("hadoop", 1)`, `("cloud", 1)`, `("hadoop", 1)` … **Shuffle & Sort Phase (1 mark):** - The framework groups all values by key and partitions them to reducers. - All occurrences of the same key are routed to the **same Reducer**. **Reduce Phase (2 marks):** - Each Reducer invokes `reduce(key, iterator)`. - It aggregates the values and writes the final output to HDFS. - **Word-Count Reduce output**: `("hadoop", 2)`, `("cloud", 1)` … *(A diagram of the Map → Shuffle → Reduce pipeline earns the remaining diagram-related marks.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) Discuss any seven major categories of cloud computing tools with one example tool for each category and its primary use.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) | # | Category | Example Tool | Primary Use | |---|----------|--------------|-------------| | 1 | **Container Orchestration** | Kubernetes (K8s) | Automated deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications | | 2 | **Infrastructure as Code** | Terraform | Declarative provisioning of multi-cloud resources via HCL scripts | | 3 | **Big Data Processing** | Apache Spark | In-memory distributed data processing for batch and streaming | | 4 | **Monitoring & Observability** | Prometheus + Grafana | Metrics collection, alerting, and visualization dashboards | | 5 | **CI/CD & DevOps** | Jenkins / ArgoCD | Continuous integration, delivery, and GitOps deployment | | 6 | **NoSQL Databases** | Apache HBase | Wide-column, horizontally scalable database on top of HDFS | | 7 | **Message Streaming** | Apache Kafka | Distributed, fault-tolerant event-streaming platform | | 8 | **Identity & Security** | HashiCorp Vault | Secrets management and dynamic credential generation | *(Each correctly explained row: 1 mark × 7 = 7 marks.)* --- **(b) Explain the YARN architecture with a block diagram. Describe the roles of ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster.** *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **YARN — Need & Overview (1 mark):** YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) was introduced in Hadoop 2.x to overcome the **scalability bottleneck** of the classic JobTracker/TaskTracker model. It separates **resource management** from **application lifecycle management**. **ResourceManager (RM) — Cluster Master (2 marks):** - Runs on the master node. It has two sub-components: - **Scheduler** — Allocates cluster resources (CPU, memory) to applications based on capacity/fairness policies. - **ApplicationsManager (AsM)** — Accepts job submissions and launches the ApplicationMaster. - RM is the **single source of truth** for cluster resources. **NodeManager (NM) — Per-Slave Agent (2 marks):** - Runs on every slave node. - Manages **containers** (resource envelopes) — start, monitor, and kill them. - Sends periodic **heartbeats** to the RM with container status. - Enforces resource isolation via `cgroups` on Linux. **ApplicationMaster (AM) — Per-Job Master (2 marks):** - One AM is spawned **per application**. - Negotiates resources with the RM. - Works with the NMs to execute and monitor individual tasks (Map/Reduce). - Reports progress back to the RM. *(A labelled block diagram of YARN earns the remaining marks; mention that containers = actual task-execution slots.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with a Standby NameNode** — In Hadoop 1.x, there is no HA. Secondary NameNode is **not** a hot standby; it only merges the edit log. *(Lose 1 mark if confused.)* > 2. **Forgetting the "3-2-1" Replication Policy** — Many students write "3 replicas" but forget to mention rack-awareness (2 racks, 1 same + 1 different). > 3. **MapReduce ≠ HDFS** — These are **two separate modules** of Hadoop. Don't merge them in diagrams. > 4. **YARN without ResourceManager components** — Always mention **Scheduler + ApplicationsManager** as the two sub-parts of RM. > 5. **Amdahl's Law misapplication** — Always state the **sequential fraction (1 - P)**; speedup cannot exceed $\frac{1}{1-P}$. > 6. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions** — Even a hand-drawn ASCII diagram earns 1–2 marks; an absent diagram typically loses 2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** ### A. Core Concepts - **Cloud Tools** = software enabling **build, deploy, monitor, scale** on cloud. - **Hadoop** = open-source framework for **distributed storage + batch processing** of Big Data. - **4 Hadoop Modules** → HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, Hadoop Common. ### B. HDFS — Must-Know - **Block size = 128 MB** (default), **RF = 3** (default). - **NameNode** = master metadata; **DataNode** = slave data storage. - **Rack-awareness** = 2 replicas on 1 rack, 1 replica on another. - **Heartbeat** every 3 seconds; lost heartbeat → block re-replication. ### C. MapReduce — Must-Know - Stages: **Input Split → Map → Shuffle/Sort → Reduce → Output**. - **Combiner** = mini-reducer run on mapper output to reduce shuffle volume. - **Partitioner** = decides which reducer a key goes to (default = `hash(key) mod R`). ### D. YARN — Must-Know - **RM** = Scheduler + ApplicationsManager (global master). - **NM** = per-node container manager. - **AM** = per-application coordinator. ### E. Tool Categories - **Orchestration** → Kubernetes, Docker Swarm. - **IaC** → Terraform, Ansible. - **Big Data** → Spark, Flink, Storm. - **Storage** → HDFS, S3, Azure Blob. - **Streaming** → Kafka, RabbitMQ. - **Monitoring** → Prometheus, Grafana, ELK. ### F. Key Formulas - $\text{Blocks} = \lceil F / B \rceil$ - $S_{total} = F \times RF$ - $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$ - $\text{Overhead} = (RF - 1) \times 100\%$ ### G. Common Exam Buzzwords - **"Write once, read many"** — HDFS design principle. - **"Data locality"** — process data where it is stored. - **"Commodity hardware"** — Hadoop runs on cheap, off-the-shelf servers. - **"Schema-on-read"** — Hadoop's flexibility vs. RDBMS schema-on-write. - **"Cloud-native"** — built for containers, microservices, and dynamic orchestration. > 🎯 **Last-Minute Tip:** Before entering the exam hall, write down on a chit: *"HDFS = 128 MB blocks, RF = 3, NameNode + DataNode. MapReduce = Map → Shuffle → Reduce. YARN = RM + NM + AM. Amdahl = 1 / ((1-P) + P/N)."* — These four lines cover **~70%** of typical 14-mark Part B questions on this topic.

topics.title: Cloud Applications - Moving Applications to the Cloud, Microsoft Cloud Services, Google Cloud Applications, Amazon Cloud Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT746 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Applications - Moving Applications to the Cloud, Microsoft Cloud Services, Google Cloud Applications, Amazon Cloud Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Applications — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Cloud Applications** are network-based, distributed, virtualized, and dynamically scalable software systems that are delivered as services over the internet. They leverage the pooled resources of cloud infrastructure (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and are characterized by multi-tenancy, elasticity, pay-per-use economics, and high availability.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEADT746)**, *Cloud Applications* is a board-exam-tested term that specifically refers to **end-user / business applications hosted on public cloud platforms** (Azure, GCP, AWS) and the engineering process of *moving* (migrating) existing on-premise applications into these cloud environments.

### 1.1.1 The Three Major "Hyperscale" Cloud Application Providers

| Provider | Cloud Brand | Parent Company | Launch Year | Global Market Share (Q4 2024, Synergy Research) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Amazon** | **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | Amazon.com | **2006** | ~31 % |
| **Microsoft** | **Microsoft Azure** | Microsoft Corp. | **2010** | ~24 % |
| **Google** | **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** | Alphabet (Google) | **2008** | ~11 % |

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining ~34 % is split among Alibaba Cloud, Oracle Cloud, IBM Cloud, Tencent Cloud, and others. KTU examiners frequently test the *3-provider comparison* matrix (Compute, Storage, Database, AI/ML, Networking).

---

## 1.2 What is "Moving Applications to the Cloud"? (Cloud Migration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)**
> **Cloud Migration** is the strategic process of relocating an enterprise's on-premise application portfolio — including its code, data, runtime, middleware, operating system, and virtualization layer — to a cloud infrastructure (public, private, hybrid, or community), while preserving functionality, improving agility, and optimizing Total Cost of Ownership (TCO).

### Real-World Analogy — The "House Relocation" Analogy 🏠➡️🏢

Imagine you have been running your own **kitchen at home** (on-premise server) for years. You buy groceries, cook, wash utensils, maintain the stove, fix the plumbing, pay electricity, and only your family eats there. This is reliable but **expensive, limited, and stressful**.

Now a *cloud kitchen* (AWS/Azure/GCP) opens next door:
- You stop buying stoves and pans → **Infrastructure is rented**.
- A chef (managed service) cooks for you → **PaaS/FaaS**.
- You just order meals from a menu and pay-per-plate → **SaaS + Pay-per-use**.

The act of **packing your recipes, choosing the kitchen, transporting, validating taste, and finally closing your home kitchen** is exactly what **Cloud Migration** means in software engineering. Some recipes you fully copy (Rehost / "lift-and-shift"), some you re-write to use the kitchen's smarter ovens (Refactor), and some you outsource entirely to a catering specialist (Replace with SaaS).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love the **"6 R's of Cloud Migration"** coined by AWS. Memorize them — they appear in **2-mark, 3-mark and 14-mark** questions every semester.

### The 6 R's of Cloud Migration (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Rehost** — Lift-and-shift (move VMs as-is to EC2/Azure VMs).
2. **Replatform** — Minor optimizations (e.g., move Oracle → Aurora, move WebLogic → Tomcat).
3. **Refactor / Re-architect** — Rewrite to be cloud-native (microservices, containers, serverless).
4. **Repurchase** — Drop legacy license, buy SaaS (e.g., own CRM → Salesforce).
5. **Retire** — Decommission applications no longer needed.
6. **Relocate** — Move to a different physical datacenter (e.g., Hyper-V → VMware on Azure — *Project Azure VMware Solution*).

---

## 1.3 Why Move Applications to the Cloud? (Business Drivers)

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects the following **5 business drivers** in any "justify migration" question:

- **Cost Transformation** — CapEx → OpEx (no upfront hardware purchase).
- **Business Agility** — Provision a server in **minutes**, not **weeks**.
- **Scalability & Elasticity** — Auto-scale from 2 to 2000 instances under load.
- **Innovation Enablement** — Instant access to AI/ML, IoT, Big Data, Blockchain.
- **Global Reach & Resilience** — Deploy across 30+ geographic regions, with SLAs of **99.99 %** availability (commonly called "four nines" — equals ~52.6 minutes downtime/year).

---

## 1.4 Pre-Migration Assessment — The CMAC Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CMAC** framework is taught under PEADT746 Module 4 for *analyzing an application* before migration.

| Phase | Meaning | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| **C — Cloud Readiness Assessment** | Identify which apps are cloud-ready | Readiness scorecard |
| **M — Migration Strategy Selection** | Pick 1 of the 6 R's per workload | Migration roadmap |
| **A — Architecture & Design** | Target cloud architecture diagrams | HLD / LLD documents |
| **C — Cost & Performance Modeling** | TCO, ROI, capacity planning | Business case document |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Cloud Migration Methodology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Microsoft/AWS CAF (Cloud Adoption Framework) aligned 6-step process** that KTU examiners expect for full-mark answers in 14-mark questions.

### Step 1 — Strategy & Define
- Identify business outcomes (faster time-to-market, geographic expansion).
- Build a **business case** with measurable KPIs.
- Identify stakeholders: CIO, CTO, CISO, DevOps lead, application owners.

### Step 2 — Discover & Assess
- Inventory all applications (CMDB, ServiceNow, manual audit).
- Categorize each by: criticality, compliance, dependencies, performance, data gravity.
- Tools: **AWS Application Discovery Service**, **Azure Migrate**, **Migrate for Compute Engine (Google)**.

### Step 3 — Plan & Design
- Map each application to one of the **6 R's**.
- Select target **cloud account structure** (single-account vs. multi-account with AWS Organizations / Azure Management Groups).
- Plan **landing zone**: VPCs, subnets, IAM, encryption, logging (CloudTrail / Azure Monitor / Cloud Audit Logs).

### Step 4 — Migrate (The 4 Migration Patterns)

| Pattern | Description | Tools (AWS / Azure / GCP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Rehost (Bulk)** | Server-to-server copy | AWS SMS, Azure Migrate Server Migration, Migrate to Virtual Machines |
| **Replatform** | Database conversion, OS swap | AWS DMS, Azure Database Migration Service, Database Migration Service |
| **Refactor** | Containerize / microservices | EKS / AKS / GKE, Lambda / Azure Functions / Cloud Functions |
| **Repurchase** | Move to SaaS | AppExchange, AppSource, Google Workspace Marketplace |

### Step 5 — Validate (Test & Optimize)
- Functional testing, performance testing, security testing, DR drill.
- **Right-sizing** of compute (don't over-provision).
- Set up **CloudWatch / Azure Monitor / Cloud Monitoring** dashboards.

### Step 6 — Operate & Innovate
- Adopt **Well-Architected Framework** (AWS 5 pillars, Azure WAF, Google Cloud Architecture Framework).
- Continuous FinOps, SecOps, DevOps.

---

## 2.2 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Analysis — The KTU "Formula Sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> Although Cloud Computing is not math-heavy, the following 4 formulas are **guaranteed** in KTU 14-mark numerical/analytical questions on cloud migration. Memorize them.

### Formula 1 — Total Cost of Ownership (3-Year TCO)

$$
TCO_{3yr} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ C_{infra,i} + C_{license,i} + C_{power,i} + C_{cooling,i} + C_{admin,i} + C_{facility,i} \right] - C_{residual}
$$

Where:
- $C_{infra,i}$ = Hardware/server cost per application *i*
- $C_{license,i}$ = Software license (Windows, SQL, Oracle, etc.)
- $C_{power,i}$ = Electricity cost (typically **\$0.10/kWh** in US benchmarks)
- $C_{cooling,i}$ = Cooling cost ≈ **0.5 × Power cost** (PUE ≈ 1.5)
- $C_{admin,i}$ = FTE salaries allocated (~$120k/yr per FTE)
- $C_{facility,i}$ = Datacenter colocation rent
- $C_{residual}$ = Salvage value of hardware after 3 years

### Formula 2 — Pay-Per-Use Cloud Cost (Monthly)

$$
C_{cloud,monthly} = (N_{instances} \times P_{hourly} \times U_{hours}) + C_{storage} + C_{egress} + C_{API}
$$

Where:
- $N_{instances}$ = number of VMs / containers
- $P_{hourly}$ = on-demand hourly price (e.g., AWS m5.large = **\$0.096/hr**)
- $U_{hours}$ = monthly usage hours (730 nominal, varies with utilization)
- $C_{egress}$ = data transfer OUT (the famous "egress tax", **\$0.09/GB** in AWS)

### Formula 3 — Availability (Uptime) Conversion

$$
Availability (\%) = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100
$$

$$
Downtime_{year} = (1 - Availability) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite**: *"How many minutes of downtime per year does 99.9% ('three nines') SLA allow?"*
> Answer: $(1 - 0.999) \times 525600 = 525.6$ minutes (≈ 8.76 hours/year).

### Formula 4 — Migration ROI

$$
ROI_{migration} = \frac{(TCO_{on-prem} - TCO_{cloud}) - Cost_{migration}}{Cost_{migration}} \times 100\%
$$

A **positive ROI** justifies migration. Most enterprises target an ROI of **> 200 %** over 3 years.

## 2.3 The Three Hyperscaler Service Catalogs (KTU Comparison Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 4. Memorize the exact service names — examiners test naming conventions.

| Service Category | **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | **Microsoft Azure** | **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Virtual Machines** | EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) | Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| **Serverless Functions** | Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions |
| **Containers (Managed K8s)** | EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) | AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) | GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine) |
| **Object Storage** | S3 (Simple Storage Service) | Blob Storage | Cloud Storage (GCS) |
| **Block Storage** | EBS (Elastic Block Store) | Managed Disks | Persistent Disk |
| **Relational DB** | RDS, Aurora | Azure SQL, Cosmos DB (NoSQL) | Cloud SQL, Spanner (global) |
| **NoSQL DB** | DynamoDB | Cosmos DB | Firestore, Bigtable |
| **Data Warehouse** | Redshift | Synapse Analytics | BigQuery |
| **CDN** | CloudFront | Azure CDN / Front Door | Cloud CDN |
| **DNS** | Route 53 | Azure DNS | Cloud DNS |
| **Identity / IAM** | IAM + Cognito | Entra ID (Azure AD) | Cloud IAM + Identity Platform |
| **AI/ML Platform** | SageMaker | Azure Machine Learning | Vertex AI |
| **IoT** | AWS IoT Core | Azure IoT Hub | Cloud IoT Core (deprecated → partners) |
| **Networking (VPC)** | VPC | Virtual Network (VNet) | VPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick**: *S3 → Simple Storage, S in S3 = Simple.* *EC2 → Elastic Compute Cloud, the "2" = "to" = EC-to.* *EBS → Elastic Block Store — Block = Block storage.*

## 2.4 Service Models Used for Cloud Applications

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects you to map **any cloud application** to one of these 3 models.

| Model | Who Manages What | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) | User manages OS, runtime, app, data. Provider manages servers, storage, networking. | Running a Windows VM on EC2 with your own IIS + ASP.NET app |
| **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) | User manages only app + data. Provider manages everything else. | Deploying a Spring Boot JAR to Azure App Service |
| **SaaS** (Software as a Service) | Provider manages everything. User just consumes via browser/UI. | Microsoft 365, Gmail, Salesforce, Dropbox |
| **FaaS** (Functions as a Service) | Serverless; user ships only code, no server awareness. | AWS Lambda triggered by S3 upload |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 KTU Numerical Problem — Full Solved Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical 14-mark KTU numerical problem** on cloud migration TCO. Understand every line — examiners value step-by-step working.

### 📘 Problem Statement

> *"A KTU-affiliated engineering college currently hosts its Moodle LMS on 4 on-premise servers (each costing \$8,000 with 3-year depreciation, \$1,200/year in electricity, \$900/year in cooling, \$600/year in OS license, \$5,000/year per FTE administrator, and 0.5 FTE is allocated to the LMS). The college wants to migrate to AWS.*
> *AWS quotes: 4 × m5.large instances @ \$0.096/hour (running 24/7), 500 GB EBS storage @ \$0.10/GB-month, 2 TB monthly data egress @ \$0.09/GB, and \$50/month in CloudWatch logs.*
> *Migration cost (one-time) = \$15,000.*
> *Calculate: (a) 3-year TCO on-premise, (b) 3-year TCO on AWS, (c) ROI of migration."*

---

### Part (a) — 3-Year TCO On-Premise

> **Step 1 — Hardware Cost (Amortized per year)**
> Cost per server = \$8,000
> Useful life = 3 years
> Amortized cost per server per year = \$8,000 ÷ 3 = \$2,666.67
> For 4 servers: $4 \times 2{,}666.67 = 10{,}666.67$ per year
> Over 3 years: $10{,}666.67 \times 3 = 32{,}000.00$

> **Step 2 — Electricity Cost (per year)**
> Cost per server per year = \$1,200
> For 4 servers: $4 \times 1{,}200 = 4{,}800$ per year
> Over 3 years: $4{,}800 \times 3 = 14{,}400$

> **Step 3 — Cooling Cost (per year)**
> Cost per server per year = \$900
> For 4 servers: $4 \times 900 = 3{,}600$ per year
> Over 3 years: $3{,}600 \times 3 = 10{,}800$

> **Step 4 — OS License Cost (per year)**
> Cost per server per year = \$600
> For 4 servers: $4 \times 600 = 2{,}400$ per year
> Over 3 years: $2{,}400 \times 3 = 7{,}200$

> **Step 5 — Administrator Cost (per year)**
> Allocation = 0.5 FTE
> Cost per FTE per year = \$5,000
> Allocated cost per year: $0.5 \times 5{,}000 = 2{,}500$
> Over 3 years: $2{,}500 \times 3 = 7{,}500$

> **Step 6 — Sum the 3-Year TCO**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> TCO_{on-prem, 3yr} &= C_{hardware} + C_{electricity} + C_{cooling} + C_{license} + C_{admin} \\
> &= 32{,}000 + 14{,}400 + 10{,}800 + 7{,}200 + 7{,}500 \\
> &= 71{,}900
> \end{aligned}
> $$

### Part (b) — 3-Year TCO on AWS

> **Step 1 — Compute Cost (per month)**
> Hourly cost = \$0.096
> Hours per month (24/7) = 730
> Cost per instance per month: $0.096 \times 730 = 70.08$
> For 4 instances: $4 \times 70.08 = 280.32$/month
> Over 36 months: $280.32 \times 36 = 10{,}091.52$

> **Step 2 — EBS Storage Cost (per month)**
> 500 GB × \$0.10/GB-month = \$50/month
> Over 36 months: $50 \times 36 = 1{,}800$

> **Step 3 — Data Egress Cost (per month)**
> 2 TB = $2 \times 1024 = 2048$ GB
> Cost per month: $2048 \times 0.09 = 184.32$
> Over 36 months: $184.32 \times 36 = 6{,}635.52$

> **Step 4 — CloudWatch Logs Cost (per month)**
> \$50/month × 36 months = \$1,800

> **Step 5 — Sum the 3-Year TCO on AWS (excl. migration)**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> TCO_{cloud, 3yr} &= C_{compute} + C_{EBS} + C_{egress} + C_{logs} \\
> &= 10{,}091.52 + 1{,}800 + 6{,}635.52 + 1{,}800 \\
> &= 20{,}327.04
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 6 — Add One-Time Migration Cost**
> $TCO_{cloud, 3yr, total} = 20{,}327.04 + 15{,}000 = 35{,}327.04$

### Part (c) — Migration ROI

> **Step 1 — Compute Net Savings**
> $\text{Savings} = TCO_{on-prem} - TCO_{cloud,total} = 71{,}900 - 35{,}327.04 = 36{,}572.96$

> **Step 2 — Apply ROI Formula**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> ROI &= \frac{\text{Savings} - \text{Migration Cost}}{\text{Migration Cost}} \times 100\% \\
> &= \frac{36{,}572.96 - 15{,}000}{15{,}000} \times 100\% \\
> &= \frac{21{,}572.96}{15{,}000} \times 100\% \\
> &= 143.82\%
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 3 — Conclusion (Mandatory for full marks)**
> *"Since ROI ≈ 143.82 % (positive, well above the 100 % target threshold), migration of the Moodle LMS to AWS is **economically justified** over a 3-year horizon. Additional non-financial benefits include elasticity during exam season and high availability via multi-AZ deployment."*

---

## 3.2 Availability / SLA Tier Calculation — KTU Variant

> **Problem**: *"Compare annual downtime for the following SLAs: 99 % (one nine), 99.9 % (three nines), 99.95 %, 99.99 % (four nines), 99.999 % (five nines). Convert each to minutes/year."*

> **Step 1 — Common Constant**
> 
> $$
> \text{Minutes in a year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
> $$

> **Step 2 — Apply Formula** $Downtime = (1 - Availability) \times 525{,}600$

| SLA | Availability | Allowed Downtime (minutes/year) | Allowed Downtime (days/year) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 nine | 0.9900 | 5,256.00 | 3.65 days |
| 2 nines | 0.9990 | 525.60 | 8.77 hours |
| 3 nines | 0.9990 | 525.60 | 8.77 hours |
| **3 nines** | **0.9990** | **525.60** | **8.77 hours** |
| **4 nines** | **0.9999** | **52.56** | **52.6 minutes** |
| **5 nines** | **0.99999** | **5.26** | **5.26 minutes** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's most-favoured question: *"Which SLA does AWS EC2 single-region single-AZ offer?"* — Answer: **99.95 %** (≈ 4.38 hours/year). Multi-AZ raises it to **99.99 %**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Which Cloud Provider

> [!TIP]
> KTU 14-mark descriptive questions often end with "Which provider would you recommend and why?" Use this decision matrix.

| Requirement | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| .NET / Active Directory / Windows-heavy | **Azure** | Native Entra ID, Azure AD, Visual Studio integration |

| Tightest budget startup | **GCP** | Sustained-use discounts + committed-use discounts |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and explain any 3 of the 6 R's of cloud migration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 6 R's of cloud migration (defined by AWS) are the strategic patterns used when moving applications to the cloud. Any 3 of the following:

1. **Rehost (Lift-and-Shift)**: Move the application from on-premise to a cloud IaaS environment (e.g., EC2) with minimal or no code changes. Fastest and lowest risk. *Example*: VMware VM → AWS EC2 via AWS Server Migration Service.

2. **Replatform**: Make minor cloud-specific optimizations during migration without changing core architecture. *Example*: Move from Oracle DB → Amazon Aurora to reduce license cost; move WebSphere → Tomcat.

3. **Refactor (Re-architect)**: Re-architect the application to be cloud-native — typically using microservices, containers, and serverless. Highest long-term benefit, highest cost and risk. *Example*: Monolithic Java EE app split into Spring Boot microservices on EKS.

*(For full 3 marks, students may write any 3 R's with one real cloud example each.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS using a real cloud example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Model | Who Manages | Real Example |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | User manages OS, runtime, app, data; provider manages servers, storage, network | Amazon **EC2** (launch a Linux VM and install your own web server) |
| **PaaS** | User manages only app + data; provider manages everything else | Microsoft **Azure App Service** (deploy a Spring Boot JAR without managing the JVM/server) |
| **SaaS** | Provider manages everything; user only consumes | **Microsoft 365** (use Word/Excel in browser; nothing to install) |

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - **Common mistake 1**: Confusing **Rehost** with **Replatform** — read the question carefully, "with no code change" means Rehost, "minor optimization" means Replatform.
> - **Common mistake 2**: Writing only AWS or only Azure — the question may ask for **all 3 providers**. Always give a comparison table.
> - **Common mistake 3**: Skipping the **migration methodology steps** — these are mandatory for full marks (Strategy → Discover → Plan → Migrate → Validate → Operate).
> - **Common mistake 4**: Forgetting to mention **region, multi-AZ, and SLA** in the architecture diagram — these keywords carry 1–2 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **AWS service architecture** for hosting a scalable, highly available e-commerce web application. Cover compute, storage, database, CDN, and monitoring layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain the **6 R's of cloud application migration** with one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — AWS Architecture for E-Commerce Application (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Requirements Analysis** (1 mark)
- The application must handle variable traffic (festivals, sales), store customer data securely, deliver product images fast globally, and remain available even if one datacenter fails.

**Step 2 — Architecture Selection** (3 marks)
The application is hosted in **AWS Region: ap-south-1 (Mumbai)** with two Availability Zones for high availability.

| Layer | AWS Service | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge / CDN** | Amazon CloudFront | Caches product images at 600+ edge locations worldwide |
| **DNS** | Route 53 | Routes users to nearest region; health-check based failover |
| **Load Balancer** | Application Load Balancer (ALB) | Distributes HTTP traffic across multiple EC2 instances |
| **Compute (Web tier)** | EC2 instances (m5.large) in Auto Scaling Group | Auto-scales 2 → 50 instances based on CPU > 70 % |
| **Compute (App tier)** | EC2 / ECS Fargate containers | Runs business logic (cart, checkout, payment) |
| **Object Storage** | Amazon S3 (Standard + Intelligent-Tiering) | Stores product images, videos, invoices |
| **Database** | Amazon Aurora MySQL (Multi-AZ, 2 read replicas) | Transactional data with automatic failover |
| **Cache** | Amazon ElastiCache (Redis) | Reduces DB load for product catalog & sessions |
| **Async Queue** | Amazon SQS + SNS | Order processing, email notifications |
| **Monitoring** | CloudWatch + CloudTrail | Metrics, logs, audit trail |
| **Security** | IAM + WAF + KMS + Cognito | Role-based access, SQLi protection, encryption at rest/transit |
| **CI/CD** | CodePipeline + CodeBuild + CodeDeploy | Automated deployments |

**Step 3 — Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3)** (2 marks)
Refer to the **AWS Service Architecture** mermaid diagram. The examiner expects:

- User → CloudFront → ALB → EC2 (multi-AZ) → Aurora (multi-AZ) + S3
- CloudWatch monitoring all layers
- IAM securing all components

**Step 4 — SLA & Cost Summary** (1 mark)
- Multi-AZ deployment: **99.99 % SLA** (≈ 52.56 minutes/year downtime)
- Pay-per-use; auto-scaling prevents over-provisioning; S3 Intelligent-Tiering reduces storage cost by up to **40 %**.

---

#### Part (b) — 6 R's of Cloud Migration (7 marks)

Each R carries approximately **1 mark (definition + example)** = 6 marks, plus **1 mark** for an introductory sentence or summary table.

| R | Definition | Real Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Rehost** | "Lift-and-shift" — move application as-is to cloud IaaS with no code change | A VMware vSphere VM running Moodle is exported as OVA and imported to AWS EC2 using AWS Server Migration Service. |
| **2. Replatform** | Move with minor cloud-specific optimizations (no core code rewrite) | Migrate MySQL 5.7 on RDS → Amazon Aurora MySQL for 3× throughput and lower cost. |
| **3. Refactor / Re-architect** | Re-architect to be cloud-native — break monolith into microservices / serverless | Decompose a Java EE monolith into 12 Spring Boot microservices deployed on Amazon EKS with Lambda for image processing. |
| **4. Repurchase** | Drop a legacy license, adopt a SaaS product | Replace in-house CRM built on Lotus Notes → Salesforce; replace custom email server → Microsoft 365. |
| **5. Retire** | Decommission applications that are obsolete or rarely used | A 2012 internal "Employee Birthday Portal" used by 4 people — turned off after 1 month of analysis. |
| **6. Relocate** | Move workloads to a different physical infrastructure (e.g., on-prem VMware → VMware Cloud on AWS) | Migrate 200+ VMs from on-prem vSphere 7 to VMware Cloud on AWS using HCX without re-architecting. |

**Concluding statement (1 mark):** *The 6 R's framework helps enterprises classify each application and choose the most cost-effective migration path, balancing speed (Rehost) against long-term benefit (Refactor).*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With neat functional block diagrams, explain the service catalogs of **Microsoft Azure** and **Google Cloud Platform** for hosting a SaaS-based Learning Management System (LMS). Cover compute, storage, database, networking, and identity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup currently spends ₹18,00,000 per year on on-premise infrastructure (3-year total = ₹54,00,000). AWS quotes ₹16,00,000 per year. The one-time migration cost is ₹6,00,000. Calculate the **3-year TCO on AWS** including migration, and the **ROI of migration**. State whether the migration is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — Azure + GCP Architecture for SaaS LMS (7 marks)

**Azure Architecture** (3.5 marks)

| Layer | Azure Service |
|---|---|
| **Identity** | Microsoft **Entra ID** (Azure AD) — SSO for students & faculty |
| **Compute (Web)** | **Azure App Service** (PaaS) — hosts the LMS front-end (React/Angular) and backend (Node.js / .NET) |
| **Compute (Batch jobs)** | **Azure Functions** — serverless, e.g., generate report cards at semester end |
| **Containers** | **AKS** — if LMS is split into microservices |
| **Storage** | **Azure Blob Storage** (Hot tier) for videos, Cool tier for old course material |
| **Database** | **Azure SQL Database** (relational — student records, marks) + **Cosmos DB** (NoSQL — chat, discussion forum) |
| **CDN** | **Azure Front Door** — global CDN for video lectures |
| **Networking** | **Azure Virtual Network (VNet)**, NSG, Application Gateway WAF |
| **Monitoring** | **Azure Monitor** + **Log Analytics** |
| **DevOps** | **Azure DevOps** (Repos, Pipelines, Boards) |

**GCP Architecture** (3.5 marks)

| Layer | GCP Service |
|---|---|
| **Identity** | **Cloud IAM** + **Identity Platform** — supports Google login & SAML |
| **Compute (Web)** | **Compute Engine** (VMs) OR **Cloud Run** (fully managed containers) — for the LMS web app |
| **Serverless** | **Cloud Functions** — triggered when a new assignment is uploaded |
| **Containers** | **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** — industry-leading, since K8s was created by Google |
| **Storage** | **Cloud Storage (GCS)** — multi-regional bucket for lecture videos |
| **Database (SQL)** | **Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL** — student records, marks |
| **Database (NoSQL)** | **Firestore** — real-time chat, notifications |
| **Big Data / Analytics** | **BigQuery** — analyze student performance trends |
| **CDN** | **Cloud CDN** in front of Cloud Load Balancing |
| **Monitoring** | **Cloud Monitoring** + **Cloud Logging** |
| **ML** | **Vertex AI** — auto-grade essay answers, predict at-risk students |

**Conclusion (included in marks):** Both Azure and GCP can host a multi-tenant SaaS LMS. **Azure** is preferable if the institution already uses Microsoft 365 / Active Directory. **GCP** is preferable for institutions focused on **AI-driven analytics, container orchestration, and cost-effective egress**.

---

#### Part (b) — 3-Year TCO on AWS + ROI (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $TCO_{on-prem} = 54{,}00{,}000$ over 3 years (₹18 lakh/year × 3)
- $C_{cloud, annual} = 16{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{migration, one-time} = 6{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1 — 3-Year Cloud TCO (Operating Cost only)** (2 marks)
$$
TCO_{cloud, 3yr, ops} = 16{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 48{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Add Migration Cost** (1 mark)
$$
TCO_{cloud, 3yr, total} = 48{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 54{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Savings** (1 mark)
$$
\text{Savings} = TCO_{on-prem} - TCO_{cloud,total} = 54{,}00{,}000 - 54{,}00{,}000 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI** (2 marks)
$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{\text{Savings} - C_{migration}}{C_{migration}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0 - 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= -100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion & Recommendation** (1 mark)
> *"ROI = -100 %, meaning the migration is **NOT justified** on cost alone over a 3-year horizon. The startup should either (a) extend the horizon to 5 years, (b) negotiate Reserved Instance / Savings Plan discounts from AWS (typically 30–60 % off on-demand), (c) consider GCP's sustained-use discounts, or (d) factor non-financial benefits (agility, scalability, DR) before rejecting the migration."*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — re-read this the night before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Lists
- **6 R's of Migration**: Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retire, Relocate.
- **6-Step Migration Methodology**: Strategy → Discover → Plan → Migrate → Validate → Operate.
- **3 Hyperscalers**: AWS (2006, ~31 %), Azure (2010, ~24 %), GCP (2008, ~11 %).
- **5 Business Drivers**: Cost, Agility, Scalability, Innovation, Global Reach.

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Service Name Mappings
- **Compute (VM)** = EC2 / Azure VM / Compute Engine
- **Compute (Serverless)** = Lambda / Azure Functions / Cloud Functions
- **K8s** = EKS / AKS / GKE
- **Object Storage** = S3 / Blob / GCS
- **SQL DB** = RDS+Aurora / Azure SQL / Cloud SQL
- **NoSQL DB** = DynamoDB / Cosmos DB / Firestore
- **CDN** = CloudFront / Front Door / Cloud CDN
- **Data Warehouse** = Redshift / Synapse / BigQuery
- **IAM** = IAM / Entra ID / Cloud IAM
- **AI/ML** = SageMaker / Azure ML / Vertex AI

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Numbers
- Minutes in a year = **525,600**
- 99.9 % (3 nines) downtime = **525.6 minutes/year** (≈ 8.76 hours)
- 99.99 % (4 nines) downtime = **52.56 minutes/year**
- 99.999 % (5 nines) downtime = **5.26 minutes/year**
- AWS m5.large = **\$0.096/hour** (used in numerical problems)
- AWS S3 Standard ≈ **\$0.023/GB-month** (cold ≈ \$0.004/GB-month)
- AWS data egress ≈ **\$0.09/GB**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas
1. $TCO = C_{infra} + C_{license} + C_{power} + C_{cooling} + C_{admin} + C_{facility}$
2. $C_{cloud} = (N \times P \times U) + C_{storage} + C_{egress} + C_{API}$
3. $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$
4. $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Savings} - C_{migration}}{C_{migration}} \times 100\%$
5. $\text{Downtime}_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes

### 🔑 Examiner-Favourite Sentences (verbatim write these in answers)
- *"The application is deployed in a multi-AZ configuration to ensure 99.99 % availability (~52.56 minutes of allowed annual downtime)."*
- *"Cloud migration converts CapEx to OpEx, enabling the business to scale elastically and pay only for what is consumed."*
- *"The 6 R's framework enables enterprises to classify each workload and choose the most cost-effective migration strategy, balancing speed (Rehost) against long-term cloud-native benefit (Refactor)."*
- *"AWS offers the broadest service catalog (~200 services), Azure offers the best hybrid integration via Azure Arc, and GCP offers the strongest data analytics and AI/ML capabilities."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-minute Pitfall Avoidance**
> - Do NOT confuse **Rehost** (no code change) with **Replatform** (minor code change). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> - Always write **region + multi-AZ + SLA** when drawing cloud architecture diagrams.
> - In numerical questions, **show the formula, substitute values, compute, and state the conclusion** — skipping the conclusion loses 1 mark.
> - When asked "which cloud to choose", do **NOT** answer "AWS" alone — give a comparative justification based on workload, ecosystem, and cost.
